So what: Deal is a scale/reserves play; South32 pivots to copper and zinc under its new CEO.
“ALCOA CEMENTS ITS POSITION AS ONE OF THE LARGEST ALUMINUM PRODUCERS IN THE WESTERN WORLD.” ▶ 1:06:30
South32's new CEO is refocusing the company on copper and zinc, seeing more growth in copper than aluminum, which spurred the sale of roughly one-third of its business to Alcoa; the stock rose about 10%, the most in at least three months.
thesis🗣 Paul-Alain Huntconviction: mediumSouth32copperzincAlcoa
So what: South32 is repositioning as a base-metals (copper) growth story, rewarded by the market.
“THE COMPANY IS GOING TO BE FOCUSED ON COPPER AND ZINC UNDER HIS TERM.” ▶ 1:08:36
President Trump reported at least $1.4 billion in 2025 earnings from crypto and memecoin businesses (World Liberty Financial sales >$594M; CDC/memecoin ~$636M), far exceeding hotels and golf, renewing conflict-of-interest concerns Democrats plan to weaponize into midterm investigations.
data_point🗣 Derek Wallbank / Jon Herskovitzconviction: highDonald TrumpWorld Liberty FinancialcryptomemecoinDemocrats
So what: Crypto is now Trump's dominant income source and a live midterm political/regulatory risk.
“HE REPORTED EARNING AT LEAST 1.4 BILLION DOLLARS IN 2025 FROM CRYPTO AND MEME COIN RELATED BUSINESSES.” ▶ 13:52
Geopolitics: Iran talks and oil 2
President Trump has opted to stick with diplomacy over more strikes on Iran, telling aides he is fine if talks blow past the roughly August 18 / 60-day deadline for a nuclear deal, believing more full-scale attacks would hurt U.S. chances of dismantling Iran's nuclear program.
causal🗣 Haidi Lunconviction: mediumDonald TrumpIranHegsethGeneral Kane
So what: Diplomatic path extends the runway on Iran risk, capping near-term oil upside.
“THE PRESIDENT'S PREFERENCE IS ALWAYS DIPLOMACY. HE'S FINE IF WE BLOW PAST THAT AUGUST 18 DEADLINE.” ▶ 34:25
Oil posted its biggest quarterly drop since the pandemic even as crude ticks up modestly, with Strait of Hormuz traffic quiet and shipping returning as U.S.-Iran talks continue.
data_point🗣 Shery Ahn / Haidi Lunconviction: highcrude oilWTIBrentStrait of HormuzIran
So what: Fading war-risk premium is deflating oil, a tailwind for consumer inflation relief.
“FOR THE QUARTER, THOUGH, THERE WAS THE BIGGEST DROP FOR OIL SINCE THE PANDEMIC.” ▶ 26:31
US Tech Rebounds, Traders On Yen Intervention Watch | The Asia Trade 6/30/202695 min35 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's Asia Trade covers a choppy US chip/AI rebound bleeding into Asia, the yen hitting a 40-year low (~162/USD) with intervention watch but no BOJ action, and King Dollar strength on hawkish new Fed chair Warsh. Deep dives cover South Korea's $880B AI/chip buildout, DRAM oligopoly dynamics, Morgan Stanley's oil forecast cuts as the Strait of Hormuz reopens, the Supreme Court's Lisa Cook ruling reaffirming Fed independence, and the Super Micro NVIDIA-smuggling raid in Taiwan.
Yen at 40-year low and intervention watch · 4King Dollar and the Warsh Fed · 4US tech and AI rebound feeding into Asia · 5South Korea's $880B chip buildout and DRAM cycle · 6Oil, the Strait of Hormuz and geopolitics · 6China-EU trade, chip smuggling and consumer plays · 6Asia macro data and Australia's US-China pivot · 4
Yen at 40-year low and intervention watch 4
The yen has breached a 40-year low near 162 per dollar (161.95/161.98 area), a level not seen since the 2024 intervention, but the move has been a slow grind rather than a sharp drop, which lowers the odds Japanese authorities intervene.
causal🗣 Ruth Carsonconviction: highJapanese yenUSD/JPYBank of JapanMinistry of Finance
So what: Intervention is triggered by pace, not level; a controlled grind means traders can keep pressing dollar-yen higher without near-term MOF pushback.
“ONE OF THE THINGS OFFICIALS WATCH FOR WHEN IT COMES TO THE NECESSITY TO INTERVENE IN MARKETS IS THE PACE OF YEN WEAKNESS. IF THERE'S A SUDDEN SHARP DROP, YOU HAVE TO BE ON HIGH ALERT.” ▶ 4:29
Japanese authorities have already spent roughly $70 billion this year defending the yen, and Citigroup has abandoned a long-yen recommendation it opened only last month because no real support has materialized from officials.
data_point🗣 Shery Ahnconviction: highJapanese yenCitigroupMinistry of Finance
So what: A sell-side capitulation on long-yen positioning plus prior FX spend signals the market sees limited official firepower or resolve, favoring continued yen weakness.
“WE HAVE SEEN CITIGROUP GIVING UP ON THEIR LONG YEN CALL.” ▶ 46:18
Wide US-Japan rate differentials mean there is no fundamental reason for the yen to hold current levels; traders are pushing dollar-yen up toward the historic cap to test where the official line in the sand sits.
thesis🗣 Bloomberg markets reporterconviction: mediumJapanese yenUSD/JPYFederal ReserveBank of Japan
So what: Until the yield gap narrows, yen weakness is the path of least resistance and any intervention would be a fade opportunity rather than a trend change.
“INTIMATE DIFFERENCES BRIDGE, THERE IS NO FUNDAMENTAL REASON FOR THE YEN TO STAY WHERE IT IS.” ▶ 1:15:32
A weak yen is boosting Japanese exporters and carmakers, with company forecasts already flagging profit windfalls of billions of dollars this year, helping lift the Nikkei to record highs even as broader industrial output disappoints.
causal🗣 Shery Ahnconviction: highNikkeiJapanese yenJapanese carmakersexporters
So what: Yen depreciation is a direct earnings tailwind for Japanese exporters, decoupling equity strength from soft domestic macro data.
“WE HAVE ALREADY SEEN COMPANY FORECASTS BY CARMAKERS THAT COULD SEE A PROFIT WINDFALL OF BILLIONS OF DOLLARS THIS YEAR ALONE.” ▶ 1:07:39
King Dollar and the Warsh Fed 4
The dollar is reasserting dominance because macro traders are focused on rate differentials after new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh was judged more hawkish than expected at the last FOMC meeting, driving currency weakness across the Aussie (below 70 US cents), the yen, and EM.
causal🗣 Ruth Carsonconviction: highUS dollarKevin WarshFederal ReserveAustralian dollaryenemerging markets
So what: A hawkish Warsh Fed underpins a broad dollar-strength regime, meaning FX pain ripples globally and there is no clear alternative reserve currency to hide in.
“THERE'S A REASON WHY IT'S CALLED THE KING DOLLAR AND IT'S ON 80% OF ALL FX TRADES IN A $9.5 TRILLION MARKET. THERE IS NO ALTERNATIVE TO IT.” ▶ 7:48
The Supreme Court rejected Trump's bid to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook on procedural grounds, reaffirming the Fed's carveout, but left the door open for Trump to try again and did not define 'for cause', so Fed-independence tail risk is dented not eliminated.
thesis🗣 Enda Curranconviction: highLisa CookFederal ReserveSupreme CourtDonald TrumpKevin Warsh
So what: Markets shrugged because the ruling is a near-term win for Fed independence, but the unresolved 'for cause' question keeps a slow-burn political risk alive over the Fed board.
“IT'S A SOLID WIN ON PAPER FOR THE FED AND FED INDEPENDENCE, BUT IT DOES OPEN BROADER QUESTIONS GOING FORWARD.” ▶ 35:29
The real urgency behind firing Lisa Cook and Powell was to install a majority of seven Washington-based Fed governors to veto regional Fed heads who began new five-year terms this year; that window to engineer a 'political putsch' has largely passed, so the tail risk was already fading.
framework🗣 John Authersconviction: mediumLisa CookJay PowellFederal ReserveDonald Trump
So what: The threat to Fed control was time-sensitive and structural, so the muted market reaction reflects that the most dangerous scenario is now off the table regardless of the ruling's wording.
“THE GREATER URGENCY TO FIRE LISA COOK WAS TO TRY TO INSTALL A MAJORITY OF SEVEN GOVERNORS OF THE FED WHO SIT IN WASHINGTON IN TIME TO VETO THEIR REGIONAL HEADS OF THE FED.” ▶ 9:29
J.P. Morgan argues the 10-year Treasury yield is underpriced relative to prospective Fed tightening, setting up for a lower bar to further tightening ahead of Thursday's US employment data.
prediction🗣 Haidi Stroud-Wattsconviction: mediumJ.P. Morgan10-year TreasuryFederal Reserve
So what: If JPM is right, long-end yields have room to rise and rate-sensitive assets face repricing risk into the payrolls print.
“YOU HAVE J.P. MORGAN SAYING THE 10 YEAR YIELD UNDERPRICED WITH THE FED TIGHTENING.” ▶ 50:09
US tech and AI rebound feeding into Asia 5
The overnight US tech bounce is less impressive than it looks because much of it was Elon Musk-linked names (SpaceX rebounding, Tesla posting its best day in about five years), which skews perceptions; the Nasdaq 100's 2.25% gain still leaves it well below prior lows.
contrarian🗣 John Authersconviction: highTeslaSpaceXNasdaq 100Elon Musk
So what: The AI/tech 'dip buying' signal is weaker than headline gains suggest, so investors should not read the rebound as a durable trend reversal.
“A LOT OF WHAT WE HAVE SEEN IS THE ELON MUSK COMPANIES. SPACEX REBOUNDED QUITE NICELY. TESLA HAD ITS BEST DAY I THINK IN SOMETHING LIKE FIVE YEARS.” ▶ 3:04
Memory is the epicenter of current volatility, with Micron trading in nearly a 13% intraday range overnight while closing on the highs, and that whipsaw closely tracks Korean chip volatility.
data_point🗣 Anthony Stevensconviction: highMicronSK HynixSamsungPhiladelphia Semiconductor IndexSoftBank
So what: Extreme memory-name volatility is the dominant risk transmitting between US and Asian chip trades, so position sizing around Samsung/SK Hynix/Micron matters more than direction.
“MICRON TRADING IN ALMOST A 13% RANGE OVERNIGHT, AND THAT TRACKS THE KOREAN VOLATILITY RECENTLY.” ▶ 45:18
The preferred way to play a multi-year AI trade is via 'AI-adjacent' names, hardware, durables, chemicals and equipment used to build data centers, because they capture the AI tailwind with less direct volatility than the core semiconductor stocks.
framework🗣 Chris (equity strategist)conviction: highartificial intelligencedata centerssemiconductorsJapanese equipment makers
So what: AI-adjacency offers a lower-volatility, longer-runway entry into the AI theme for investors wary of stretched core-chip valuations.
“THOSE TYPES OF COMPANIES CAN REALLY CARRY A NOT A BENEFIT FOR INVESTORS AND NOT HAVE THE SAME TYPE OF VOLATILITY OR EXPOSURE DIRECTLY RELATED TO.” ▶ 52:18
The KOSPI is roughly 50% concentrated in Samsung and SK Hynix, making the index highly unidirectional and correlated to those two names, so a chip pullback would hit the KOSPI on the chin and could offer a good entry point.
framework🗣 Chris (equity strategist)conviction: highKOSPISamsungSK Hynix
So what: Concentration risk means KOSPI exposure is effectively a leveraged bet on two memory stocks; index dips driven by chip corrections may be buyable rather than structural.
“THE KOSPI IS HEAVILY CONCENTRATED BETWEEN SAMSUNG AND SK HYNIX. THEY ARE APPROXIMATE THE 50% OF THE MARKET VALUE OF THE INDEX, SO AS THOSE TWO STOCKS GO, SO WILL THE KOSPI.” ▶ 55:30
Foreign investors are dumping Korean and Taiwanese equities, with almost $5 billion of foreign selling in Korea in a single day taking the year-to-date total to roughly $92 billion.
data_point🗣 Anthony Stevensconviction: highKOSPIKoreaTaiwan
So what: Massive persistent foreign outflows are a structural overhang on Korean/Taiwanese equities despite the AI-driven index gains.
“YESTERDAY WAS A MASSIVE DAY OF FOREIGN SELLING IN KOREA, ALMOST $5 BILLION, TAKING THE YEAR TO DATE TOTAL TO ALMOST $92 BILLION.” ▶ 45:37
South Korea's $880B chip buildout and DRAM cycle 6
South Korea's plan will roughly double DRAM memory capacity over five years (about 50% supply growth per year via Samsung and SK Hynix each building two new fabs), an ambitious bet premised on AI-driven memory demand staying healthy and thus a key oversupply risk factor.
data_point🗣 Tom Kangconviction: highDRAMSamsungSK HynixSouth Koreaartificial intelligence
So what: A doubling of DRAM capacity is a large future supply overhang that could break the current shortage if AI demand growth decelerates.
“THIS PROJECT WILL DOUBLE THE RAM -- DOUBLE DRAM MEMORY CAPACITY IN THE NEXT FIVE YEARS. THAT COMES TO AN AVERAGE OF 50% GROWTH IN SUPPLY EVERY YEAR TO DOUBLE THE SIZE.” ▶ 12:53
Memory demand has been rising about 20% annually over the past two years while supply grew only 10%, an unprecedented run of consecutive 20% demand-growth years that is sustaining a genuine supply shortage.
data_point🗣 Tom Kangconviction: mediumDRAMmemoryartificial intelligence
So what: The abnormal demand-supply gap explains current memory pricing power but is explicitly flagged as not extrapolable, framing the boom's fragility.
“THE INDUSTRY HAS NEVER SEEN 20% DEMAND GROWTH IN CONSECUTIVE YEARS. SO THIS IS NOT THE USUAL TYPE OF DEMAND GROWTH.” ▶ 13:53
Samsung and SK Hynix have secured long-term contracts, sometimes with clients paying upfront to lock in capacity, meaning demand is effectively absorbed for at least two years and de-risking the new investment while lowering DRAM price-spike fears.
causal🗣 Tom Kangconviction: highSamsungSK HynixDRAM
So what: Pre-paid long-term contracts give near-term earnings visibility for the Korean memory duopoly but signal to the market that added capacity will cap future price upside.
“SOMETIMES CLIENTS WILL PAY UPFRONT TO SECURE CAPACITY. SO THIS IS A MORE SAFE SITUATION FOR SAMSUNG AND SK HYNIX.” ▶ 14:55
The Korean government is intervening to make Samsung and SK Hynix reinvest excess oligopoly profits into the wider economy, pressuring the pair to break their habit of investing in lockstep and raising prices back-to-back while watching for price-fixing.
thesis🗣 Tom Kangconviction: mediumSouth KoreaSamsungSK HynixDRAM
So what: Government pressure to spread profits and deter coordinated pricing could compress the memory duopoly's historically cyclical pricing power.
“THIS IS A GESTURE THAT'S PUSHING THESE TWO COMPANIES TO BREAK THEIR HABIT OF INVESTING TOGETHER AND RAISING PRICES CONSECUTIVELY, BACK TO BACK.” ▶ 18:57
Chinese memory makers like CXMT have been investing heavily since last year and will bring meaningful production online at end-2026 and into 2027, which is why Apple is exploring diversifying and buying memory from Chinese suppliers.
prediction🗣 Tom Kangconviction: mediumCXMTAppleChinaDRAMSamsungSK Hynix
So what: Chinese memory supply plus Apple diversification is a rising competitive and pricing threat to the Korean-US memory oligopoly on a 12-24 month horizon.
“THAT'S WHY APPLE IS TALKING ABOUT DIVERSIFYING AND EVEN GETTING MEMORY FROM CHINESE PLAYERS LIKE CXMT.” ▶ 17:41
The South Korean AI/chip investment plan is expected to lift the country's 2026 growth forecast to 3.5% from a prior 2.6%, with Samsung, SK Hynix (both in the trillion-dollar club) and the AI export boom as the primary driver.
data_point🗣 Haidi Stroud-Wattsconviction: mediumSouth KoreaSamsungSK Hynixartificial intelligence
So what: A near full-point upward GDP revision shows how singularly Korea's macro fortunes are now tied to the semiconductor/AI cycle.
“IT WILL HELP FUEL KOREA'S GROWTH TO 3.5% THIS YEAR FROM A PREVIOUS FORECAST OF 2.6%.” ▶ 1:28:19
Oil, the Strait of Hormuz and geopolitics 6
Morgan Stanley cut its Brent forecast, trimming Q3 2026 by $15 to $75 (Q4 also $75) and to $70 for H2 2027, citing a faster-than-expected Strait of Hormuz reopening, high US exports and low Chinese imports.
prediction🗣 Haidi Stroud-Wattsconviction: highMorgan StanleyBrent crudeStrait of HormuzChinaUnited States
So what: A sell-side downgrade back toward $70 signals the war-driven oil premium is unwinding and the base case is renewed oversupply.
“THIRD QUARTER 2026 FORECAST CUT BY $15 TO $75. FOURTH QUARTER DOWN TO $75 AS WELL. DOWN TO $70 BY THE THIRD AND FOURTH QUARTERS OF NEXT YEAR.” ▶ 21:34
Looking past the Hormuz disruption, the pre-war oversupply thesis is reasserting: UAE has left OPEC, the US is exporting more crude, and top importer China's crude appetite stays muted, all pointing back to an oversupplied 2027 market and lower prices.
thesis🗣 Rong Wei Neoconviction: highOPECUAEChinaUnited Statescrude oil
So what: The structural supply-demand backdrop favors lower oil into 2027 once the geopolitical premium fades, making war-driven spikes fadeable.
“WE HAVE UAE LEAVING OPEC AND RIGHT NOW AS A RESULT OF THE IRAN WAR, WE HAVE U.S. EXPORTING MORE CRUDE OIL.” ▶ 42:20
Markets remain surprisingly confident that inflation is coming down as oil retreats, but the Strait closure appears to have fueled a significant pickup in US inflation that is not fully reflected in market views, setting up a contested few months over lasting rate and inflation effects.
contrarian🗣 John Authersconviction: mediuminflation swapsBrent crudeFederal Reserveeuro zoneIndia
So what: If US inflation stickiness from the oil shock is underpriced, inflation-swap and rate positioning could be caught offside.
“THIS APPEARS TO HAVE FUELED QUITE A SIGNIFICANT PICKUP AND INFLATION TO ME IN THE STATES. THAT IS NOT REFLECTED IN ALL THE MARKET VIEWS.” ▶ 6:58
The Strait closure hit some oil-import-exposed countries mainly through growth rather than inflation, giving their central banks more leeway; the euro zone is the most interesting case and India is the most obvious candidate for a rebound.
framework🗣 John Authersconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzeuro zoneIndiacentral banks
So what: The differential growth-vs-price impact creates relative-value opportunities, with India and easing-capable central banks positioned for a rebound as the strait reopens.
“THOSE COUNTRIES THAT REALLY ARE EXPOSED TO OIL IMPORTS THROUGH THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ HAVE SIMPLY -- YOU HAVE SEEN MORE OF A DECLINING GROWTH.” ▶ 6:31
Gold has been one of the most disadvantaged asset classes since the war began, down roughly 21-24% since late February and holding most of those losses.
data_point🗣 Haidi Stroud-Wattsconviction: highgold
So what: Gold's sharp drawdown alongside dollar strength challenges its safe-haven role in the current regime and flags positioning risk in the metal.
≈ “GOLD HAS BEEN ONE OF THE MOST DISADVANTAGED ASSET CLASSES SINCE THE WAR BEGAN, DOWN 21% SINCE LATE FEBRUARY.” paraphrase
US-Iran talks in Doha may stall, with Iran signaling it will not talk directly with US envoys Kushner and Witkoff; Iran commits to no Hormuz tolls only for the 60-day negotiation window, after which it gets murky, and Oman has signaled openness to jointly controlling the strait with Iran.
thesis🗣 Matthew James Macarthurconviction: mediumIranUnited StatesStrait of HormuzJared KushnerSteve WitkoffOman
So what: Beyond the 60-day window, Iranian/Omani control over Hormuz transit and tolls is an unresolved tail risk to oil supply and prices.
“IRAN SAYS YEP, WE ARE COMMITTED TO THAT FOR THE 60 DAY PERIOD OF TIME DURING THE TALKS. AFTER THAT, IT GETS MURKY.” ▶ 1:00:40
China-EU trade, chip smuggling and consumer plays 6
The EU and China set an October deadline (aligned with the mid-October EU summit and Xi's late-September US trip) to make trade progress, against an unsustainable ~360 billion euro (~$410 billion) 2025 EU trade deficit with China, with Beijing warning it is ready for a freeze in ties if talks are not meaningful.
data_point🗣 Stephen Engleconviction: mediumEuropean UnionChinaSefcovicXi JinpingGermany
So what: The October deadline risks being can-kicking; a breakdown threatens German industrials (Volkswagen job cuts, BMW profit cuts) and EU access to Chinese rare earths and chips.
≈ “IN 2025 IT WAS 360 BILLION EUROS, ABOUT $410 BILLION U.S. HE SAYS THAT IS UNSUSTAINABLE.” paraphrase
Taiwan raided Super Micro's offices, a distributor and a data-center operator plus six individuals' residences over alleged smuggling of NVIDIA chips into China; the acts violate US export controls but not Taiwanese law, and Super Micro shares fell more than 8%.
data_point🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highSuper MicroNVIDIATaiwanChinaAMD
So what: The probe raises the compliance burden across US hyperscalers and NVIDIA/AMD supply chains and further constrains Chinese access to advanced chips.
“SUPER MICRO SHARES ENDED THE U.S. SESSION MORE THAN 8% LOWER.” ▶ 22:10
Onshore Chinese equities (CSI 300 up ~6%) are far outperforming offshore, driven by AI-supply-chain upstream names, raw materials, fabrics and glass, some up 300-800% this year; Goldman Sachs prefers onshore over offshore for more opportunity.
thesis🗣 Bloomberg Asian equities reporterconviction: mediumCSI 300ChinaGoldman SachsAI supply chain
So what: The onshore-offshore divergence favors playing China AI via smaller onshore upstream names rather than the underperforming Hong Kong internet/tech complex.
“GOLDMAN SACHS SAYS THEY STILL PREFER ONSHORE AGAINST OFFSHORE.” ▶ 1:20:16
Offshore Chinese tech is expected to keep underperforming (index down ~15% in 2026) with no clear catalyst, weighed by soft consumer demand, lack of stimulus, weak AI-related earnings, and cross-border flow clampdowns, and stronger AI-led growth reduces policymakers' urgency to stimulate.
causal🗣 Bloomberg Asian equities reporterconviction: mediumChinaChinese internet stocksDeepSeek
So what: Ironically, robust AI-driven growth lowers the odds of the policy stimulus offshore Chinese tech needs to re-rate, prolonging its underperformance.
“THANKS TO THE STRONG AI DEMAND. THERE IS LESS URGENCY FOR POLICYMAKERS TO STEP IN.” ▶ 1:18:27
Miniso USA plans ~20% year-over-year store growth (about 100 new stores in 2026 from ~500), is opening large 'Miniso Land' experiential stores (Columbus and a 15,000+ sq ft flagship on the Las Vegas Strip), and expects sustainable margins as it pushes into lifestyle, apparel, food and beauty via IP like Yo-Yo and Disney/Pokemon collaborations.
prediction🗣 Tom Bartlebaughconviction: highMinisoDisneyPokemonKaty Perry
So what: Miniso is betting resilient US consumers and experiential large-format retail plus IP collaborations sustain 20% expansion and margins despite US-China tension.
“FOR 2026, WE ARE LOOKING TO INCREASE OUR STORE ACCOUNT BY ABOUT 20% YEAR-OVER-YEAR. SO WE WILL OPEN ROUGHLY 100 NEW STORES.” ▶ 28:13
Rocket Lab is acquiring satellite operator Iridium in an $8 billion cash-and-stock deal, combining launch and satellite-making with Iridium's low-earth-orbit and spectrum assets to create an end-to-end, self-launching space communications provider.
data_point🗣 Rocket Lab CEOconviction: highRocket LabIridium
So what: Vertical integration into applications makes Rocket Lab a self-launching end-to-end space comms player, a differentiated model in the sector.
“BY COMBINING ROCKET LAB AND IRIDIUM, WE MAKE THAT COMPLETE PICTURE. THAT MEANS WE ARE A SELF LAUNCHING COMPANY.” ▶ 22:50
Asia macro data and Australia's US-China pivot 4
Japanese and South Korean industrial output both surprised sharply to the downside for May (Japan year-on-year contracting, contracting month-on-month), likely reflecting lingering Iran-war impacts on oil prices and refining activity despite strong AI-related chip exports.
data_point🗣 Shery Ahnconviction: highJapanSouth Koreaindustrial outputrefining
So what: The refining-driven output miss shows the oil shock is dragging on Asian industrial economies even as their semiconductor export engines run hot.
“DOWNSIDE SURPRISE IN INDUSTRIAL OUTPUT ALSO IN THE SOUTH KOREAN NUMBER FOR MAY.” ▶ 44:02
Analysts argue South Korea's $880B AI/chip investment could support the won because firms need to buy won to invest domestically and pay employees, though that support is not yet visible with the won still near record lows.
causal🗣 Shery Ahnconviction: lowKorean wonSouth KoreaSamsungSK Hynix
So what: A potential domestic-investment-driven won bid is a contrarian FX angle, but it is unconfirmed and currently overwhelmed by dollar strength.
“THESE COMPANIES WOULD NEED TO REALLY BY KOREAN WON IN ORDER TO INVEST IN SOUTH KOREA, PAY THEIR EMPLOYEES IN KOREAN WON. THAT COULD SUPPORT THE CURRENCY.” ▶ 49:12
Australian confidence in Trump has cratered (record lows) yet 73% still say the US alliance is important to security, while 51% now see China as more important than the US, six in ten see China as a likely military threat within 20 years, and six in ten are pessimistic on the economy over five years.
data_point🗣 Charles Lyons-Jonesconviction: highAustraliaUnited StatesChinaDonald TrumpXi Jinping
So what: Australians are increasingly reframing China as an economic partner over a pure security threat, a durable shift with implications for regional trade and alliance politics.
“CONFIDENCE IN PRESIDENT TRUMP HAS CRATERED BUT SUPPORT FOR THE ALLIANCE WITH THE U.S. REMAINS... 73% OF AUSTRALIANS STILL SAY THE ALLIANCE WITH THE U.S. REMAINS IMPORTANT TO SECURITY.” ▶ 1:21:30
Japan's May jobless rate held at 2.5% (in line) but the job-to-applicant ratio slipped to 117 jobs per 100 applicants, slightly below expectations, keeping the labor market tight amid the question of whether yen-driven cost inflation will translate into sustainable wage growth.
data_point🗣 Shery Ahnconviction: mediumJapanlabor marketJapanese yen
So what: A still-tight but softening labor market keeps the BOJ's wage-inflation reflation thesis in doubt while yen weakness raises import costs.
“THE JOB TO APPLICANT RATIO, 117 JOBS AVAILABLE PER 100 APPLICANTS RIGHT NOW. THIS HAS FALLEN A LITTLE BIT FROM THE PREVIOUS MONTH.” ▶ 25:09
Nike Earnings Surpass Expectations as Q2 Comes to a Close | The Close 6/30/202692 min39 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's The Close caps the strongest quarter for the S&P 500 since 2020 (chips posting their best quarter ever), then dissects Nike's fiscal Q4 beat (EPS $0.72 vs $0.14 y/y, revenue $10.97B, China down 12%) amid Elliott Hill's turnaround and a new Pfizer-sourced CFO. Guests span BlackRock fixed-income flows, Treasury central-clearing structure, an RBC bull case on beaten-down Microsoft, and Jack Lew on sanctions and the Iran nuclear MOU.
Fixed-income flows and the front-end tilt · 5Treasury market structure and central clearing · 4Microsoft: AI-vs-software fear and the buy case · 4Sanctions, the dollar and the Iran nuclear MOU · 4Quarter-end market recap: rotation and concentration · 6Sector rotation, telecom disruption and single-name moves · 5Nike earnings, turnaround and competition · 8Nike's new CFO and the AI-driven leadership playbook · 3
Fixed-income flows and the front-end tilt 5
Despite yields on the long end being attractive (30-year Treasury ~4.9%), the vast majority of new fixed-income money is going to the short end, with ~$64B into ultra-short-duration funds in 2026 while long-dated funds saw ~$500M of outflows.
data_point🗣 Steve (Global Head of iShares, BlackRock) / Katie Greifeldconviction: highBlackRockiSharesTLTUS TreasuriesBloomberg Intelligence
So what: Investors are allocating for income and avoiding duration despite a functional long-end return, signaling persistent distaste for term risk.
“THE BIGGEST DEMAND IS FOR ULTRA SHORT DURATION FIXED INCOME FUNDS. ABOUT $64 BILLION HAS BEEN POURED INTO THAT MATURITY BUCKET.” ▶ 2:47
The 20-plus-year part of the Treasury curve (e.g. TLT) is actually outperforming the 1-to-3-year part, a counterintuitive fact given money is still flowing to the short end.
contrarian🗣 Steve (BlackRock)conviction: mediumTLTUS Treasuries
So what: Flows are chasing yield/income rather than total return, so long-end price performance is being ignored by allocators.
“THE 20-PLUS-YEAR PART OF THE CURVE, WE LOOK AT SOMETHING LIKE T.L.T. IS OUT-PERFORMING THE ONE TO THREE-YEAR PART OF THE CURVE THAT MIGHT SURPRISE PEOPLE.” ▶ 4:11
Market-implied inflation at the short end has plummeted, with the two-year breakeven falling from a high around 3.4% to roughly 2%, so short-dated TIPS look attractive for investors still worried about inflation.
thesis🗣 Steve (BlackRock)conviction: mediumTIPSUS Treasuries
So what: Markets are looking past energy/conflict-driven price pressure, creating a potential entry point in short-dated inflation protection.
≈ “LOOK AT TWO-YEAR BREAK EVEN THEY WENT FROM A HIGH AROUND 340 ALL THE WAY DOWN TO ABOUT 2%.” paraphrase
June was a record month for investment-grade issuance driven partly by Magnificent Seven tapping primary markets for AI buildout, yet spreads stayed tight (IG in the mid-70s bps, high-yield a couple hundred above), showing the market absorbed the supply.
data_point🗣 Steve (BlackRock)conviction: highMagnificent Seveninvestment-grade credithigh-yield credit
So what: AI-driven IG supply is being digested without spread widening, so credit remains a functioning funding channel for hyperscaler capex.
≈ “JUNE WAS A RECORD MONTH FOR I.G. ISSUANCE. THERE'S TALK THAT THAT'S CAUSING SOME CROWDING OUT... BUT REALLY, THE ISSUANCE HAS BEEN FAIRLY WELL-RECEIVED.” paraphrase
European investment-grade and high-yield credit is an overlooked opportunity that is performing well even relative to US investment grade, a story BlackRock focuses on in active strategies.
thesis🗣 Steve (BlackRock)conviction: mediumEuropean creditBlackRock
So what: A non-consensus allocation idea for credit investors seeking relative value outside the crowded US market.
“IF YOU LOOK AT EUROPEAN INVESTMENT GRADE AND EUROPEAN HIGH-YIELD, IT'S DOING PRETTY WELL EVEN COMPARED TO U.S. INVESTMENT GRADE... A STORY THAT I THINK HASN'T BEEN LOOKED AT THAT CLOSELY.” ▶ 8:54
Treasury market structure and central clearing 4
Central clearing interposes a CCP between the two counterparties on a Treasury trade, so participants no longer face each other, which reduces counterparty credit risk and can bring more participation into the market during times of stress.
framework🗣 Nate (Head of Market Structure, BNY; former head of domestic markets, NY Fed)conviction: highBNYUS Treasury marketSECNY Fed
So what: The clearing mandate structurally re-plumbs the Treasury market and could improve liquidity precisely when it matters most.
“IT INTERPOSES A C.C.P. IN BETWEEN THE TWO COUNTERPARTIES OF THE TRADE. SO YOU DON'T FACE YOUR COUNTERPARTY ANYMORE YOU NOW FACE THE C.C.P.” ▶ 12:38
The end-of-June repo central-clearing deadline is a bigger deal than the December cash-Treasury deadline because it involves more complex infrastructure change, and the SEC has signaled it granted an extra year and now expects industry to show progress.
prediction🗣 Nate (BNY)conviction: mediumrepo marketSECUS Treasury market
So what: Repo clearing is the harder operational lift and the more likely candidate for future friction or extension debate.
“AN EVEN BIGGER... I WOULD ARGUE IS AT THE END OF JUNE FOR THE REPO MARKET AND THAT IS MORE COMPLEX BECAUSE IT INVOLVES MORE INFRASTRUCTURE CHANGE.” ▶ 13:48
The Treasury market is ~$30 trillion today and CBO forecasts have it growing to over $50 trillion in the next 10 years, making absorption capacity and intermediary resilience the central structural questions.
data_point🗣 Nate (BNY)conviction: highUS Treasury marketCongressional Budget OfficeFederal Reserve
So what: Supply growth of this magnitude puts dealer intermediation capacity at the center of future market functioning and stress dynamics.
“IT'S A $30 TRILLION MARKET TODAY... YOU HAVE... THE MARKET GROWING TO BE OVER $50 TRILLION IN THE NEXT 10 YEARS.” ▶ 14:42
Loosening of the leverage ratio and liquidity ratio restrictions could over time help intermediaries facilitate more transactions in the Treasury market amid growing supply.
causal🗣 Nate (BNY)conviction: mediumbank regulationleverage ratioUS Treasury market
So what: Regulatory relief is a lever policymakers are pulling to expand dealer balance-sheet capacity ahead of the supply wave.
“YOU HAVE SEEN SOME CHANGES IN REGULATIONS, LOOSENING OF RESTRICTIONS AROUND THE LEVERAGE RATIO... THAT COULD OVER TIME HELP INTERMEDIARIES.” ▶ 15:56
Microsoft: AI-vs-software fear and the buy case 4
Microsoft's worst month in 26 years and four straight quarters of S&P underperformance stem from being hit on both sides: fears AI will replace application software, and impatience over high capex with no sign of slowing.
causal🗣 Rishi (Managing Director, Software Equity Research, RBC Capital)conviction: highMicrosoftMSFTRBC CapitalS&P 500
So what: Frames the exact narrative overhang a contrarian buyer must believe is overdone.
≈ “THEY ARE GETTING HIT ON THAT NARRATIVE THAT AI IS GOING TO REPLACE APPLICATION SOFTWARE... AND ON THE OTHER SIDE, THERE IS GROWING IMPATIENCE OVER HIGH CAPEX.” paraphrase ▶ 18:34
The cost of replacing Microsoft's Office suite (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, Teams, OneDrive, SharePoint) yourself would be at least 10x more expensive even for the most capable organization, making obsolescence-by-AI fears overblown.
thesis🗣 Rishi (RBC Capital)conviction: highMicrosoftOfficeExcelTeams
So what: The replacement-cost moat argument underpins the case that AI won't cannibalize Microsoft's core franchise.
“THE ACTUAL COST OF THAT WOULD BE A MINIMUM 10 TIMES MORE EXPENSIVE IF YOU DID IT YOURSELF EVEN IF YOU ARE THE MOST CAPABLE ORGANIZATION IN THE WORLD.” ▶ 20:23
Office Copilot is finally improving after Cynthia stepped back into product/engineering in November, adding 5 million paid subscribers last quarter with engagement now on par with Outlook and more expected this quarter.
data_point🗣 Rishi (RBC Capital)conviction: mediumMicrosoftOffice CopilotOutlook
So what: Copilot traction is the leading indicator RBC watches to validate the turnaround, and it is now inflecting positively.
“THEY ADDED 5 MILLION PAID SUBSCRIBERS LAST QUARTER... THEY TALKED ABOUT OFFICE COPILOT ENGAGEMENT BEING ON PAR WITH OUTLOOK.” ▶ 21:04
The market is unhappy that Microsoft is allocating first-party Azure capacity to its own apps like Office Copilot and GitHub Copilot (no revenue association) rather than to third-party customers including OpenAI and Anthropic, though RBC argues this is the right long-term call.
contrarian🗣 Rishi (RBC Capital)conviction: mediumMicrosoftAzureOpenAIAnthropicGitHub Copilot
So what: Capacity-allocation choices are pressuring near-term Azure optics and explain part of the underperformance, but may build durable owned-app value.
≈ “ALLOCATING FIRST PARTY CAPACITY TO APPLICATIONS LIKE OFFICE COPILOT, GITHUB COPILOT WHERE THERE IS NO REVENUE ASSOCIATION. VERSUS TWO THIRD-PARTY CUSTOMERS INCLUDING OPENAI AND ANTHROPIC.” paraphrase
Sanctions, the dollar and the Iran nuclear MOU 4
The Iran agreement reached weeks ago is only a memorandum of understanding that does not detail how the toughest issues (removing highly enriched material, an inspection regime, timing of sanctions relief) would be resolved.
framework🗣 Jack Lew (former US Treasury Secretary, former Ambassador to Israel)conviction: highIranUnited Statesnuclear agreement
So what: Investors treating the MOU as a resolved deal are overreading it; substantial and market-relevant negotiation risk remains.
≈ “THE AGREEMENT REACHED... IS A MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING. IT DOESN'T LAY OUT IN ANY DETAIL WHAT A NUCLEAR AGREEMENT WOULD LOOK LIKE.” paraphrase
At moments of maximum stress during the Iran conflict there was a rise in non-dollar transactions and use of dollar-avoidance plumbing, but flows returned to the dollar within days once tension eased because nothing else offers comparable depth and liquidity.
thesis🗣 Jack Lewconviction: highUS dollarChinaRussiaIndiaIran
So what: De-dollarization is real at the margin under stress but not yet structurally displacing the dollar's reserve role.
“AS SOON AS THE TENSION WENT DOWN, EVEN FOR A DAY OR A WEEK, IT CAME BACK TO THE DOLLAR. THERE'S SIMPLY NOTHING OTHER THAN THE DOLLAR THAT'S AVAILABLE IN THE DEPTH AND LIQUIDITY.” ▶ 28:59
Imposing pain on Iran in some ways increased their endurance for pain, and the US reaction to higher gasoline prices weakened America's ability to withstand a Strait of Hormuz closure, showing sanctions must be used with care around counterparty leverage.
causal🗣 Jack Lewconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzUS sanctionsgasoline
So what: Energy-price sensitivity is a binding constraint on US coercive leverage, relevant to oil and geopolitical risk pricing.
“THEY ALSO SAW THE U.S. RESPONSE TO HIGHER GASOLINE PRICES, WEAKENED OUR ABILITY TO WITHSTAND A CLOSURE OF THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.” ▶ 27:52
Iran now appears to have gained increased control over the Strait of Hormuz coming out of the war and MOU, with ambiguous language each side reads differently on whether Iran can charge tolls to reopen it.
prediction🗣 Jack Lewconviction: mediumIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: A structurally more Iran-controlled chokepoint is a lingering risk premium for global energy shipping.
“WHETHER THEY CHARGE A FEE OR NOT, THEY CERTAINLY HAVE INCREASED THEIR KIND OF CONTROL OVER THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.” ▶ 26:09
Quarter-end market recap: rotation and concentration 6
The S&P 500 rose ~15% (18.5% total return) for the quarter, its best since 2020, the Nasdaq gained over 21%, and semiconductors posted their best quarter ever and are on track for their best year ever if gains hold.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick / Carol Massar / Tim Stenovecconviction: highS&P 500Nasdaq 100Philadelphia Semiconductor IndexDow
So what: Sets the risk-on backdrop: chip-led melt-up with the Dow up 13% and Russell 2000 up ~21% for the quarter.
≈ “BEST QUARTER FOR THE S&P 500 SINCE 2020 AND MAYBE EVEN ELEVATED FURTHER BACK. BEST QUARTER EVER FOR CHIP STOCKS.” paraphrase
Market leadership is broadening in all directions, not just away from Mag 7: emerging markets up almost 23% YTD, small caps up 22%, and value up over 16% while growth is not even up 1% YTD.
contrarian🗣 Tiffany McKee (CFO/CIO, Pivotal Advisors)conviction: highMagnificent Sevenemerging marketssmall capsvalue stocksgrowth stocks
So what: The AI narrative masks a pronounced value-over-growth and EM outperformance that argues for genuine diversification.
“VALUE IS UP OVER 16% YEAR TO DATE VERSUS GROWTH WHICH THROUGH LAST WEEK IS NOT EVEN DOING 1% YEAR TO DATE.” ▶ 38:04
US equities are over 60% of global market cap, creating dangerous concentration in portfolios, so investors should be deliberate about regional diversification within the 60% equity sleeve of a 60/40.
framework🗣 Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)conviction: mediumUS equitiesglobal markets60/40 portfolio
So what: A concrete risk-management flag: passive S&P indexing embeds extreme US concentration that rebalancing should address by region.
“U.S. GLOBAL MARKET CAP IS OVER 60% SO THERE IS A LOT OF CONCENTRATION IN PORTFOLIOS RIGHT NOW.” ▶ 38:49
In the March 2026 market turmoil, opportunistic/private credit, real assets, and defensive (hedge-like) equities were what kept endowment client portfolios afloat, and manager selection favored smaller (not the biggest) private-credit firms.
anecdote🗣 Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)conviction: mediumprivate creditopportunistic creditreal assetsendowments
So what: Non-core satellite allocations provided downside protection during the March shock, informing where to seek resilience.
≈ “WHEN I LOOK AT THE AREAS THAT KEPT OUR CLIENTS AFLOAT IN THAT CRAZINESS WAS PRIVATE CREDIT, OPPORTUNISTIC CREDIT, REAL ASSETS, AND SOMETHING WE LIKE TO CALL DEFENSIVE EQUITIES.” paraphrase
To play AI in a diversified way, own the creators (foundation companies), the builders (infrastructure), and the adopters, with copper as a key metal to power AI rather than concentrating in Nvidia and Microsoft.
framework🗣 Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)conviction: mediumNvidiaMicrosoftcopperAI
So what: A three-layer AI framework points investors toward second-order beneficiaries like industrial metals.
“WE WANT TO OWN THE CREATORS... THE BUILDERS... AND... THE ADOPTERS AND SO THAT IS WHERE COPPER FITS IN.” ▶ 42:45
S&P earnings could exceed 20% EPS growth in 2026, a number rarely achieved outside recession-recovery periods, and if cyclical and non-AI sectors contribute more it could replay the 2021 post-pandemic profit boom.
prediction🗣 Romaine Bostick (citing Citadel data)conviction: mediumS&P 500CitadelAI
So what: Frames the bull case that the rally is earnings-driven, not purely speculative, with room for cyclical breadth.
≈ “THE S&P OVERALL COULD EXCEED 20% E.P.S. GROWTH IN 2026. THAT'S A NUMBER RARELY ACHIEVED OUTSIDE OF RECESSION RECOVERY PERIODS.” paraphrase
Sector rotation, telecom disruption and single-name moves 5
Telecom stocks fell for a second straight day (AT&T down over 5% after falling 6.3% the prior day) on news that SpaceX and Charter Communications are in executive-level talks on a consumer phone offering that could challenge the big three wireless providers.
causal🗣 Tim Stenovecconviction: highAT&TVerizonSpaceXCharter Communications
So what: A satellite/cable entrant threatens the core mobile business of incumbent carriers, a fresh disruption vector for telecom valuations.
“SPACEX AND CHARTER COMMUNICATIONS ARE HAVING EXECUTIVE LEVEL TALKS ON A CONSUMER PHONE OFFERING THAT COULD CHALLENGE WIRELESS PROVIDERS.” ▶ 49:37
Call-center outsourcers Concentrix (down 11.2%) and Teleperformance fell as Concentrix cut its FY2026 revenue outlook and warned customers are cutting back, amid fears human-handled requests will be replaced by AI.
causal🗣 Tim Stenovecconviction: highConcentrixTeleperformanceAI
So what: AI substitution risk is now showing up in guidance cuts for labor-arbitrage BPO firms, a concrete AI-displacement casualty.
≈ “CONCENTRIX CUT ITS REVENUE OUTLOOK, WARNED ITS CUSTOMERS ARE CUTTING BACK ON SPENDING.” paraphrase
Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix announced plans to build two chip-making plants apiece in South Korea totaling about $500 billion, boosting chip-tool names like KLA and ASML (up ~5.6%).
data_point🗣 Carol Massarconviction: highSamsung ElectronicsSK HynixKLAASMLSouth Korea
So what: A massive Korean fab investment cycle underpins the semiconductor capital-equipment rally.
≈ “SAMSUNG ELECTRONICS AND S.K. HYNIX ANNOUNCED PLANS TO BUILD TWO CHIP MAKING PLANTS APIECE IN SOUTH KOREA FOR A TOTAL OF ABOUT $500 BILLION U.S.” paraphrase
Analyst top calls flagged a downgrade of Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to underperform on heavy leverage risk (advising 'boring banks'), a Rocket Companies initiation at $21 as a future dominant real-estate portal, and a SanDisk target raise to $3,000 from $1,700 on new customer agreements.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg anchor (Top Calls segment)conviction: lowGoldman SachsMorgan StanleyRocket CompaniesSanDiskBernsteinBenchmark
So what: Captures the sell-side positioning skew: cautious on levered banks, constructive on mortgage fintech and memory chips.
“STICK TO BORING BANKS.” ▶ 17:01
Blackstone is selling ~$3.5B of data centers to Digital Realty not to exit the sector but to recycle capital into other hyperscale projects, reinforcing rather than reducing its data-center position.
thesis🗣 Norah Mulinda (Bloomberg TV markets correspondent)conviction: mediumBlackstoneDigital Realtydata centersNorthern Virginia
So what: A capital-recycling read on the deal signals continued conviction in AI infrastructure, not a top in data-center demand.
“BLACKSTONE IS NOT DITCHING DATA CENTERS. IF ANYTHING, THEY ARE REINFORCING THE POSITION... ESSENTIALLY RECYCLING THEIR CASH FLOW.” ▶ 33:34
Nike earnings, turnaround and competition 8
Nike's fiscal Q4 beat with EPS of $0.72 (vs $0.14 a year earlier) and revenue of $10.97B above the ~$10.8B estimate, with gross margin at 39.2% and inventory up only ~0.2%.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeldconviction: highNikeNKE
So what: A clean beat against low expectations, but the market reaction was muted as guidance and China remained the swing factors.
≈ “EPS, $.72 A SHARE VERSUS 14 CENTS YEAR-OVER-YEAR... THAT REVENUE NUMBER DOES APPEAR TO BE RELATIVELY ABOVE ESTIMATES.” paraphrase
Greater China revenue was $1.3B, down 12% year-over-year, but that was much better than the high-teens decline that had been expected, while North America grew ~2.7%.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeld / Poonam Goyal (Bloomberg Intelligence)conviction: highNikeGreater ChinaNorth America
So what: China is still the key overhang, but a less-bad print eases the worst fears while North America carries the recovery.
“GREATER CHINA REVENUE, $1.3 BILLION. THAT IS DOWN 12% YEAR-OVER-YEAR... MUCH BETTER THAN THE HIGH TEENS DECLINE THAT WAS EXPECTED.” ▶ 58:58
Nike's gross-margin increase this quarter is not indicative of the go-forward trajectory because the company still has a lot of discounting to do to clear inventory, and the benefit is largely one-time and tied to tariff reform.
contrarian🗣 Poonam Goyal (Bloomberg Intelligence)conviction: highNikegross margintariffs
So what: Warns investors not to extrapolate the margin beat; underlying markdown pressure remains.
“THE INCREASE WE SEE HERE, IS NOT INDICATIVE OF A GROSS MARGIN TRAJECTORY. THEY DO HAVE A LOT OF DISCOUNTING TO DO. THAT IS ALL ONE TIME.” ▶ 1:03:11
CFRA argues analysts are underpredicting Nike's earnings power, seeing over $2 EPS achievable fairly easily in a decent year and $3 within reach over the next two to three years, with running up over 20% last quarter.
thesis🗣 Zachary Warren (equity analyst, CFRA)conviction: highNikeCFRArunning
So what: The bull case rests on an earnings-power rerating that consensus is missing, driven by product momentum.
“WE THINK NIKE CAN DO OVER $2 IN EARNINGS PER SHARE. PRETTY EASILY IN JUST A DECENT YEAR WE THINK 3 IS IN REACH IN THE NEXT TWO TO THREE YEARS.” ▶ 57:14
Nike historically sees revenue up ~8% in FIFA World Cup years versus 5-6% in non-World-Cup years, giving CFRA higher expectations for the coming fiscal year.
data_point🗣 Zachary Warren (CFRA)conviction: mediumNikeFIFA World Cup
So what: A quantified seasonal tailwind supports the near-term revenue setup for the turnaround narrative.
“TYPICALLY THEY SEE REVENUES UP 8% IN WORLD CUP YEARS COMPARED TO 5% TO 6% IN NON-WORLD CUP YEARS.” ▶ 56:06
In M Science data, Nike is winning in World Cup apparel but Adidas is well outpacing Nike in soccer jerseys, and footwear remains the segment where Nike is relatively challenged.
data_point🗣 Drake McFarlane (research analyst, M Science)conviction: highNikeAdidasPumafootwearapparel
So what: Alternative data shows the World Cup lift is uneven and Adidas is the sponsor beneficiary, tempering the Nike demand read.
“EVEN THOUGH NIKE HAS DONE WELL, WE HAVE SEEN ADIDAS WELL OUTPACE THEM IN SOCCER JERSEYS.” ▶ 1:06:01
Salomon has grown substantially in US footwear, showing a shoe's popularity is not tied to its intended use case (its hit was originally a 1970s indoor soccer shoe), and low-profile styles are re-emerging over chunky designs across Salomon, Adidas Samba and Nike's retro Moon Shoe.
thesis🗣 Drake McFarlane (M Science)conviction: mediumSalomonAdidasNikefootwear
So what: A design-cycle turn toward low-profile shoes plus new upstart competitors reshapes where footwear share will accrue.
“YOU CAN SEE EARLY HINTS THAT THE CHUNKY WAVE MIGHT NOT BE HERE FOREVER. FOR LIFESTYLE, I THINK LOW-PROFILE IS THE WAY.” ▶ 1:09:47
Nike now competes not just with other shoe brands but for discretionary dollars against everyday essentials as cost-of-living rises, meaning even a strong brand must repeatedly prove its worth to a stretched shopper.
framework🗣 Wendy Liebman (CEO/Chief Shopper, WSL Strategic Retail)conviction: mediumNikeconsumerretail
So what: Reframes Nike's addressable competition as the whole household budget, a macro-consumer headwind to premium footwear demand.
“NOW YOU'RE COMPETING WITH EVERY DAY ESSENTIALS AND NOT JUST ANOTHER PAIR OF SNEAKERS.” ▶ 1:13:10
Nike's new CFO and the AI-driven leadership playbook 3
Nike named David Denton (from Pfizer) as CFO to replace Matthew Friend, starting in August; industry experience is seen as less important than knowing how to run a business of scale with heavy capex and a global supply chain.
thesis🗣 Bloomberg senior editor (CFO Briefing)conviction: mediumNikeDavid DentonPfizerMatthew Friend
So what: The unconventional pharma-to-apparel CFO hire signals Nike prioritizes scale/supply-chain management over sector pedigree.
≈ “WHAT MATTERS IS IF YOU KNOW THE BUSINESS OF SCALE THAT HAS A CERTAIN... CAPEX AND A WIDENING SUPPLY CHAIN AROUND THE GLOBE, WHICH DAVE HAS.” paraphrase ▶ 1:17:35
An executive-search CEO argues the most interesting thing about Denton is Pfizer's AI-native transformation, which supposedly drove close to $5 billion in efficiency improvement, and expects similar finance-function AI automation at Nike.
thesis🗣 Jeff Christian (founder/CEO, Christian & Timbers)conviction: mediumNikePfizerDavid DentonAI
So what: Positions the CFO hire as an AI-driven cost-and-efficiency lever, with FP&A/finance headcount reduction as the near-term result.
“SUPPOSEDLY THEY'VE DONE CLOSE TO 5 BILLION IN EFFICIENCY IMPROVEMENT AS A RESULT OF AI TRANSFORMATION.” ▶ 1:20:38
CEO runways have collapsed from three-to-five years to about three, and given AI's pace, Elliott Hill likely has only about another year to turn Nike around before succession questions arise; Nike has already cut ~1,400 tech jobs.
prediction🗣 Jeff Christian (Christian & Timbers)conviction: mediumNikeElliott HillAI
So what: The turnaround clock is short, raising the odds of further executive change and AI-driven restructuring if performance lags.
“I WOULD SAY ELLIOTT PROBABLY ONLY HAS ANOTHER YEAR, TO TURN THE COMPANY AROUND BEFORE I WOULD START THINKING ABOUT A NEW CEO.” ▶ 1:23:49
Supreme Court Re-Shapes Power of Presidency | The Close 6/29/202689 min36 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's The Close breaks down a landmark Supreme Court ruling that lets the president fire independent-agency heads (FTC/FCC) at will while shielding the Fed for now, and layers on market signal across the AI-infrastructure rally, Fed-on-hold rate views, Comcast's media spinoff and telecom/media M&A, and Goldman's AI job-displacement forecast.
Supreme Court reshaping executive power over agencies · 5Fed independence and Powell's status · 4Fed on hold, inflation stickiness and front-end positioning · 4AI-driven equity rally, concentration and overlap risk · 6Media and telecom M&A wave · 4London Stock Exchange, data monetization and listings competition · 4AI labor displacement and job creation · 4Defense, aerospace and consumer/retail signals · 5
Supreme Court reshaping executive power over agencies 5
The Supreme Court ruled the president can fire commissioners of independent agencies like the FTC regardless of good cause, rejecting the idea of a fourth branch of government not directly accountable to the president.
causal🗣 Bill (former FTC chair)conviction: highSupreme CourtFTCJohn RobertsRebecca Kelly SlaughterTrump
So what: Independent regulators (FTC, FCC and dozens more) now effectively become extensions of the executive branch, raising regulatory-policy volatility for regulated industries.
“THE PRESIDENT MUST HAVE THE POWER TO FIRE COMMISSIONERS REGARDLESS OF WHETHER OR NOT HE HAS GOOD CAUSE.” ▶ 11:02
Agencies will likely abandon the bipartisan multi-member model and shift to single-party representation because nothing compels the president to seat minority commissioners on a timetable.
prediction🗣 Bill (former FTC chair)conviction: highFTCAndrew Ferguson
So what: Regulatory decisions become more partisan and swing harder with each administration, increasing policy risk for companies planning multi-year strategies.
“WE WILL WALK AWAY IN MANY WAYS FROM HAVING A COMBINATION OF REPUBLICANS AND DEMOCRATS. WE WILL HAVE SINGLE PARTY REPRESENTATION” ▶ 12:10
Stripping agencies of political autonomy weakens their standing before courts because judges no longer defer to decisions that appear driven by political expediency rather than professional expertise.
framework🗣 Bill (former FTC chair)conviction: highFTCfederal courts
So what: Politicized agency rulings may be more easily overturned in litigation, adding uncertainty to enforcement outcomes companies face.
“AS SOON AS IT APPEARS AS THOUGH DECISIONS OF THE PRODUCT OF POLITICAL EXPEDIENCY ARE PRESSURE FROM ELECTED OFFICIALS, THERE IS NO BASIS FOR THE COURT TO RESPECT THE DECISIONS OF THE AGENCY” ▶ 13:38
The Fed ruling rested largely on procedural grounds around how Governor Cook was dismissed, leaving open the possibility that a more carefully constructed case could later expand presidential firing power over the Fed.
thesis🗣 Bill (former FTC chair)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveLisa CookSupreme Court
So what: Fed independence is protected for now but not permanently settled, a lingering tail risk for rate expectations and markets.
“IT WAS THE IMPETUOUS AND AS OF THE DISMISSAL THAT I THINK RAISED CONCERNS BY THE COURT” ▶ 15:16
The court appeared reluctant to send markets into a tailspin by dismissing a Fed board member central to the monetary system, suggesting market-stability concerns shaped the Fed carve-out.
causal🗣 Bill (former FTC chair)conviction: mediumSupreme CourtFederal Reserve
So what: The judiciary is factoring market fragility into rulings, implying continued protection of Fed independence to avoid disruption.
“THE COURT WAS ALSO VERY CONCERNED HERE THAT IT DOES NOT WANT TO SEND THE MARKETS INTO A TAILSPIN SIMPLY BY DISMISSING A MEMBER OF A BOARD” ▶ 15:32
Fed independence and Powell's status 4
The Fed ruling was 5-4 but roughly eight of nine justices would stand by Fed independence provided a member could still be fired for cause, per a former Fed governor's read.
thesis🗣 Betsy Dukeconviction: highSupreme CourtFederal ReserveBrett Kavanaugh
So what: Broad justice support means Fed independence is more durable than the narrow vote suggests, a stabilizing signal for rate markets.
“IT LOOKS TO ME LIKE EIGHT OF THE JUSTICES WOULD STAND BY FED INDEPENDENCE WITH THE PROVISION THAT A MEMBER COULD BE FIRED FOR CAUSE.” ▶ 57:30
Roberts cited the first and second US attempts to form a central bank as precedent that the founding fathers believed in an independent central bank.
anecdote🗣 Betsy Dukeconviction: mediumJohn RobertsFederal Reserve
So what: The originalist grounding makes the pro-independence stance harder to reverse in future cases.
“HE REACHED ALL THE WAY BACK TO THE FIRST ATTEMPT TO FORM A CENTRAL BANK” ▶ 56:03
Even with firing protection, sitting Fed governors can still be harassed and dragged through the courts, which is costly for individuals and remains a residual pressure risk.
contrarian🗣 Betsy Dukeconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Legal harassment is a backdoor channel to pressure Fed officials even if outright dismissal is barred.
“YOU MIGHT NOT BE ABLE TO FIRE A MEMBER OF THE BOARD OF GOVERNORS BUT YOU CAN CERTAINLY HARASS THEM AND DRAGGED HIM THROUGH THE COURTS” ▶ 59:10
Jerome Powell is expected to leave the board once the DOJ-suspended investigation is formally closed, with an inspector general report expected by end of summer.
prediction🗣 Betsy Dukeconviction: mediumJerome PowellKevin WarshDepartment of JusticeFederal Reserve
So what: Clears a potential overhang on Fed leadership continuity; Warsh is referenced as the new Fed chair.
“HE PLANS TO LEAVE AS SOON AS HE IS -- IT IS DETERMINED THIS CASE IS TRULY ENDED.” ▶ 59:59
Fed on hold, inflation stickiness and front-end positioning 4
The Fed is now priced as the most hawkish of the Fed, ECB and BOE, with about 92 basis points of change and its implied-rate line climbing over recent months while ECB and BOE odds fell.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeldconviction: highFederal ReserveECBBank of England
So what: Divergent central-bank paths favor the dollar and reprice front-end rate trades.
“RIGHT NOW THE FED IS SEEN AS THE MOST HAWKISH OF THOSE THREE. WERE LOOKING AT 92 BASIS POINTS OF CHANGE.” ▶ 2:57
PIMCO forecasts inflation staying above 3% over the medium term, keeping the Fed on hold for the remainder of the year rather than reacting proactively.
prediction🗣 Jerome Schneiderconviction: highPIMCOFederal Reserve
So what: Cash yielding ~3% underperforms inflation, so investors risk negative real returns holding cash.
“IT MIGHT TAKE SEVERAL YEARS WHICH MEANS THE FEDERAL RESERVE WILL PROBABLY BE ON ITS HANDS OVER THE COURSE OF THE NEXT SEVERAL MEETINGS” ▶ 5:28
Investors should move out of the $7.9 trillion sitting in money market funds into a diversified front-end fixed-income portfolio that can pick up 100-200 basis points and produce 5-7% equity-like returns.
thesis🗣 Jerome Schneiderconviction: highPIMCOmoney market fundsasset-backed securities
So what: Actionable rotation call: cash is a losing real trade; short-duration diversified credit offers better risk-adjusted yield.
“TO THE SHORT-TERM SPACE WHICH IS PRODUCING FIVE TO 7% RETURNS” ▶ 7:25
Much of the growth in money market funds is organic compounding plus a holding place for capital earmarked for AI pursuits, not necessarily conviction cash allocation.
causal🗣 Jerome Schneiderconviction: mediummoney market funds
So what: Headline MMF growth overstates true risk-aversion; some is dry powder awaiting deployment.
“ALSO SEEING A HOLDING PLACE FOR THE CAPITOL AI PURSUITS AND OTHER PLACES” ▶ 8:09
AI-driven equity rally, concentration and overlap risk 6
AI infrastructure stocks alone likely contributed 60% of S&P 500 EPS growth for the quarter, per Goldman strategists, and the quarter was the best ever for the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: highS&P 500Goldman SachsPhiladelphia Semiconductor IndexRBC
So what: Extreme earnings concentration in AI infra means any single miss could disproportionately hit index EPS; RBC raised its 12-month S&P target to 8150.
≈ “AS A INFRASTRUCTURE STOCKS ALONE HAVE LIKELY CONTRIBUTED 60% OF S&P EPS GROWTH FOR THE QUARTER” paraphrase
The BIS warned in its annual report that any disappointment in AI-related returns could trigger a pullback in financing that turns the current boom into a protracted bust with larger macroeconomic consequences.
contrarian🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: mediumBank for International Settlements
So what: Systemic tail risk: AI-financing fragility, amplified by levered ETFs and hedge-fund positioning, could fuel a rapid reversal.
“DURING THIS BOOM INTO A PROTRACTED BUST WITH LARGER MACROECONOMIC CONSEQUENCES” ▶ 2:32
There is hidden overlap risk because within the Russell 1000 top-10 stocks account for ~16% of value and ~55% of growth, so investors think they are diversified across value and growth but hold the same names.
framework🗣 Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)conviction: highRussell 1000Magnificent 7JPMorgan Asset Management
So what: Style diversification is illusory when Mag 7 dominates both value and growth indices; investors are unknowingly doubling down.
“PEOPLE THINK THEY HAVE VALUE. I HAVE GROWS. THERE IS OVERLAP RISK HERE” ▶ 40:34
As tech shifts from capital-light to capital-intensive due to AI buildouts, those earnings streams and their ecosystem offshoots become much more cyclical, forcing investors to prepare for cyclicality.
thesis🗣 Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)conviction: highJPMorgan Asset Managementhyperscalers
So what: The traditional treatment of tech as a secular grower breaks down; portfolios need to price in cyclical AI-capex risk.
“AS TECH MOVES FROM CAPITAL LIGHT TO CAPITAL INTENSIVE” ▶ 39:17
Financials have strong fundamentals (earnings growth, low credit-card charge-offs, M&A/IPO tailwinds) but weak sentiment and multiple compression, presenting a balanced high-quality value opportunity.
contrarian🗣 Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)conviction: mediumfinancialsS&P 500
So what: Financials, the worst-performing S&P sector, may be mispriced given they can take share from a private-credit reckoning as a loan-growth opportunity.
“FINANCIALS ARE ABLE TO TAKE THE SHARE IN THE PUBLIC MARKETS FROM PRIVATE CREDIT AS PRIVATE CREDIT GOES THROUGH A LITTLE BIT OF A RECKONING” ▶ 42:22
Just as picking internet winners 30 years ago would mostly have been wrong, today's AI winners may not be the leaders in five to ten years.
framework🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: mediumAI
So what: Cautions against concentrated bets on current AI leaders given historical difficulty of picking durable disruptors.
“PICKING THE WINNERS BACK THEN MOST LIKELY WOULD HAVE BEEN WRONG.” ▶ 23:09
Media and telecom M&A wave 4
Comcast's plan to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate company creates two pure plays (content and distribution) that each become an acquirer or target, with Comcast more likely to be an acquirer.
thesis🗣 David (Seaport Research Partners)conviction: mediumComcastNBCUniversalSkyWarner Bros. DiscoveryCharter
So what: The split unlocks strategic flexibility and could catalyze broad connectivity/media consolidation; Comcast stock hit +17% intraday, closing +4.5%, Charter +9.4%.

SpaceX having a public equity that could be used as currency introduces a new variable that could pressure media/telecom targets, especially around the Starlink asset.
causal🗣 David (Seaport Research Partners)conviction: mediumSpaceXStarlinkCharter
So what: SpaceX joining the Nasdaq 100 (July 7) and its equity currency reshape competitive dynamics for cable/telecom incumbents like Verizon and Charter.
“YOU DO HAVE THE NEW VARIABLE OF SPACEX HAVING A PUBLIC EQUITY THAT COULD BE USED” ▶ 20:27
Verizon fell 5.2% after reports that SpaceX and Charter held executive-level talks about partnering on a consumer mobile phone offering, plus a projected $700-800 million Q2 loss.
data_point🗣 Tim Stenovecconviction: highVerizonSpaceXCharter
So what: A SpaceX-Charter wireless partnership is a direct competitive threat to incumbent carriers.
“SHARES FELL AFTER BLOOMBERG NEWS REPORTED THAT SPACEX AND CHARTER HAVE HELD EXECUTIVE-LEVEL TALKS ABOUT PARTNERING ON A CONSUMER MOBILE PHONE OFFERING” ▶ 49:46
Rocket Lab's acquisition of Iridium is primarily about acquiring scarce spectrum needed to compete with Starlink, not just launch capability.
thesis🗣 Norah Mulindaconviction: mediumRocket LabIridiumStarlinkSpaceX

London Stock Exchange, data monetization and listings competition 4
Over 90% of LSEG's data is effectively proprietary, and AI is making that data more valuable because agents and models consume more data than humans through new AI distribution channels.
thesis🗣 David Schwimmerconviction: highLondon Stock Exchange GroupOpenAIAnthropicMicrosoftGoogle
So what: AI is a demand tailwind, not a threat, for proprietary financial-data owners; higher machine consumption drives revenue.
“AN AGENT OR MODEL TENDS TO CONSUME MORE DATA THAN A HUMAN. I THINK IT'S GOING TO BE A BIG POSITIVE FOR US” ▶ 25:02
UK companies that moved to list and raise capital in the US have a terrible track record: of ~20 that raised over $100M, only 3-4 are still doing well while ~17 were delisted or trade down 60-70% on average.
data_point🗣 David Schwimmerconviction: highLondon Stock Exchange Group
So what: Undercuts the narrative that US listings are superior; smaller non-US companies risk becoming orphan stocks in the US.

LSEG's IPO pipeline is the strongest Schwimmer has seen in eight years, stronger than before the financial crisis, spanning UK, Europe, Middle East and Asia into the back half and next year.

So what: Signals a genuine revival in London listings despite political instability, a competitive counterpoint to US exchanges.
“WE ARE SEEING A HEALTHIER PIPELINE THAN I'VE SEEN IN MY EIGHT YEARS” ▶ 27:59
LSEG targeted a 3 billion pound buyback (below activist Elliott's requested 5 billion) to preserve strategic flexibility while landing at 1.5-2.5x net EBITDA range by year-end.

AI labor displacement and job creation 4
Goldman estimates AI could displace more than 9% of workers over the full transition (up from an earlier ~6% estimate), driven by AI delivering 15% gross productivity upside after full adoption.
prediction🗣 Joseph Briggsconviction: highGoldman Sachs

“A.I. WOULD DELIVER 15% GROSS PRODUCTIVITY UPSIDE FOLLOWING FULL ADOPTION” ▶ 1:01:57
AI hiring impact is already visible and concentrated in tech, management consulting and graphic design, where hiring over the last couple years has underperformed by around 5%.

“HIRING OF THE LAST COUPLE OF YEARS HAS UNDERPERFORMED BY AROUND 5%” ▶ 1:02:54
85% of US job growth over the last 85 years has been driven by technology-led creative destruction, and gross job creation runs 25-35 million a year versus ~1 million net.
framework🗣 Joseph Briggsconviction: highGoldman Sachs

“85% OF JOB GROWTH OVER THE LAST 85 YEARS HAS BEEN DRIVEN BY THE CREATIVE DESTRUCTION DUE TO TECHNOLOGY” ▶ 1:04:51
Data center construction is already generating 200,000 to 250,000 jobs, and specific AI use cases show productivity uplift of 20-30% in applications to date.

“ALREADY TODAY WE ARE SEEING 200,000 TO 250,000 JOBS EMERGE ON THE BACK OF DATA CENTER CONSTRUCTION” ▶ 1:05:43
Defense, aerospace and consumer/retail signals 5
Honeywell Aerospace enters its spinoff with a $90 billion backlog and $19 billion of current-year demand (up 20% year-on-year), with capacity unlock rather than M&A as its top capital priority.

Trump & Vance Release Financial Disclosure Reports | Balance of Power 06/30/202653 min27 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's Balance of Power breaks President Trump's 900+ page financial disclosure showing $1.2B in crypto-related earnings (mostly from World Liberty Financial) plus large NVIDIA, Apple and Palantir stock positions, alongside a mixed Supreme Court term (birthright citizenship upheld, campaign-finance limits struck down, presidential removal power expanded) and updates on Iran/Hormuz talks, immigration-driven labor shortages, USMCA and Nike earnings.
Trump financial disclosure: crypto and mega-cap tech holdings · 6Crypto legislation and the emoluments question · 4Supreme Court term: citizenship, campaign finance, removal power · 4Immigration policy and labor-market squeeze · 4Energy prices, Iran and Strait of Hormuz · 3Trade, China and semiconductor supply chain · 3Congressional gridlock and Nike earnings · 3
Trump financial disclosure: crypto and mega-cap tech holdings 6
Trump's 2025 financial disclosure reports more than $1.2 billion in crypto-related earnings, of which roughly $588 million came from World Liberty Financial, the crypto firm co-founded by Trump, his sons and Steve Witkoff.
data_point🗣 Megan Scullyconviction: highDonald TrumpWorld Liberty FinancialSteve Witkoffcryptocurrency
So what: Ties the sitting president's personal wealth directly to a crypto venture his administration is deregulating, sharpening conflict-of-interest scrutiny.
“THE $1.2 BILLION IN EARNINGS FROM CRYPTO INCLUDES $588 BILLION IN EARNINGS FROM ROAD LIBERTY FINANCIAL WHICH IS OF COURSE THE FIRM THAT DR. CRYPTO FROM THAT TRUMP AND HIS SONS AND STEVE WITKOFF AND OTHERS HAD STARTED.” ▶ 31:20
Trump's disclosure shows personal stock positions of roughly $5 million in NVIDIA, $5 million in Apple and at least $1 million in Palantir shares.
data_point🗣 Joe Mathieuconviction: highDonald TrumpNVIDIAApplePalantir
So what: The president holds concentrated positions in AI/tech names whose businesses depend heavily on federal policy and government contracts.
“DONALD TRUMP BOUGHT AT LEAST $100,000 IN PALANTIR SHARES... $5 MILLION IN NVIDIA SHARES AND $5 MILLION MORE IN APPLE SHARES.” ▶ 1:51
Trump's crypto gains came largely from smaller altcoins including his own tokens rather than bitcoin, which was relatively flat over the period and would have generated far smaller returns.
causal🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: highDonald TrumpbitcoinTrump coincryptocurrency
So what: The outsized returns reflect insider token issuance, not passive crypto market beta, underscoring how the profits were manufactured.
“IT WASN'T FROM BITCOIN. A LOT OF IT WAS TIED TO THE OTHER SMALLER COINS INCLUDING TRUMP'S OWN COINS THAT APPARENTLY CONTRIBUTED TO THE NUMBER.” ▶ 21:16
Trump's disclosure shows he sold at least $250,000 of Meta stock and $250,000 of Apple on October 16, 2025, indicating active trading in the president's portfolio.
data_point🗣 Joe Mathieuconviction: mediumDonald TrumpMetaApple
So what: Documented dated sell transactions add fuel to STOCK Act and conflict-of-interest debates around a president trading individual equities.
“PRESIDENT TRUMP SOLD AT LEAST $250,000 WORTH OF IN BETA STOCK ON THE OCTOBER 16... AND SOLD A QUARTER MILLION DOLLARS OF APPLE ON THAT SAME DAY.” ▶ 42:40
Palantir, Apple and NVIDIA were the highest-flying stocks of 2025 but have lagged significantly in the first six months of 2026, with money rotating into memory chipmakers like SanDisk and Micron.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostickconviction: highPalantirAppleNVIDIASanDiskMicronmemory chips
So what: Signals a leadership rotation within tech from 2025 AI winners into memory names, a tradeable shift for equity investors.
“A LOT OF FOLKS WHO HAVE CHASED THE TECH TRADE HAVE ROTATED INTO OTHER NAMES IN THAT SPACE, PRIMARILY MEMORY CHIPMAKERS... INCLUDING SANDISK AND MICRON.” ▶ 22:03
Congress currently lacks a STOCK Act-style check, so there is no legislative mechanism forcing the president or members to divest holdings in companies with business before the government.
framework🗣 Megan Scullyconviction: mediumCongressSTOCK ActDonald Trump
So what: Absence of enforcement means disclosed conflicts persist and become a political rather than legal issue heading into midterms.
“OR A STOCK ACT, YES INDEED. WHAT IS SO ALARMING OR SURPRISING ABOUT THIS IS JUST THE SCOPE OF IT, IT'S MORE THAN 900 PAGES.” ▶ 31:13
Crypto legislation and the emoluments question 4
The Trump disclosure could complicate passage of the CLARITY Act crypto market-structure bill because Democrats holding out for ethics provisions now have concrete disclosures to cite as justification.
prediction🗣 Kailey Leinzconviction: mediumCLARITY ActcryptocurrencyCongressDemocrats
So what: Crypto market-structure legislation may stall despite House passage, keeping regulatory uncertainty for the sector.
“MAYBE IT WILL BE MORE DIFFICULT FOR CLARITY TO GET THROUGH THOUGH IT HAD ALREADY PASSED THE HOUSE.” ▶ 31:58
Rep. Greg Stanton argues foreign direct investment into World Liberty Financial that personally profits the president may violate the Constitution's emoluments clause, a bigger issue than the CLARITY Act itself.
thesis🗣 Greg Stantonconviction: highWorld Liberty FinancialDonald TrumpSteve Witkoffemoluments clause
So what: Frames the crypto earnings as a potential constitutional violation, escalating the legal and political stakes beyond ethics rules.
“WHAT ABOUT THE EMOLUMENTS CLAUSE THE UNITED STATES CONSTITUTION? SO MUCH OF THAT BILLION PLUS DOLLARS YOU DESCRIBED HAVE COME FROM DIRECT INVESTMENT INTO WORLD LIBERTY FINANCIAL.” ▶ 41:46
Trump-linked meme coins have collapsed from their peaks, with Melania coin down from around $8.50 to seven cents and Trump coin down from around $45 to $1.66.
data_point🗣 Joe Mathieuconviction: highTrump coinMelania coinbitcoincryptocurrency
So what: Demonstrates that retail buyers of the president's tokens lost heavily even as insiders realized gains, a reputational and regulatory flashpoint.
“IT TOPPED OUT AROUND $8.50, TRADING TODAY FOR SEVEN CENTS. TRUMP COINED TOPPED OUT AROUND $45... IT IS NOW $1.66.” ▶ 48:46
Democrats plan to make Trump's crypto enrichment and a related pardon of a World Liberty Financial investor a central 'culture of corruption' pillar in 2026 midterm races, including the Texas Senate contest.
prediction🗣 Adamconviction: highDemocratsWorld Liberty FinancialKen PaxtonJames TalaricoTexas

“TALKING ABOUT THE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION AND THE PRESIDENT HIMSELF, ENRICHING HIMSELF AND ENGAGING IN ALL TYPES OF CORRUPT BEHAVIOR IS DOING NOTHING TO LOWER COSTS FOR THE AMERICAN PEOPLE.” ▶ 49:20
Supreme Court term: citizenship, campaign finance, removal power 4
The Supreme Court ruled 6-3, in an opinion by Chief Justice Roberts, that Trump's executive order restricting birthright citizenship violated the 14th Amendment, upholding citizenship for anyone born on U.S. soil.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg legal reporterconviction: highSupreme CourtChief Justice RobertsDonald Trump14th Amendment
So what: Removes near-term legal risk to birthright citizenship, giving employers hiring certainty but leaving Trump to pursue a legislative route.
“THE COURT WAS 6-3 AND SAYING TRUMP OVERREACH BY TRYING TO RESTRICT BIRTHRIGHT CITIZENSHIP.” ▶ 3:07
The Court struck down long-standing federal limits on political parties coordinating spending with candidates, letting parties spend more and access lower candidate ad rates.
causal🗣 Bloomberg legal reporterconviction: highSupreme Courtcampaign financeRepublican Party
So what: Expect a surge of coordinated party advertising into the election, structurally favoring the better-funded party.
“THEY CAN SPEND MORE MONEY IN COORDINATION WITH CANDIDATES AND THEY CAN BENEFIT FROM LOWER AD RATES THAT CANDIDATES GET.” ▶ 4:42
A Supreme Court decision in the Slaughter case expanded the president's power to fire officials at independent agencies, carving out an exception only for the Federal Reserve.
causal🗣 Joe Mathieuconviction: highSupreme CourtRebecca Kelly SlaughterFTCFederal ReserveDonald Trump
So what: Materially increases executive control over independent regulators every administration change, raising policy-continuity risk for regulated industries; Fed independence preserved.
“IT IS A MASSIVE TRANSFER OF POWER FROM CONGRESS TO THE PRESIDENT... INTO INSTRUMENTS OF THE POWERFUL AND THE EXECUTIVE.” ▶ 43:24
Even with legislation, Republican Rep. LaLota believes there is a legislative path to narrow birthright citizenship to lawfully present residents, while a Democratic analyst calls it settled since 1868 and 1890s rulings.
thesis🗣 Rep. Nick LaLotaconviction: mediumCongress14th Amendmentbirthright citizenship
So what: Legislative fight over citizenship will persist but faces steep constitutional and bipartisan hurdles, limiting near-term policy change.
“I DO THINK THERE IS A LEGISLATIVE PATH TO NARROW THE SCOPE TO PERHAPS LIMIT BIRTHRIGHT CITIZENSHIP TO THOSE WHO ARE LAWFULLY PRESENT.” ▶ 11:44
Immigration policy and labor-market squeeze 4
The ruling ending Haitian Temporary Protected Status threatens the workforce in nursing homes and long-term care, where 30% of the 350,000 Haitian TPS holders work, potentially leaving over a million senior citizens without care.

“WE ARE TALKING WELL OVER A MILLION PEOPLE WHO ARE UNDER THIS WORK AUTHORIZATION STATUS THAT ARE CONTRIBUTING.” ▶ 28:10
Healthcare has been the only sector consistently adding jobs in recent months, making it especially exposed to immigration-driven labor withdrawal.
data_point🗣 Rebecca (American Business Immigration Coalition CEO)conviction: mediumhealthcarejobs datalabor market

“THE ONLY SECTOR THAT HAS BEEN IMPROVING CONSISTENTLY IN TERMS OF JOBS HAS BEEN HEALTH CARE.” ▶ 27:51
Highly skilled immigrants are increasingly choosing other countries over the U.S. because they don't feel welcome, threatening U.S. job creation and talent inflows.

“INSTEAD OF COMING TO AMERICA, THEY ARE DECIDING TO GO TO OR ANOTHER COUNTRY BECAUSE THEY DON'T FEEL WELCOME IN THIS COUNTRY.” ▶ 29:18
Energy prices, Iran and Strait of Hormuz 3
With oil around $70 a barrel, gasoline retail prices have not fallen at the same rate; New York averages about $4.10 versus a $3.84 national average, and Trump is pushing for $2.50 and alleging price gouging.

U.S.-Iran indirect talks in Doha remain stuck on control of the Strait of Hormuz, with Iran claiming it will maintain control via fees and uncertainty around a promised $300 billion allied contribution fund.

A Financial Times report says Apple is lobbying the White House for approval to buy scarce, pricier memory chips from Chinese company CXMT, which the Pentagon blacklisted over ties to the Chinese military.
data_point🗣 Joe Mathieuconviction: highAppleCXMTmemory chipsChinaPentagon

Rep. Stanton warns that cancelling USMCA rather than modernizing it would push Canada and Mexico toward China, citing Canada already pursuing Chinese EV import deals, and would weaken the North American semiconductor supply chain.

The USMCA faces a deadline the following day, with Trump signaling he would like the agreement cancelled altogether rather than automatically renewed.
data_point🗣 Kailey Leinzconviction: mediumUSMCADonald Trumptrade

“PRESIDENT TRUMP IS GONZO FOR US TO SAY THAT HE WOULD LIKE TO SEE USMCA CANCELED ALL TOGETHER.” ▶ 38:29

A House procedural rule vote failed with 14 Republicans defecting over an attempt to attach the SAVE Act voter-ID legislation to the must-pass defense authorization bill, sending lawmakers home for July 4 recess.

Nike fell about 8% after hours on earnings, with North America growth negative, discretionary-spending pressure, pull-forward in demand and difficulty clearing inventory built up under the prior CEO.
