Trump Calls Bipartisan Housing Bill 'A Big Yawn' | Balance of Power 06/29/202648 min26 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's Balance of Power covers a market-relevant Supreme Court day: the Court carved out the Fed from a sweeping ruling expanding presidential firing power (letting Lisa Cook keep her seat for now), while a bipartisan housing-affordability bill heads to Trump's desk that he dismisses as 'a big yawn' in favor of the stalled SAVE America Act. Additional segments cover fragile US-Iran talks over the Strait of Hormuz, a mail-in-ballot ruling, and the erosion of skilled-immigrant appeal to the US.
Fed independence carveout and Lisa Cook litigation risk · 5Sweeping expansion of presidential removal power · 3Housing affordability bill and GOP midterm messaging · 5US-Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz oil risk · 6Immigration policy and US skilled-labor competitiveness · 3Elections ruling and market-day tape · 4
Fed independence carveout and Lisa Cook litigation risk 5
The Supreme Court explicitly treated the Federal Reserve as distinct from other independent agencies in its removal-power rulings, letting Governor Lisa Cook keep her seat for now even as it expanded presidential firing power elsewhere.
data_point🗣 Enda Curran / Natasha Sarinconviction: highFederal ReserveLisa CookSupreme CourtSlaughter caseFTC
So what: Removes near-term tail risk of direct White House control over monetary policy, explaining the muted bond-market reaction.
“IT IS A CLEAR WIN FOR THE FED. THE FED WINS TO FIGHT ANOTHER DAY.” ▶ 5:18
The Lisa Cook case is not over because the Court ruled on narrow procedural grounds (lack of due process) and left open whether proven mortgage-fraud allegations could later constitute adequate 'cause' to remove a Fed governor.
causal🗣 Jessica Rothconviction: highLisa CookSupreme CourtFederal ReserveJohn Roberts
So what: Fed-independence uncertainty remains an open litigation overhang that could return to the Supreme Court.
“WHAT THEY REALLY FOCUSED ON AT THE END OF THE DAY WAS THE LACK OF PROCESS HERE” ▶ 11:16
The bond and housing markets reacted more to the Fed ruling than to the housing bill, with Treasury yields staying flat because the outcome favored the Fed and was roughly where the Court was expected to land.
causal🗣 Natasha Sarinconviction: mediumTreasury marketbond marketFederal Reserve
So what: The decision was already priced in, so it validates rather than moves the rates market.
“THIS IS WHERE THE COURT WAS EXPECTED TO LAND ON THIS QUESTION” ▶ 8:12
Chief Justice Roberts grounded Fed exceptionalism in two pillars—the unique history of US central banking and the Fed's singular role in maintaining global financial stability—and warned that even the appearance of independence matters.
framework🗣 Jessica Roth / Natasha Sarinconviction: highJohn RobertsFederal ReserveSupreme Court
So what: Gives markets a durable legal rationale for why the Fed should stay insulated from politics.
“MONETARY POLICY SHOULD NOT BE SUBJECT TO POLITICAL INTERFERENCE” ▶ 11:38
The 1970s precedent of politically driven Fed policy under Nixon saw US inflation rise from 3% to 13% over two years, cited as the economic case against political interference in monetary policy.
data_point🗣 Natasha Sarinconviction: highFederal ReserveRichard Nixoninflation
So what: Provides the historical inflation-risk anchor investors use to price Fed-independence threats.
“OVER TWO YEARS INFLATION IN THIS COUNTRY GO UP FROM 3% TO 13%” ▶ 12:25
Sweeping expansion of presidential removal power 3
The Court's Slaughter ruling (6-3) overturned a 93-year-old precedent underpinning the modern administrative state, letting the president fire officials at most independent executive-branch agencies (regulators included), excluding only the Fed.
data_point🗣 Jeff Mason / Joe Mathieuconviction: highSupreme CourtSlaughter caseFTCindependent agenciesDonald Trump
So what: Concentrates regulatory control in the White House and sets precedent future presidents of both parties will inherit.
≈ “IT OVERTURNS A 93-YEAR-OLD PRECEDENT THAT LEAD TO THE LEGAL GROUNDWORK FOR WHAT WE CONSIDER THE MODERN ADMINISTRATIVE STATE” paraphrase
The ruling risks a governance pattern where independent-agency leadership is churned every four to eight years with each new president, and could clog a president's first year with leadership fights.
prediction🗣 Jamie Jackson / Joe Mathieuconviction: mediumindependent agenciesDonald Trump
So what: Regulatory continuity for markets may erode, raising policy volatility at agencies like the FTC and SEC.
Trump has already de facto neutered several independent agencies by simply declining to appoint commissioners, leaving bodies like the Federal Election Commission without a functioning quorum.
anecdote🗣 Rick Davisconviction: highFederal Election CommissionDonald Trumpindependent agencies
So what: The practical effect of the ruling may be smaller than feared because agencies were already hobbled.
“JUST DON'T APPOINT ANYBODY AND THEY DON'T HAVE A QUORUM” ▶ 42:29
Housing affordability bill and GOP midterm messaging 5
The bipartisan 'Road to Housing' affordability bill has been sent to Trump, starting a 10-day clock after which it becomes law without his signature unless he vetoes it, and a veto would likely be overridden.
prediction🗣 Erik Wasson / Rick Davisconviction: highhousing billMike JohnsonDonald TrumpCongress
So what: The housing bill is likely to become law regardless of Trump's stance, a supply-side positive for homebuilding.
“10 DAYS FROM NOW BECOMES LAW WITHOUT HIM DOING A THING” ▶ 47:18
The housing bill is a supply-side measure that reduces environmental regulations for apartment buildings and limits private-equity investors' ability to buy up single-family homes.
data_point🗣 Erik Wassonconviction: highhousing billprivate equitysingle-family homesapartment buildings
So what: Directly relevant to homebuilders and institutional single-family-rental buyers whose acquisition runway could be constrained.
“REDUCING ENVIRONMENTAL REGULATIONS FOR APARTMENT BUILDINGS, LIMITING THE ABILITY OF RICH PRIVATE EQUITY INVESTORS TO BUY UP SINGLE-FAMILY HOMES” ▶ 20:21
Trump's dismissal of the housing bill as 'a big yawn' steps on Republicans' key affordability message heading into the midterms, reflecting reporting that he is disengaged from midterm strategy.
thesis🗣 Erik Wassonconviction: highDonald TrumpRepublicanshousing billmidterms
So what: Signals internal GOP friction that could weaken their economic messaging and midterm hold on Congress.
“THE PRESIDENT IS NOT FOCUSED ON THE MIDTERMS, NOT TRAVELING AS MUCH AS HE SHOULD” ▶ 20:44
The House floor is effectively shut down as Rep. Anna Paulina Luna holds legislation hostage to attach the SAVE America Act, stalling the defense policy bill and online kids-safety legislation.
data_point🗣 Joe Mathieu / Erik Wassonconviction: highAnna Paulina LunaSAVE America Actdefense policy billCongress
So what: Legislative gridlock delays defense and other must-pass bills, raising execution risk for the GOP agenda.
There is no viable path for the SAVE Act in this Congress because attaching it would poison the defense bill (which needs Democrats) and the full version violates Senate budget rules requiring 60 votes.
prediction🗣 Mick Mulvaney / Erik Wassonconviction: highSAVE ActSAVE America ActJohn ThuneSenatefilibuster
So what: Trump's top legislative priority is likely dead, meaning the floor standoff has no near-term legislative resolution.
“I STILL DON'T SEE A PATHWAY FOR SUCCESS FOR THE SAVE ACT” ▶ 21:39
US-Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz oil risk 6
Persistent low-level violence in the Strait of Hormuz is a deliberate feature of the US-Iran conflict, not a bug, as both sides shoot to shape negotiations while avoiding full-scale war.
framework🗣 Becca Wasserconviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: Implies a durable oil-supply risk premium rather than a clean ceasefire, keeping shipping and crude volatility elevated.
“IT'S A FEATURE OF THE CONFLICT. IT IS NOT A BUG.” ▶ 25:30
The US is providing armed overwatch to commercial ships transiting the Strait, and Bloomberg's defense analyst expects an uptick in that activity to restore shipping confidence after Iranian drone strikes.
prediction🗣 Becca Wasserconviction: mediumUnited StatesIranStrait of Hormuzcommercial shipping
So what: Continued US naval commitment supports oil flow but signals ongoing tension, relevant to freight rates and insurance.
“I WOULDN'T BE SURPRISED IF WE ACTUALLY SEE AN UPTICK IN U.S. OVERWATCH ACTIVITY” ▶ 27:56
Iran wants to be paid transit fees to 'manage' the Strait and has been attacking commercial shipping to enforce its preferred channel, while the US insists on freedom of navigation—making violence likely to persist despite any ceasefire.
thesis🗣 Stephen A. Cookconviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: The core dispute over Strait control has no easy resolution, sustaining a structural oil-price risk.
“THAT INCLUDES SOME SORT OF FEE FOR TRANSIT. THAT'S WHY THEY'VE BEEN ATTACKING COMMERCIAL SHIPPING” ▶ 30:33
Trump is primarily focused on the free flow of oil and US gasoline prices rather than fully halting Iran's nuclear program, and is looking for a face-saving exit from the conflict.
thesis🗣 Stephen A. Cookconviction: highDonald TrumpIranoilgasoline prices
So what: US policy is anchored to energy prices, so oil-market stability may be prioritized over nuclear enforcement.
“THE PRESIDENT IS MAINLY INTERESTED IN THE QUESTION OF THE FREE FLOW OF OIL AND WHAT IT MEANS FOR GASOLINE PRICES” ▶ 29:38
The Vance-brokered MOU with Iran is lopsided in Iran's favor, giving Iran more up-front sanctions relief than the JCPOA ever did, so Iran would not accept the old JCPOA if re-offered.
contrarian🗣 Stephen A. Cookconviction: highIranJD VanceJCPOAMOUsanctions
So what: Sanctions relief flowing to Iran reshapes the outlook for Iranian oil supply returning to global markets.
“THEY ARE CERTAINLY GETTING MORE AND THE WAY OF SANCTIONS RELIEF UP FRONT THAN THEY EVER DID WITH THE JCPOA” ▶ 34:43
The Israel-Lebanon agreement looks good on paper but is unlikely to be implemented due to opposition from Iran, Hezbollah, and pro-Hezbollah Lebanese politicians.
prediction🗣 Stephen A. Cookconviction: mediumIsraelLebanonHezbollahIran
So what: Regional de-escalation that could ease the broader conflict is fragile, keeping Middle East risk elevated.
“ALTHOUGH A GOOD AGREEMENT, I AM SUSPICIOUS. I DON'T BELIEVE IT'S GOING TO BE IMPLEMENTED.” ▶ 32:27
Immigration policy and US skilled-labor competitiveness 3
Roughly half of expected US labor-force productivity growth is predicated on continued immigration and birthright citizenship, so restrictive immigration policy is already cascading onto the economy of an aging country.
causal🗣 Natasha Sarinconviction: highimmigrationlabor forcebirthright citizenship
So what: Immigration curbs pose a structural drag on US growth potential that investors should weigh in long-run productivity assumptions.
“HALF OF EXPECTED LABOR FORCE PRODUCTIVITY IS COMING ON THE HEELS OF THE IDEA THAT YOU THINK IMMIGRANTS ARE GOING TO BE MOVING INTO THIS COUNTRY” ▶ 15:18
Two-thirds of Silicon Valley tech workers are foreign-born, and the AI-fueled US economy has long relied on skilled immigrants from countries like India and China who are now reconsidering the US amid visa chaos.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg Big Take reporterconviction: highSilicon ValleyH-1B visaF-1 visaIndiaChinaAI
So what: Erosion of US appeal to top tech talent threatens the innovation edge underpinning US tech-sector valuations.
“TWO THIRDS OF TECH WORKERS IN SILICON VALLEY ARE FOREIGN-BORN” ▶ 38:39
The US issues about 400,000 F-1 student visas and 85,000 H-1B work visas annually, with the H-1B historically the most viable path to a green card, but administration flip-flopping is now deterring skilled immigrants.

“EACH YEAR AMERICA ISSUES 400,000 F-1 VISAS... THEY OFFER 85,000 H-1B VISAS” ▶ 38:19
Elections ruling and market-day tape 4
The Supreme Court ruled 5-4 (Barrett and Roberts joining the liberals) that federal law permits mail-in ballots postmarked by Election Day to be counted after the fact, preserving grace periods in 30 states.

Kevin Warsh to Speak at Sintra, ECB Warns Inflation Shock Not Over | Daybreak Europe 7/1/202647 min38 insights2026-07-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg Daybreak Europe previews Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first international appearance at the ECB Sintra forum amid a hawkish central-bank tilt: traders now price a >1-in-3 chance of a Fed rate HIKE (not cut), ECB officials warn the Iran-war inflation shock is stagflationary and not over, and Anthropic wins removal of US export curbs on its frontier AI model.
Fed under Warsh: hike risk, not cut · 8ECB and the stagflationary Iran shock · 7FX: yen and won intervention watch · 4AI capex cycle and semis leadership · 6Anthropic export curbs lifted and AI-for-science push · 4Oil, Iran and the Strait of Hormuz · 4Corporates, consumer and political-risk signals · 5
Fed under Warsh: hike risk, not cut 8
Interest-rate swaps are pricing more than a one-in-three chance of the Fed moving rates HIGHER, an unusual repricing toward a hike rather than a cut following Kevin Warsh's hawkish commentary and focus on price stability.
data_point🗣 Lizzy (host)conviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Positioning is skewed to a surprise Fed hike, a tail risk most portfolios are not built for.
“INTEREST-RATE SWAPS ARE PRICING MORE THAN A ONE IN THREE CHANCE OF A MOVE HIGHER BY THE FED.” ▶ 5:43
Warsh may signal a single early 'insurance' rate hike to build credibility on inflation, buying time and avoiding a later series of aggressive hikes, mirroring the ECB's recent quick 25bp move.
prediction🗣 Mark (strategist)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal ReserveECB
So what: An early insurance hike would front-run the curve and reset rate-cut expectations for 2026.
“HE GOES FOR A SMALL MOVE EARLY TO GET PEOPLE ON SIDE, BUYS HIM A BIT OF TIME AND THEN HE DOESN'T NEED TO FOLLOW UP WITH AGGRESSIVE HIKES DOWN THE ROAD.” ▶ 6:41
A Fed hike is seen most likely in September or October rather than July, with the July meeting described as still a long shot, driven ultimately by CPI data due later in the month rather than the jobs report.
prediction🗣 Mark (strategist)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveCPI
So what: Sets a concrete timeline: watch CPI, not payrolls, as the decisive trigger for the first hike.
“WE MIGHT GET A LITTLE HIKE PROBABLY IN SEPTEMBER OR OCTOBER. JULY IS STILL A LONG SHOT.” ▶ 8:52
Last week's PCE printed at 4%, described as way too high, so the Fed needs strong evidence inflation can fall before holding, otherwise a hike follows.
data_point🗣 Mark (strategist)conviction: highPCEFederal Reserve
So what: A 4% PCE anchors the hawkish case and raises the bar for the Fed to stay on hold.
“THEY'VE SEEN THE PC LAST WEEK OF 4%, WAY TOO HIGH.” ▶ 8:42
Planned changes to how the Bureau of Economic Analysis calculates the PCE price index, taking effect in September, may cut core PCE inflation and make Warsh's job easier on the inflation front.
causal🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumBureau of Economic AnalysisPCEKevin Warsh
So what: A methodological change could mechanically lower reported inflation, reducing the pressure to hike.
“PLANNED CHANGES TO HOW THE BUREAU OF ECONOMIC ANALYSIS CALCULATES THE PC PRICE INDEX THAT TAKES EFFECT IN SEPTEMBER. MAYBE THAT WILL CUT CORE PCE INFLATION” ▶ 13:00
Warsh must push back against FOMC members who want to hike simply to prove their independence to Donald Trump, framing internal Fed politics as a driver of policy risk.
contrarian🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumKevin WarshFOMCDonald TrumpFederal Reserve
So what: Fed policy may be shaped by signaling independence from Trump rather than pure macro data.
“HE ALSO HAS TO PUSH BACK AGAINST A LOT OF PEOPLE ON THE FOMC THAT WANTS TO HIKE JUST TO PROVE TO DONALD TRUMP THEY ARE INDEPENDENT.” ▶ 13:11
Nordea (Sabrina) expects the Fed to remain on hold until year-end despite Warsh's hawkish messaging, contrasting with market pricing that has moved toward a hike.
prediction🗣 Sabrina (senior economist)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: A credible economist view that the market is over-pricing hawkishness offers a fade opportunity.
“ACTUALLY WE EXPECT THE FED TO REMAIN ON HOLD UNTIL THE END OF THE YEAR.” ▶ 27:07
The US economy is described as K-shaped, with private consumption revised down in Q1 GDP and households doing less well, while reduced labor supply from lower migration has cut the payroll level consistent with a steady economy.
framework🗣 Sabrina (senior economist)conviction: mediumUS economyGDPlabor market
So what: Reframes strong headline data as masking weaker household demand and a structurally lower breakeven payroll number.
“WHEN WE LOOK AT THE U.S. ECONOMY CURRENTLY, IT'S MORE OF A KATE SHAPE.” ▶ 27:51
ECB and the stagflationary Iran shock 7
The Bank of Finland governor characterizes the Iran-war shock as clearly stagflationary, not a 1970s-style deep stagflation but a similar kind, with inflation up in the short term while growth has weakened.
framework🗣 Bank of Finland governorconviction: highBank of FinlandIranECBinflation
So what: A sitting ECB governor labeling the shock stagflationary supports a hold-or-hike bias over cuts.
“THE SHOCK HAS BEEN CLEARLY STAGFLATIONARY, NOT LIKE THE 1970'S, THAT WAS DEEP STAGFLATION, BUT IT IS A SERIAL KIND.” ▶ 33:01
The Bank of Finland governor's personal view is that policymakers should be prepared for a prolonged Iran conflict and plenty of geopolitical uncertainty even while hoping a ceasefire holds.
thesis🗣 Bank of Finland governorconviction: mediumBank of FinlandIran
So what: Central banks are planning for durable geopolitical risk, keeping an inflation-risk premium in rates.
“MY OWN PERSONAL VIEW IS WE BETTER BE PREPARED FOR A PROLONGED CONFLICT WHILE WE HOPE A CEASE-FIRE WILL HOLD.” ▶ 34:23
Three central bank governors interviewed at Sintra were hawkish at the margins and uniformly stressed being data-dependent meeting-by-meeting, giving no clear signal on July versus September ECB moves.
data_point🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: highECBSintra
So what: The consensus hawkish-but-non-committal stance means markets, not guidance, are pricing the path.
“THEY WERE HAWKISH AT THE MARGINS. CAUTIOUS” ▶ 10:32
Christine Lagarde pushed back on framing the June ECB hike as an insurance hike, and the ECB is relying on markets to keep a hike priced in because if the market removes it, the ECB would have to start over.
causal🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumECBChristine Lagarde
So what: The ECB is deliberately managing market expectations rather than committing, so guidance stays vague by design.
“THEY ARE COUNTING ON THE MARKETS DOING A LOT OF TIMING FOR THEM AND THE NEED TO MAKE SURE THE MARKET DOESN'T TAKE A HIKE OFF THE TABLE” ▶ 11:39
Nordea (Sabrina) expects another ECB rate hike in September, citing sentiment indicators showing output prices easing less than input prices, meaning firms are protecting margins and retaining pricing power.
prediction🗣 Sabrina (senior economist)conviction: mediumECBeurozone inflation
So what: Sticky margin-driven pricing power is the specific mechanism keeping a September ECB hike live.
“WE EXPECT ANOTHER RATE HIKE IN SEPTEMBER BECAUSE THEY ARE AT RISK” ▶ 29:22
Eurozone inflation is expected to trade around 3% at year-end, with a negative base effect from lower oil prices only pulling inflation back toward target around March next year.
prediction🗣 Sabrina (senior economist)conviction: mediumeurozone inflationoil
So what: Gives a datable timeline: no return to target until roughly March 2027, keeping the ECB restrictive into H1.
“THE END OF THE YEAR, INFLATION TRADING AROUND 3%.” ▶ 30:37
Analysts are uncertain whether Warsh is a hawk or dove, viewing him as politician-like after giving very few clues in prior testimony, making the Sintra policy panel the key catalyst for reading his rate stance.
thesis🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumKevin WarshSintra

“IT WAS THE BEST QUARTER EVER FOR THE STOCKS, UP MORE THAN 100% IN THE FIRST HALF OF 2026.” ▶ 44:36
AI is expected to be inflationary during the investment stage and eventually deflationary as productivity gains arrive, but the transition implies higher interest rates for good reason given higher growth potential.
framework🗣 Sabrina (senior economist)conviction: mediumAIproductivityinterest rates
So what: Frames AI capex as a rates-positive force near-term, cautioning against betting on AI-led disinflation yet.
“IT WOULD LEAD TO HIGHER INTEREST RATES FOR GOOD REASON BECAUSE OF HIGHER GROWTH POTENTIAL.” ▶ 32:00
Despite the AI boom, total-factor productivity even in the US remains below its long-term average and below late-1990s and early-2000s levels, revealing a discrepancy between AI adoption and measured productivity.

Anthropic export curbs lifted and AI-for-science push 4
The US government lifted export restrictions on foreign access to Anthropic's frontier AI model (referred to as Mythos/Fable Five) after Anthropic added cybersecurity safeguards that fixed jailbreak-style behavior letting users bypass security, resolving Trump-administration safety concerns.
data_point🗣 senior strategistconviction: highAnthropicTrump administrationChina

Chinese AI models have recently been catching up with US frontier models, so lifting the Anthropic curbs is framed as enabling the US to keep pace with China's AI development.
causal🗣 senior strategistconviction: mediumChinaAnthropicAI
So what: US export policy is being calibrated in direct competitive response to Chinese frontier-model progress.
“SOME OF THE CHINESE AI MODELS HAD BEEN CATCHING UP WITH SOME OF THE U.S. FRONTIER MODELS.” ▶ 20:16
Anthropic's new AI software for scientists can accelerate drug-discovery time-to-market by searching many databases at once, making Google a competitive threat and, per the strategist, pushing Google from AI-for-science leader to playing catch-up.
thesis🗣 senior strategistconviction: mediumAnthropicGoogledrug discoveryAI

Oil, Iran and the Strait of Hormuz 4
US-Iran talks in Doha involving Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff ended positively, with Iran claiming $12 million of frozen assets unlocked and oil sanctions lifted, though the Strait of Hormuz control remains the key unresolved sticking point.

Iran maintains it will begin charging tolls on the Strait of Hormuz once the 60-day negotiation period ends, with or without Oman, while the US (via Rubio) calls tolling completely unacceptable and Macron backs the strait as an uncontrolled natural waterway.

Morgan Stanley cut its oil forecast for the second time in two weeks to around $75 a barrel for Q3 and Q4, and Goldman Sachs sees an oversupply hitting markets later this year once flow through the strait normalizes.

President Trump reportedly opted out of a military option in Iran and does not mind if negotiations run past the August deadline, per the Wall Street Journal, signaling strong US commitment to a deal.

Iran Says it Will Control Hormuz & Has No Plans to Meet US in Qatar | Daybreak Europe 6/30/202646 min27 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
On the last trading day of a quarter that saw global equities post their best gains in ~6-17 years on the AI trade, Bloomberg Daybreak Europe frames three market-moving crosscurrents: Iran's threat to toll the Strait of Hormuz (yet oil is falling toward its worst quarter since the pandemic on faster-than-expected flows and a Morgan Stanley glut call), a yen collapse to a 40-year low against a dominant dollar, and a Supreme Court ruling that shields Fed Governor Lisa Cook while broadly expanding presidential power to fire agency heads.
Hormuz Toll Threat vs. Oil Glut Paradox · 7King Dollar and the 40-Year Yen Low · 5AI-Fueled Quarter and Market Bifurcation · 4Fed Independence and Executive Power · 2ECB Resilience and Trade Frictions · 5Political and Structural Risk Signals · 4
Hormuz Toll Threat vs. Oil Glut Paradox 7
Iran intends to impose a toll on traffic through the Strait of Hormuz once its 60-day negotiation window with the US expires, and says it will do so with or without Oman's blessing.
causal🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzOmanUnited States
So what: A tolled Hormuz would be a structural, standing tax on ~20% of global oil flows rather than a one-off war-risk spike.
“WITH OR WITHOUT THE BLESSING OF OHMAN... THEY WILL TOLL THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ ALONGSIDE WITH OHMAN.” ▶ 9:30
Iran officials state the Strait of Hormuz will never return to its pre-war status, signaling the toll is a permanent policy shift rather than a temporary negotiating tactic.
thesis🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Investors should price a persistent geopolitical premium into Gulf shipping and insurance even if oil spot prices fall.
“THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ WILL NEVER GO BACK TO THE STATE IT WAS AT BEFORE THE WAR.” ▶ 10:10
Despite the toll threat, oil is heading for its biggest quarterly decline since the pandemic with Brent around $72, because shipments are accelerating through Hormuz faster than markets expected.
data_point🗣 Lizzy Burden / Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highBrentStrait of Hormuzoil
So what: The market is trading actual flow data over headline risk; verified tanker movement matters more than official rhetoric.
“OIL ON TRACK FOR ITS BIGGEST FALL SINCE THE PANDEMIC.” ▶ 11:45
Morgan Stanley cut its Q3 and Q4 Brent average price forecast to $75 a barrel, $15 below its prior Q3 estimate and $5 below its prior Q4 estimate, and expects prices to fall toward pre-war levels by 2027, warning of a potential glut.
prediction🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highMorgan StanleyBrentoil
So what: A sell-side house is fading the geopolitical premium twice in two weeks, signaling conviction that supply outweighs Hormuz risk.
“MORGAN STANLEY FOR THE SECOND TIME IN ABOUT TWO WEEKS BRINGING DOWN ITS AVERAGE PRICE ESTIMATE.” ▶ 11:52
Gulf producers including the UAE and Saudi Arabia are actively rerouting export strategy to bypass Hormuz risk, with the UAE leaning on the Fujairah route and Saudi Arabia active in the Red Sea.
causal🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: mediumUnited Arab EmiratesSaudi ArabiaFujairahRed SeaGCC
So what: Existing bypass infrastructure caps the true upside of a Hormuz toll and explains why oil can fall despite the threat.
“THE U.A.E. FOR EXAMPLE DOUBLE DO YOU THINK ON THE FUJARE REPORT. SAUDI ARABIA HAS BEEN ACTIVE IN THE RED SEA.” ▶ 10:45
The US, EU and GCC states have all condemned a Hormuz toll as an extremely dangerous precedent, with Secretary of State Rubio calling it completely unacceptable and EU officials warning it would create an energy crisis.
data_point🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highRubioUnited StatesEuropean UnionGCCIran
So what: Strong Western opposition raises the odds of escalation or countermeasures if Iran follows through after the 60-day window.
“THE U.S. THROUGH SECRETARY OF STATE RUBIO SAID IT IS COMPLETELY UNACCEPTABLE FOR IRAN TO EVEN THINK ABOUT TOLLING THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.” ▶ 10:58
US-Iran talks scheduled in Doha are in doubt because Iran's deputy foreign minister says Tehran is not attending directly and will only communicate through mediators in Qatar, with Hormuz, oil sanctions, frozen assets and the nuclear component all still contentious in the MOU.
data_point🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: mediumIranUnited StatesQatarDohaTrump
So what: Indirect, mediator-only talks with multiple open disputes suggest a fragile deal at risk of unraveling.
“WE'RE DEFINITELY SEEING SOME SKIRMISHES ALONG THE M.O.U. AND WHAT IT MEANS FOR THE WIDER NEGOTIATIONS.” ▶ 11:24
King Dollar and the 40-Year Yen Low 5
The yen has fallen to its weakest level against the dollar since 1986, breaching 162 per dollar, with traders now watching 164-165 as the next thresholds even though the BOJ is raising rates and Japanese growth is healthy.
data_point🗣 Ruth Carson / Winnie Hsuconviction: highyendollarBank of Japan
So what: A weak yen alongside rate hikes and healthy growth signals the move is driven by rate differentials, not domestic weakness.
“162 PER DOLLAR AT A TIME WHEN B.O.J. IS RAISING RATES, AT A TIME WHEN GROWTH IS HEALTHY IN JAPAN. SOMETHING BROKEN HERE.” ▶ 6:39
Japanese intervention is unlikely near-term because authorities key on the pace of yen declines rather than the level, and the yen is grinding rather than plunging lower, prompting officials to conserve firepower.
framework🗣 Ruth Carsonconviction: mediumyenBank of JapanJapan
So what: Traders can stay short yen with lower intervention risk as long as the decline remains orderly and gradual.
“ONE OF THE KEY THINGS THAT AUTHORITIES REALLY LATCH ONTO... IS IN FACT THE PACE OF DECLINES.” ▶ 6:09
The dollar has risen against every major currency over the past two weeks, dominating 80% of all trades in a $9.5 trillion-a-day FX market, benefiting from both a haven bid during the Iran war and a petrodollar bid, with no viable alternative.
thesis🗣 Ruth Carsonconviction: highdollaryenpoundAustralian dollarIran
So what: This is broad dollar dominance, not an idiosyncratic yen story, so the yen slide won't reverse until US-Japan rate gaps narrow.
≈ “IT'S NOT JUST A YEN PROBLEM. NOT JUST A POUND PROBLEM OR AUSSIE DOLLAR PROBLEM. IT'S A DOLLAR DOMINANCE PROBLEM.” paraphrase
Macro investors are treating incoming Fed figure Kevin Warsh as more hawkish than expected, reinforcing the dollar's strength and rate-differential trade.
thesis🗣 Ruth Carsonconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reservedollar
So what: A hawkish Warsh perception keeps US yields elevated and sustains the pressure on the yen and other majors.
“KEVIN WARSH IS SOMEONE A LOT OF MACRO INVESTORS ARE LATCHING ONTO. MARKETS ARE JUDGING HIM TO BE MORE HAWKISH THAN EXPECTED.” ▶ 7:44
A Japanese government bond auction saw demand stronger than the 12-month average as investors turn cautious and expect the Bank of Japan to move more slowly and steadily after its recent rate hike, with long-end JGB yields rising to 3.93%, up 10 basis points.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsu / Lizzy Burdenconviction: mediumBank of JapanJGBJapanese government bonds
So what: Strong auction demand plus a rising long end signals investors expect a slower BOJ path but still demand term premium.
“JGB YIELDS AT THE LONG AND... RISING TO 3.93%, UP 10 BASIS POINTS.” ▶ 45:51
AI-Fueled Quarter and Market Bifurcation 4
Global equities are on track for their best quarter in almost six years and the S&P saw one of the fastest rebounds this century off its intra-quarter low, driven overwhelmingly by the AI trade.
data_point🗣 Lizzy Burdenconviction: highS&P 500MSCI Pacific IndexAI

“GLOBAL EQUITIES ON TRACK FOR THE BEST QUART FOR THE ALMOST SIX YEARS THANKS TO THAT A.I. TRADE.” ▶ 0:47
Asian markets are sharply bifurcated by AI exposure: Taiwan stocks head for their best quarter since 2001 and the Kospi its best since 1998, while the AI-light Hang Seng is down for a third straight quarter.

The Nikkei 225 is outperforming the broader Topix and heading for its best quarter on record, driven by both AI gains and the weak yen.
data_point🗣 Winnie Hsuconviction: highNikkei 225Topixyen

“THE NIKKEI 225 OUTPERFORMING THE BROADER TOPIX INDEX... HEADED FOR ITS BEST QUARTER ON RECORD.” ▶ 3:57
Central bankers at the ECB Sintra forum view AI as medium-term inflationary due to capex spending, with longer-term inflation effects unclear, and one central banker (Bank of Canada) has flagged possible AI valuation concerns as a financial-stability risk.
framework🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumECBOpenAIBank of CanadaAI

“MEDIUM TERM IS PROBABLY INFLATIONARY BECAUSE OF CAPEX SPENDING.” ▶ 30:20
Fed Independence and Executive Power 2
The US Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that Fed Governor Lisa Cook can keep her job while fighting unproven mortgage fraud allegations, with Roberts framing it narrowly, leaving open the possibility Trump tries to remove her another way.
data_point🗣 Jillconviction: highLisa CookSupreme CourtJohn RobertsKavanaughTrumpFederal Reserve
So what: Fed independence survives but only narrowly; the threat to Cook and the Fed's autonomy is not fully resolved.
“THIS WAS GOOD FOR THE FED BUT NOT GREAT.” ▶ 15:12
In a parallel decision, the Supreme Court expanded the president's power to fire the heads of federal agencies including the FTC, SEC and NLRB, stemming from Trump's removal of the FTC chair.
causal🗣 Jillconviction: highSupreme CourtTrumpFTCSECNLRB
So what: Broader executive removal power increases policy and regulatory uncertainty across markets even as the Fed is shielded.
“GIVING DONALD TRUMP... THE BROAD POWER TO REMOVE THE HEADS OF AGENCIES LIKE THE FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION, LIKE THE SECURITY EXCHANGE COMMISSION, THE NATIONAL LABOR RELATIONS BOARD.” ▶ 16:35
ECB Resilience and Trade Frictions 5
ECB President Christine Lagarde says the euro area weathered the largest US tariff increase in almost a century and the largest oil supply shock in history without the economy being derailed, though at substantial cost.
thesis🗣 Christine Lagardeconviction: highECBeuro areaUnited States
So what: A confident ECB narrative supports the case for further hikes rather than cuts if inflation risks persist.
“THE EURO AREA WEATHERED THE LARGEST U.S. TARIFF INCREASE IN ALMOST A CENTURY... BUT THE ECONOMY HAS NOT BEEN DERAILED.” ▶ 26:59
The ECB's June rate hike was not an insurance policy but a deliberate move against the risk of inflation running out of control amid energy-price uncertainty, and the ECB refuses to offer forward guidance because Middle East developments make it too unpredictable.
framework🗣 Francine Lacquaconviction: mediumECBChristine Lagarde
So what: Scenario-based, guidance-free policy means markets must trade ECB reaction functions rather than pre-committed paths.
“THE HIKE IN JUNE... WAS NOT AN INSURANCE POLICY... IN CASE INFLATION FREW GRAND JURY OUT OF CONTROL.” ▶ 28:37
The EU deems its economic relationship with China unsustainable, citing a roughly 360 billion euro 2025 trade deficit and Chinese state subsidies, and will bring concrete protective measures to a leaders' meeting late this year with an October deadline for talks that faces a very tall order.

Stellantis plans to sell Chinese-made Jeeps manufactured in Wuhan in Europe by the end of the decade, freeing up capacity at a Stellantis plant in France.
data_point🗣 Lizzy Burdenconviction: mediumStellantisJeepWuhanChinaFrance
So what: A Western automaker sourcing from China even amid EU-China trade tension shows cost pressure overriding geopolitical caution.
“STELLANTIS ANNOUNCED PLANS TO SELL CHINESE-MADE JEEPS IN EUROPE BY THE END OF THE DECADE.” ▶ 34:34
Maersk upgraded its full-year outlook, seeing global container volumes growing about 4% (above its prior forecast range) with stronger-than-expected underlying earnings and free cash flow, as shipping lines benefit from a recent jump in spot container rates.

South Africa faces anti-migrant demonstrations amid unemployment north of 30% since Covid and sub-1% GDP growth for years, posing a risk to the country's investment story if protests turn violent in economic hubs like Johannesburg.

“THE WORLD BANK IS DROPPING A KEY CLIMATE FINANCE TARGET AFTER CRITICISM FROM THE U.S., ITS LARGEST SHAREHOLDER.” ▶ 22:19
The US skilled-migrant advantage is eroding as immigration policy flip-flops push foreign-born workers to consider opportunities elsewhere, threatening a competitive edge given two-thirds of Silicon Valley tech workers are foreign-born.

UK food inflation fell to a 15-month low in June with prices rising 2.4% year-on-year, as bumper crops and strong competition kept prices lower according to the British Retail Consortium.

Gold Falls for the Third Session, Oil Edges Higher | Horizons Middle East & Africa 7/01/202645 min29 insights2026-07-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's Horizons Middle East & Africa opens H2 2026 with markets digesting a hawkish incoming Fed governor (Kevin Warsh): gold falls a third session below its 200-day average, the yen drifts to a four-decade low near 163 as Japan weighs intervention, and oil trades near $70-73 with banks (Goldman, Morgan Stanley) flagging a return to oversupply as Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizes. Guests debate AI/semiconductor sustainability, copper tariff positioning, central-bank gold demand, Ethiopia's debt restructuring, and Dubai's ultra-tight office market.
Hawkish Fed pivot under Warsh reprices risk assets · 3Yen at four-decade low and the intervention watch · 3Oil: glut returns as Hormuz normalizes and OPEC unshackles · 5Gold, copper and critical minerals · 5AI/semiconductor rally: due for a breather · 6US-Iran diplomacy and Middle East geopolitics · 3Africa debt and Gulf real estate · 4
Hawkish Fed pivot under Warsh reprices risk assets 3
An incoming Fed governor (Kevin Warsh) is delivering hawkish statements to establish credibility and calm bond markets, driving expectations of possible rate hikes rather than cuts and lifting the dollar.
causal🗣 Rahulconviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin WarshUS dollarbond markets
So what: A hawkish Fed regime shift is the master driver behind the dollar, gold, bitcoin and yen moves discussed all show.
“THE INCOMING FED GOVERNOR WANTED TO ESTABLISH CREDIBILITY, WHICH IS WHY YOU HAD A LOT OF STATEMENTS INTERPRETED AS HAWKISH.” ▶ 7:44
Bitcoin has fallen below $60,000 to a 21-month low, dropping under its 200-week moving average, driven by fear of Fed rate hikes after recently trading above $100,000.
data_point🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highBitcoinFederal Reserve
So what: Crypto is acting as a high-beta casualty of the hawkish rate repricing, a tape-read for risk appetite.
“IT IS NOT A 21 MONTH LOW, BELOW 60,000 DOLLARS... IT HAS FALLEN BELOW THE 200 WEEK MOVING AVERAGE” ▶ 1:31
Warsh's speech in Europe and Thursday's June nonfarm payrolls report are the key near-term catalysts markets are watching for the interest-rate path.
prediction🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highKevin Warshnonfarm payrollsFederal Reserve
So what: Positioning ahead of payrolls and Warsh's rhetoric frames the intervention, gold and dollar bets across the session.
Yen at four-decade low and the intervention watch 3
The yen is drifting weaker at a snail's pace toward 163-164 per dollar precisely because the market fears further Japanese intervention, keeping moves gradual rather than sharp.
causal🗣 Paulconviction: mediumJapanese yenUS dollarBank of JapanJapanese government
So what: Intervention risk itself is dampening yen volatility; a fast break would signal Tokyo has lost control.
“THE MARKET IS NERVOUS ABOUT THE POSSIBILITY THAT THERE WILL BE FURTHER INTERVENTION... THE MOVE IS A SNAIL'S PACE FOR THE YEN.” ▶ 4:19
Japan may wait to intervene until after Thursday's US nonfarm payrolls and act on the Friday US holiday, when thin liquidity lets them get more impact for their money.
prediction🗣 Paulconviction: mediumJapanJapanese yennonfarm payrolls
So what: Timing edge: a Friday-holiday yen intervention is the tactical scenario FX traders should hedge for.
“PARTICULARLY ON THE FRIDAY WHEN IT IS A U.S. HOLIDAY AND THEY COULD GET MORE BANG FOR THEIR BACK.” ▶ 5:03
Japan has not signaled intervention as strongly as last time, so watch policymaker rhetoric for a clear indication they've had enough; longer-term it will take more than intervention to turn the yen around for good.
framework🗣 Paulconviction: mediumJapanese yenJapanese policymakers
So what: Rhetoric, not price alone, is the leading indicator of imminent FX action.
“LONGER-TERM, IT WILL TAKE MORE THAN THAT [TO] TURN IT AROUND FOR GOOD.” ▶ 5:26
Oil: glut returns as Hormuz normalizes and OPEC unshackles 5
Oil posted its biggest quarterly drop since the pandemic as traders bet on a returning glut, with Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizing after wartime disruption.
data_point🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highBrent crudeStrait of Hormuzoil
So what: The war premium is unwinding fast; the base case is reverting to a fundamentally oversupplied market.
“OIL POSTED ITS BIGGEST QUARTERLY DROP SINCE THE [PANDEMIC] AS TRADERS BE[T] GLUT [RE]TURN WITH HORMUZ TRAFFIC NORMALIZING.” ▶ 0:40
Morgan Stanley cut its 2026 average Brent estimate to about $75 a barrel, lowering Q3 by $15 and Q4 by $5, while Goldman Sachs warns of oversupply later this year.
data_point🗣 Abeer Abu Omarconviction: highMorgan StanleyGoldman SachsBrent crude
So what: Sell-side is actively marking down oil forecasts, reinforcing the bearish supply thesis.
“MORGAN STANLEY YESTERDAY BROUGHT DOWN THEIR AVERAGE PRICE ESTIMATE FOR BRENT... $15 LOWER FOR THE THIRD QUARTER. $5 LOWER FOR THE FOURTH QUARTER. ABOUT $75 A BARREL” ▶ 24:25
The 2026 oil glut is driven by both a supply wave (waiting/loaded tankers returning, OPEC lifting production now that it's unconstrained by quotas below capacity) and a demand hit (China drawing down stockpiles and cutting refining, Indian chemical producers halting purchases).
causal🗣 Anthony DePauloconviction: highOPECChinaIndiaoil tankers
So what: The bearish oil setup is structural on both sides of the balance, not just a Hormuz relief trade.

The UAE's biggest oil company (ADNOC) is switching pricing of its offshore crude from an Abu Dhabi exchange-derived Murban benchmark to the Dubai benchmark, because market upheaval strained the old linkage and made barrels hard to price.

Iran Ratchets Up Talk of Controlling Hormuz; Stocks Rally | Horizons Middle East & Africa 6/30/202645 min33 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
On the last trading day of a quarter that is global equities' best in ~6 years (Asia's best in 17), the show weighs Iran's push to assert toll-based control over the Strait of Hormuz against surprisingly resilient supply chains, a 40-year-low yen on intervention watch, and an AI-led rotation where incumbents like Microsoft (worst month since 2000) are being reframed as tech's value stocks.
Hormuz control, tolling and shipping risk · 6Iran-US diplomacy read · 2AI-led quarter-end rally and rotation · 9Supply-chain resilience vs complacency · 2Yen at 40-year low and intervention watch · 3Fed path and US jobs data · 2China two-speed economy and diversification · 3Regional markets and single-stock flags · 4Political and social risk backdrop · 2
Hormuz control, tolling and shipping risk 6
Iran is pushing for total dominance over the Strait of Hormuz with the ultimate aim of extracting revenue by tolling all shipping that transits, and says it will do so with or without Oman's help.
thesis🗣 Stewart Livingstone Wallaceconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzOmanshipping
So what: A toll regime would raise structural transit costs for global tanker traffic and set a contested legal precedent.
“the aim ultimately is to extract some huge source of revenue from all the shipping that goes through there” ▶ 8:29
Iran has threatened full, swift action against any vessel using the so-called southern route hugging Oman's coast, effectively forcing traffic to acknowledge its control of the waterway.
causal🗣 Stewart Livingstone Wallaceconviction: highIranOmanStrait of Hormuzshipping
So what: Route restrictions narrow safe transit options and raise the odds of a fresh maritime incident.
“they are will take full swift action if anyone uses the so-called southern route, the hugs the coast of Oman” ▶ 8:13
Under law-of-the-sea transit passage, international waterways like Hormuz are toll-free; Iran can only lawfully charge specific service fees such as navigation or pilotage, so any toll regime would break precedent and require explicit published terms.
framework🗣 Victoria Mitchellconviction: highStrait of HormuzIranOmanlaw of the sea
So what: A tolling claim is legally vulnerable, meaning enforcement rather than legitimacy would drive shipping behavior.
“under the, the law, the sea transit passage is not told... the precedent is for a toll free passage through international waterways like the Strait of Hormuz” ▶ 19:38
Shipping through Hormuz will remain fragmented and cautious because each operator makes individual corporate risk-tolerance decisions, and the loss of the traditional central channel forces use of northern and southern corridors with different capacity and different counterparties (Iran vs Oman).
framework🗣 Victoria Mitchellconviction: mediumStrait of HormuzIranOmanshipping
So what: No single 'reopening' signal exists; transit normalization will be uneven and slow.
“everyone's making their own individual corporate decisions about the risk tolerance and the way that they approach shipping through these waterways” ▶ 17:12
Operational disruption in Hormuz will persist for months because the assumption of mines in the central channel will take a long time to resolve, keeping the traditional route unusable.
prediction🗣 Victoria Mitchellconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzshippingmines
So what: Even a diplomatic thaw would not quickly restore full central-channel capacity.
“things like the assumption of mines in the central channel of the strait, for example, are going to take a lot longer to resolve” ▶ 19:01
Morgan Stanley warns of an oil surplus as Hormuz traffic rebounds, even as Iran insists on maintaining control of the strait.
data_point🗣 anchor (headline)conviction: mediumMorgan StanleyStrait of HormuzoilIran
So what: A projected surplus caps oil upside despite the geopolitical overhang, consistent with Brent trading ~$72 and about 1% lower.
“Morgan Stanley warns of an oil surplus as Hormuz traffic rebounds” ▶ 0:30

Washington and Tehran gave conflicting accounts of Doha talks: Iran confirmed sending a delegation but says it will not negotiate directly with the US, working instead through Qatari and other mediators.

“they are sending a delegation to Doha, but it looks like they're probably not going to talk to the American delegation directly” ▶ 7:19
The earlier direct Switzerland meeting was the anomaly, not the norm; mediated talks are standard, so a return to indirect negotiation should not be read as a breakdown.
contrarian🗣 Stewart Livingstone Wallaceconviction: highIranUnited StatesSwitzerlandDoha
So what: Investors overreacting to 'no direct talks' headlines may be mispricing the actual trajectory.
“Switzerland in some respects was an anomaly in this entire process and that they spoke directly to each other” ▶ 7:36

Anthony Pompliano 2 episode(s) · 58 insights

Bitcoin Money Is Rotating — Here's Where It's Going75 min33 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Former crypto hedge fund manager Avi Felman explains why he has divested nearly all Bitcoin: with genuine secular tech growth (AI, robotics, biotech, defense), Bitcoin's escape-hatch narrative has lost relevance, capital is rotating into equities, and BTC faces specific overhangs (Michael Sailor/MSTR, quantum, no narrative). He lays out where he is deploying instead and how retail-driven volatility is reshaping every market.
Bitcoin Divestment Thesis and Rotation Out of Crypto · 4The Sailor / MSTR Overhang and Conditions to Re-buy Bitcoin · 6Financialization, Prediction Markets and Hyperliquid · 4The AI-Driven Rotation: Memory, Downstream Trades and Sector Bets · 6How to Generate Edge and Read Panic · 4Retail, AI-Compressed Expertise and Bubble Signals · 6Private Markets, NAV Premiums and Access Vehicles · 3
Bitcoin Divestment Thesis and Rotation Out of Crypto 4
Bitcoin's core value proposition as an escape hatch from Fed/government mistakes only matters when the Fed has massive control over the economy, and that relevance is fading because genuine secular growth in AI, robotics, biotech and defense now takes a backseat to monetary irresponsibility.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinFederal ReserveAIroboticsbiotechdefenseSpaceX
So what: The macro narrative that drove BTC demand is structurally weakening as real-economy growth dominates attention.
“Bitcoin is valuable because if the system makes mistakes, there's an escape hatch. But that only makes sense in a world where the Fed has massive control.” ▶ 0:03
Avi Felman, in crypto since 2016/2017 and formerly ~80% allocated, now owns no Bitcoin and almost no crypto, having fully divested because attention has shifted elsewhere and crypto lacks a current narrative to drive purchases.
anecdote🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinAvi Felman
So what: A former deep crypto insider capitulating on holdings signals a broader sentiment and flow shift out of the asset class.
≈ “It used to be 80% of my portfolio... today it just doesn't make a ton of sense to be holding this drag when I can be holding memory stocks.” paraphrase
Crypto will follow the internet-bubble arc: the label disappears and surviving projects become revenue-generating 'fintech companies that use crypto as a back end' rather than 'crypto companies'.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highcryptoHyperliquidfintechtokenizationNetflixFacebookGoogle
So what: Invest in crypto projects by revenue and users, not by infrastructure/token narratives.
“We're no longer going to have crypto companies in the same sense. We're going to have fintech companies that use crypto as a back end.” ▶ 6:35
The only crypto Felman still holds long-term is privacy coins Zcash and a little Monero, as a hedge against future wealth taxes or capital seizure driven by rising socialism.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumZcashMonerocryptowealth tax
So what: Privacy coins are framed as the specific crypto hedge for a political-seizure scenario, not BTC.
≈ “I own Zcash and a little bit of Monero as well because I think that it's important to have these things as a hedge.” paraphrase
The Sailor / MSTR Overhang and Conditions to Re-buy Bitcoin 6
Institutional and traditional-finance investors are avoiding Bitcoin primarily over three fears: Michael Sailor/Strategy, quantum computing, and the absence of any narrative to drive buying.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinMichael SailorStrategyMSTRquantum computing
So what: Named catalysts must resolve before institutional flows return to BTC.
≈ “They're worried about Sailor. They're worried about two things. They're worried about quantum and they're worried about Sailor.” paraphrase
Felman will re-buy Bitcoin only when three conditions are met — the Sailor problem is solved, the quantum problem is solved, and Fed liquidity injection revives inflation fears — or on a capitulation puke to around $40K.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinFederal ReserveMichael Sailorquantum computing
So what: Defines a concrete BTC re-entry level (~$40K) and the macro/idiosyncratic triggers to watch.
≈ “If we get like a puke out, if we trade 40K, I'll probably get back in. But until then, I'm waiting for these three things.” paraphrase
Strategy's 'Stretch' Bitcoin savings vehicle is trading around 76 (below par), and Sailor has the option to simply turn off the non-smart-contract dividend, nuking Stretch holders to zero while keeping all the Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highStrategyStretchMichael SailorMSTRBitcoin
So what: Stretch holders bear pausable dividend risk; Sailor's incentive is to protect BTC and MSTR, not preferred holders.
“This morning when I checked Stretch it's at 76... The dividend is not in a smart contract. He can pause it at any time.” ▶ 20:10
Felman thinks it is probable, not just possible, that Sailor lets Stretch go to zero by shutting off the dividend, because that is easier than fighting to the 'dirty end' and risking MSTR going to zero via dilutive at-the-money offerings.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumMichael SailorStretchMSTRStrategy
So what: Preferred/Stretch exposure carries a materially probable wipeout scenario per a former distressed-credit operator.
“Not only that, it's probable.” ▶ 24:16
The Stretch/MSTR situation is not a Luna analogue because Luna had a reflexive UST-to-Luna arbitrage that printed infinite supply to zero, a mechanism that does not exist here.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highLunaUSTStretchMSTRGolden Tree
So what: Rejects the popular Luna comparison; the failure mode is a policy choice, not an automated death spiral.
“It can't be a Luna... there was a continuous arb to just print infinite Luna and send it to zero. You can't do that with [Stretch].” ▶ 22:13
If Strategy is forced to sell, Sailor likely only needs to sell roughly 15–25% of total Bitcoin to cover all debt (converts and everything), and any such forced selling is a signal to buy BTC as aggressively as possible.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumStrategyMSTRBitcoinMichael Sailor
So what: Quantifies downside supply overhang and frames forced liquidation as a buying opportunity.
“Whenever there's a forced seller in the market, you need to step in there and buy as much as you possibly can.” ▶ 26:45
Financialization, Prediction Markets and Hyperliquid 4
The Hyperliquid thesis is essentially the Robinhood/Kalshi/Polymarket thesis on the financialization of the world — people now trade everything — and Felman classifies Hyperliquid as a revenue-generating fintech company with multiple potential winners in a large market.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highHyperliquidRobinhoodKalshiPolymarketfintech
So what: Own the financialization rails by revenue; the TAM supports several winners, not a single one.
≈ “The hyperliquid thesis is actually basically the same thesis as the Robin Hood thesis... the market is big enough that there are going to be multiple winners.” paraphrase
A broken social contract — pensions gone, 401k returns eroded by inflation and runaway housing/asset prices captured by the capital class — is driving average Americans to gamble and trade (sports betting, weather, Kalshi, Polymarket) to escape a 'permanent underclass'.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highprediction marketsKalshiPolymarket401ksports betting
So what: Structural demographic/economic despair is a durable tailwind for trading and prediction-market volumes.
“The social contract in many ways has been broken so people are searching for ways to escape... they're gambling on sports, they're betting on the weather.” ▶ 8:32
Traditional exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ) are moving toward prediction markets and integrating gambling because that is where the world is going, and the GameStop/COVID day-trading era created a lasting addiction to being on the winning side.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highNYSENASDAQKalshiGameStopWallStreetBets
So what: Incumbent exchanges converging with prediction/gambling venues validates the sector's expansion.
≈ “You're actually seeing the New York Stock Exchange and NASDAQ... they're integrating the gambling. They're integrating all that because they realize that's where the world is going.” paraphrase ▶ 9:20
Felman is bullish on other revenue-generating crypto projects including Hyperliquid, Courtyard/Cards (trading-card collectibles), and Eric Voorhees's VVV, on the criterion that they generate real revenue regardless of debated token value accrual.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumHyperliquidVVVEric VoorheesCards
So what: Revenue and users are the new screening filter for crypto token selection.
“If you look at VVV, which is Eric Vorhees's project... the thing is making money. It's actually pulling in revenue.” ▶ 5:10
The AI-Driven Rotation: Memory, Downstream Trades and Sector Bets 6
The Mag 7 crowded trade has been overtaken by memory stocks because hyperscalers spending ~$600 billion on data centers must buy memory, while their equity issuance depresses their own share prices — a shift from getting ahead to a game of competitive survival.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highMagnificent 7memory stocksMicronSanDiskdata centers
So what: Capex flows explain the memory-stock leadership rotation away from mega-cap tech.
“If they're going to be building out $600 billion of data centers... that money is going to memory.” ▶ 33:54
With memory now in a 'crazy bubble,' the next trade is what AI will actually change downstream: short the consultants (Accenture down 30% in a day), buy biotech (XBI, Cathie Wood's ARKG), and buy defense benefiting from smart forward-deployed drones.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highAccenturebiotechXBIARKGCathie WooddefenseAI
So what: Actionable rotation: pair short consulting against long biotech/defense as AI's second-order beneficiaries.
“Maybe short the consultant, buy biotech.” ▶ 35:10
Felman runs a barbell of 'what AI will affect' and 'American isolationism / competition with China,' making him a big Intel bull versus TSMC — expecting Intel (~$600B cap) to close the gap with TSMC (~$3T) because domestic chip capacity is a national-security imperative akin to the Manhattan Project.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highIntelTSMCChinasemiconductorsINTC
So what: US chip reshoring is a structural, policy-backed long for Intel over Taiwan-exposed TSMC.
“That's why I'm still a huge believer in Intel. Intel is at 600 billion market cap. TSMC, which is based in Taiwan, is at a 3 trillion. They're going to close that gap.” ▶ 38:42
Felman is a big uranium bull, holding uranium miners rather than the commodity, because even if uranium prices fall on oversupply, miners selling far higher volumes still see multiples of revenue growth amid a Trump-administration nuclear push.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highuraniumuranium minersnuclearTrump administrationBloom EnergyAshton Brenner
So what: Express nuclear/energy conviction through miners for volume-driven revenue, not the underlying commodity price.
≈ “If they're selling 10x and price is down 50%, they're still 5x up on their revenue. And so I hold uranium miners as well.” paraphrase
SpaceX at ~$1.8T trades near 100x price-to-sales on ~$18B revenue, but Felman is a buyer under $1 trillion, arguing it is the only viable space company and could grow into its valuation like Nvidia, which traded at 45x P/S in 2023 with only a 10% max drawdown before falling to 22x.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumSpaceXNvidiaTeslaElon Musk
So what: Sets a specific SpaceX entry (<$1T) and uses Nvidia's multiple compression as the bull template.
“I'm a buyer of SpaceX under a trillion for sure, 100%.” ▶ 59:40
Stablecoins will 'eat the world' but that does not mean Coinbase and Circle win — Circle could be in trouble as banks fight to buy time to issue their own stablecoins, while Tether is winning across the board yet is not investable.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumstablecoinsCoinbaseCircleTetherbanks
So what: Bullish thesis on stablecoins does not translate to the tradeable equities; Circle faces competitive risk.
≈ “Stable coins are going to eat the world, but that does not mean Coinbase and Circle are going to do well... I actually think that Circle could be in some trouble.” paraphrase ▶ 1:08:00
How to Generate Edge and Read Panic 4
Edge comes not from macro prognostication ('astrology for men') but from identifying what people don't understand or aren't looking at — e.g., buying memory before the market grasped post-ChatGPT memory demand.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highmemory stocksJim Simonsmacro
So what: Focus research on informational gaps, not rate/VIX/skew tea-leaves, to generate returns.
“A lot of macro talk is kind of just astrology for men.” ▶ 33:16
Buffett's 'blood on the streets' means buying only when participants are truly panicking and selling good companies at irrational prices — not merely when the market is down 10-15% for rational reasons like repricing tariffs.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highWarren BuffetttariffsChina
So what: Distinguish rational selloffs from genuine panic to time contrarian buying correctly.
“He means buy when people are truly panicking... they're selling good stocks, good companies at irrational prices because they are fully panicked.” ▶ 28:59
Felman's best-ever trade was buying Ripple equity off the FTX estate; when FTX collapsed, SBF personally called him at Golden Tree seeking $2-3B to cover what was actually an ~$8B hole, illustrating that peak-fear counterparties act irrationally.
anecdote🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highRippleFTXSam Bankman-FriedGolden Tree
So what: Distressed opportunities cluster around panicked, irrational sellers at crisis moments.
“The best trade that I've ever taken in my entire life was buying Ripple equity off of the FTX estate.” ▶ 26:48
In high-volatility regimes cash becomes more valuable; Felman allocates to mega-trends by buying on extreme downside moves (~30% collapses every few quarters) and selling or writing calls into insane upside spikes, because high implied vol raises the odds of revisiting entry prices.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highvolatilityoptionsS&PNASDAQ
So what: A volatility-harvesting playbook: buy dips, trim/sell calls on spikes, hold dry powder.
“When V is this high you get presented with opportunities basically every 3 months where things collapse 30%.” ▶ 56:05
Retail, AI-Compressed Expertise and Bubble Signals 6
AI compresses the time to become 'expert enough' to trade a theme, letting retail instantly surface names (e.g., asking Claude for AI-trade beneficiaries), which Felman argues is why SK Hynix rose as far as it did versus a hedge-fund-only move in the past.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumAIClaudeSK Hynixretail investors
So what: AI-democratized research amplifies retail crowding into narratives, intensifying single-stock moves.
“Now you go into Claude and you type in what are the biggest beneficiaries from the AI trade and all these companies populate.” ▶ 51:56
Because capital and information now move at the speed of light, everything is far more volatile, producing a K-shaped bifurcation in market outcomes; Pomp's Sylvia AI CFO users grew net worth 16-40% in six months across all income and starting-net-worth levels.
data_point🗣 Anthony Pomplianoconviction: mediumSylviaAIMicronvolatility
So what: Heightened volatility rewards good positioning and punishes bad, widening the winner/loser gap even in personal finance.
“If you use Sylvia regularly or heavily your net worth has grown 16 to 40% in the last 6 months... across all income levels.” ▶ 47:54
In today's public markets the herd is more right than not because X functions as a global chat room of many smart, well-informed participants — so edge is less about pure contrarianism and more about being online enough to catch narratives early and 'get in the way' of them.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Pomplianoconviction: highXpublic marketsretail investors
So what: Reflexively fading crowded public-market narratives is often wrong; front-running them pays better.
“The herd is actually more right than not in the public market.” ▶ 1:09:29
Bubble tops are identified by building a mosaic of overexuberance signals — taxi drivers discussing Intel, doctor friends starting to trade, Korean leverage ratios (up 3x YTD) approaching ~$50B in SK Hynix — rather than one silver bullet.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highSK HynixKoreaIntelleverage
So what: Concrete crowd-sentiment and leverage thresholds to watch for signaling the AI-trade top.
“If a taxi driver starts talking to me about Intel, I'm probably selling out.” ▶ 1:03:19
Felman recounts the crypto-cycle top signal: a multibillionaire non-crypto investor showing off a ~$1.1B crypto position built off a dinner tip, then day-trading Dogecoin in an Uber — a mosaic of euphoria that meant 'there can't be much juice left'.
anecdote🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highDogecoincryptoRobinhood
So what: Extreme late-stage retail/whale euphoria across unexpected participants marks cycle tops.
“He flips from the Uber app to Robin Hood... I go, 'What are you looking at?' He goes, 'Dogecoin.' And I should have immediately got on my computer and sold everything.” ▶ 1:06:14
Meme-stock moves (Wendy's, GameStop) are driven by shared identity and the fun of making money together rather than fundamentals — Wendy's spiked ~30% off a Reddit joke about saving jobs — and cults now surround tickers like INTC, SanDisk and Hyperliquid.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumWendy'sGameStopINTCSanDiskHyperliquidNelson Peltz
So what: Understanding the social-identity meme mechanism is now essential to trading crypto-fied equity markets.
“Whenever you get a meme stock that runs, it's because... it's so much more fun to make money together.” ▶ 1:12:09
Private Markets, NAV Premiums and Access Vehicles 3
Publicly traded closed-end funds holding private companies often carry justified NAV premiums because marks are stuck at the last funding round; the danger signal is not absolute premium level but rapid NAV expansion (e.g., 200% to 500% in two days) without a corresponding company event.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highNAVclosed-end fundsRoboStrategyAndrew Kangprivate markets
So what: Evaluate premium-to-NAV vehicles by the speed and justification of NAV changes, not the headline premium.
“The actual absolute number [is] almost irrelevant... what makes me nervous is well, the NAV just went from 200% to 500% in 2 days.” ▶ 43:20
Index-fund forward returns may be structurally lower because companies now stay private until $1T+ valuations, letting a small cabal of private investors capture gains that used to accrue to public retail — making thematic access vehicles (RoboStrategy, Fundrise, AngelList, Robinhood's VC vehicle) valuable.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highindex fundsRoboStrategyFundriseAngelListRobinhoodprivate marketsventure capital
So what: Prolonged private-market capture is a headwind for passive index returns and a tailwind for retail-access vehicles.
“Companies can wait until they're 1.5 trillion to go public. And they're actually taking those returns from your average American.” ▶ 44:31
The competitive edge in private markets can favor going with top VCs (Sequoia, a16z, Benchmark) over lone-wolf contrarianism because their capital, network and recruiting materially raise a startup's probability of success — 'do you want to be the hero or do you want to make money'.
framework🗣 Anthony Pomplianoconviction: mediumSequoiaAndreessen HorowitzBenchmarkSpaceXventure capital
So what: Co-investing alongside dominant VCs can beat pure contrarian sourcing on risk-adjusted odds.
“How many venture funds would have been better off not investing in 20 different companies and just put all their money in SpaceX.” ▶ 1:01:00
Dave Portnoy GOES OFF On Bitcoin, AI, Socialism & Being Fired From Barstool57 min25 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dave Portnoy details how ESPN forced Penn to fire him from Barstool as a condition of a 10-year, $2B deal, letting him buy the company back for $1 when the ESPN Bet deal later collapsed. He is now bearish/exhausted on Bitcoin and crypto after repeatedly buying tops, sees AI as capable of replacing ~90% of media talent, and floats an independent NYC mayoral run driven by disgust with socialist politicians.
Barstool/Penn/ESPN Deal Mechanics · 5Bitcoin & Crypto Reversal · 5AI Disrupting Media Talent · 4Creator-Media Business Model · 4Retail Finance & Wall Street Distrust · 3Media Cancellation & Reputational Risk · 2Political-Economy Takes · 2
Barstool/Penn/ESPN Deal Mechanics 5
ESPN made Dave Portnoy's removal a non-negotiable condition of its ~10-year, $2B sportsbook deal with Penn, showing acquirers/partners will structure media deals to strip out a founder-personality when regulatory or brand risk is attached.
anecdote🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highESPNPenn EntertainmentBarstool SportsJay SnowdenESPN Bet
So what: Founder brand equity can become a liability that counterparties price into M&A structure, not just an asset.
“ESPN said Dave Portoy can absolutely not be part of Barcel sports or this deal.” ▶ 3:10
Portnoy bought Barstool back from Penn for $1 by leveraging the threat that a publicly-fired founder attacking the ESPN Bet launch would damage the new venture, illustrating that reputational leverage can substitute for legal/contractual rights.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBarstool SportsPenn EntertainmentESPN BetJay Snowden
So what: A creator's distribution/audience can be weaponized as deal leverage even with zero legal standing.
“sell me back Barcel for a dollar. That's where that mythical dollar everyone” ▶ 7:08
The ESPN Bet venture with Penn failed commercially, with Barstool reportedly posting better sportsbook numbers than ESPN Bet even without active promotion, signaling the personality-driven brand outperformed the institutional media brand in gambling customer acquisition.
data_point🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumESPN BetPenn EntertainmentBarstool SportsESPN
So what: Institutional sports-media brands may underperform edgy creator brands at converting gambling customers.
“the ESPN pen deal was a total disaster. Like we weren't even promoting. We were getting better numbers than ESPN.” ▶ 8:49
Portnoy argues keeping the acquired brand name 'Barstool Sportsbook' was a mistake versus launching a fresh neutral name, because his personal notoriety created regulatory friction with state gambling councils that a de-identified brand would have avoided.
causal🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumBarstool SportsbookPenn EntertainmentMassachusetts
So what: In regulated verticals, a controversial founder's name can raise licensing costs enough to outweigh brand equity.
“hindsight probably would have been better off going a different name and pushing people to it” ▶ 12:13
Operating in regulated gambling forced Portnoy into a contractual gag on attacking government officials and politics, showing regulated licensing regimes materially constrain a media brand's core content and voice.
causal🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highPenn EntertainmentBarstool Sports
So what: Regulatory licensing can neuter the very edginess that built a media brand's audience.
“I won't attack government officials I won't talk on politics as much as I can um because we have to deal with these people” ▶ 12:33
Bitcoin & Crypto Reversal 5
Portnoy has flipped back to hating Bitcoin, framing himself as a reliable contrarian top-signal who repeatedly buys near highs and sells near lows, so his public enthusiasm should be read as a warning indicator.
contrarian🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: A widely-followed retail personality self-identifying as a top-buyer is itself a sentiment signal.
“I I mean I'm always always on the wrong side of Bitcoin and crypto. Always.” ▶ 46:16
Portnoy first bought $2M of Bitcoin at ~$11,000 (Aug/Sep 2020) then sold immediately, watching it 6x to ~$60,000 within about six months, a concrete example of a large holder capitulating right before a major run.
data_point🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBitcoinWinklevoss twins
So what: Illustrates the cost of selling conviction-less positions ahead of parabolic moves.
“I had bought 2 million of it at 11,000 they walked out of the room sold them” ▶ 44:56
Portnoy says he held as much as ~$15M in Bitcoin at peak but has drawn it down substantially, and now cites Michael Saylor selling Bitcoin (while claiming selling is good) as a reason nobody understands the market.
data_point🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Even a former 'diamond hands' whale has been shaken out, and points to Saylor's actions as confusing signal.
“you got Sailor selling it saying it's good that he's selling it. Who knows?” ▶ 45:45
Portnoy's meme-coin dabbling (notably Safe Moon, picked 'out of a hat') resulted in total loss plus being named in an influencer non-disclosure lawsuit before dismissal, underscoring legal liability risk for influencers promoting tokens.
anecdote🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highSafe Moonmeme coins
So what: Influencer token promotion carries litigation exposure even for casual/joke endorsements.
“I got mentioned in a lawsuit. They're like, "You were an insidider."” ▶ 46:47
Portnoy's current stock approach is deliberately conservative and broker-managed with 'nothing crazy,' a notable shift from his COVID-era aggressive day trading, suggesting personal de-risking after speculative losses.
thesis🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumstock market
So what: The face of retail day-trading has personally retreated to a safe, advised portfolio.
“I'm fairly safe right now. Like I I have a guy who helps me like a broker”
AI Disrupting Media Talent 4
Portnoy says he could replace ~90% of Barstool's staff with AI today and only refrains by choice, signaling that AI already threatens the cost structure of talent-driven media companies.
prediction🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBarstool SportsAI
So what: AI is a live existential cost/margin lever for creator-media businesses, gated only by owner discretion.
“I could I could replace 90% of my staff with AI.” ▶ 39:52
A $10,000 AI voice clone read Portnoy's audiobook indistinguishably from him, but the publisher legally barred using it and required a human recording, showing AI capability now outruns contractual/industry acceptance.
data_point🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highAIaudiobook publishing
So what: AI voice quality is production-ready; the binding constraint is rights/policy, not technology.
“I spent 10 grand to have this very advanced AI read it. You could not tell the difference.” ▶ 40:03
Portnoy notes employees using AI produced output 'too intelligent' and off-brand, implying authenticity/voice is the moat AI struggles to fake in personality-driven content.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumAIBarstool Sports
So what: Persona authenticity, not raw capability, is the defensible edge against AI in creator media.
“it almost makes them too intelligent where we're like, "That's not you because it's too good."” ▶ 40:36
Portnoy warns that if Barstool were owned by ESPN/Penn rather than himself, AI-driven talent replacement would be a serious threat, framing founder-ownership as the key protection for talent under corporate parents.
causal🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumAIESPNPenn EntertainmentBarstool Sports
So what: Corporate-owned creator rosters face higher AI-substitution risk than founder-owned ones.
“if we sold or I was out and it was ESPN and pen, 100% that would be a concern.” ▶ 40:46
Creator-Media Business Model 4
Barstool's talent model signs undiscovered creators on ~2-3 year deals with revenue-share terms scaled to their size at entry, treating creators like athletes to 'supercharge' careers in exchange for equity/economics.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBarstool SportsCall Her DaddyPardon My TakeSpittin Chiclets
So what: Defines the acquisition economics of a leading creator network for anyone competing in or investing in creator media.
“the talent is generally Well, you do two or three years. Very similar to an athlete in a way.” ▶ 25:16
Portnoy concedes finding undiscovered talent is now much harder because streaming, Twitch, Instagram, TikTok etc. let creators build large brands without a network, weakening the value proposition of signing to a media company.
causal🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBarstool SportsTwitchInstagramTikTokSnapchat
So what: Platform disintermediation is eroding the leverage of creator-network aggregators over talent.
“in this day and age of, you know, streaming and Twitch and Instagram and Snapchat and and Tik Tok, you can build a pretty big brand without being attached to a network.” ▶ 26:09
Portnoy says all Barstool content emerged from unplanned experimentation ('see what resonates'), citing Davey Day Trader's origin as an improvised pivot when COVID killed sports betting right after the Penn deal.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBarstool SportsDavey Day Trader
So what: Rapid disposable experimentation, not planning, is the stated content-innovation engine.
“All of the content... You try different things. You see what resonates” ▶ 14:46
Portnoy declined a 50/50 Barstool Finance partnership with Pompliano because the structure asked Pompliano to contribute his assets for free rather than have Barstool buy them, revealing Barstool typically acquires creators via contributed equity, not cash purchase.
anecdote🗣 Anthony Pomplianoconviction: mediumBarstool SportsAnthony Pompliano
So what: Barstool's default deal is asset-contribution partnership, which sophisticated operators may reject.
“the deal that you had offered at the time was let's do bar stool finance and we'd be 50/50 partners on it” ▶ 23:48
Retail Finance & Wall Street Distrust 3
Portnoy frames Wall Street as an insiders' game and the biggest casino run by gatekeepers, positioning his blunt 'stocks always go up' messaging as simply saying out loud what institutions obscure.
contrarian🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highWall StreetCNBC
So what: Captures the durable retail-populist distrust of institutional finance that drives meme-stock behavior.
“I still uh like think Wall Street is just an insiders game and and the biggest casino and the people try to like you know the gatekeep basically.” ▶ 16:48
During GameStop, when buying was disabled and only selling allowed, Portnoy lost ~$70K and publicly accused Citadel and Steve Cohen of colluding to bail out shorting hedge funds and getting order flow from Robinhood.
anecdote🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumGameStopCitadelSteve CohenRobinhoodVlad Tenev
So what: Reflects the payment-for-order-flow and PFOF collusion narrative that fueled retail rage in the GameStop episode.
“it appeared to me that Citadel Steve Cohen were trying to bail out all these the the hedge funds” ▶ 20:08
Portnoy positions himself as a real-time mirror of retail sentiment during COVID, correctly betting Spirit Airlines (rallied ~$6 to ~$21) against Warren Buffett's exit from airlines, arguing internet-native operators read markets better than aging institutional voices.
contrarian🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumSpirit AirlinesWarren BuffettShake Shack
So what: Retail crowd-following occasionally beat consensus institutional calls in the COVID reopening trade.
“Warren Buffett's like, I'm selling all my stock in the airlines. So I posed it, me verse Buffett. And Spirit Airlines actually ripped. It went from like 6 to 21” ▶ 17:54
Media Cancellation & Reputational Risk 2
Portnoy's core defensive tactic against legacy journalists is demanding to record interviews unedited, exploiting that outlets (Business Insider, NYT, WSJ) refused to sit down on-camera with their own subject, which he treats as evidence of hit-piece intent.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBusiness InsiderNew York TimesWall Street Journal
So what: Recording-as-defense is a replicable playbook for public figures facing adversarial legacy media.
“why don't we just sit down, have a conversation, and we'll just roll the cameras, no editing, and we'll just see what happens.” ▶ 32:10
Portnoy failed to win a defamation suit over the Business Insider article because the 'actual malice' bar for public figures is nearly impossible to clear, illustrating how weak legal recourse is against reputational attacks.
causal🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: highBusiness Insider
So what: Public figures have almost no viable legal remedy against damaging coverage under the malice standard.
“I tried to sue didn't get anywhere with that... that will be misconst malice” ▶ 30:52
Political-Economy Takes 2
Portnoy floats a genuine (though unlikely-to-win) independent run for NYC mayor, driven by opposition to Mamdani and self-described socialist candidates he says openly want to overthrow Western society and 'seize the means.'
thesis🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumNew York CityZohran Mamdani
So what: Signals capital-class alarm at NYC's leftward political shift and potential celebrity opposition candidacy.
“It is real. Do I think it's going to happen? No... I feel like Mandami is the head of the snake.” ▶ 37:00
Portnoy argues Mamdani's internet/social-media savvy is his key advantage and that his opponents have 'no answers,' framing meme/internet warfare as the decisive battleground in the NYC mayoral race.
framework🗣 Dave Portnoyconviction: mediumZohran MamdaniNew York City
