So what: Internet-native communication, not policy, is positioned as the deciding factor in the race.
“he knows how to use the internet and social media... the people he's campaigned against have absolutely no answers” ▶ 36:23

Capital Allocators · Ted Seides 1 episode(s) · 47 insights

Trinity Wall Street's Nicholas Csicsko: How Transparency Survives a 50% Drawdown59 min47 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Nicholas Csicsko of Trinity Wall Street's endowment explains how prioritizing manager transparency and relationship depth lets an allocator survive severe drawdowns (including a manager that fell ~50% before compounding 5x), and details his frameworks for manager selection, portfolio tilt management, exit discipline, and his skepticism about private-market marks and illiquidity in today's environment.
Transparency as Drawdown Insurance · 5Surviving the 50% Drawdown Case Study · 6Manager Selection and Sourcing Discipline · 6Transparency's Edge Cases and IR as a Weapon · 8Portfolio Construction and Tilt Management · 6Exit Discipline and Governance · 6Private Markets, Liquidity and Marks Skepticism · 7Trinity's Origins and the Allocator's Mindset · 3
Transparency as Drawdown Insurance 5
Building deep transparency and trust with managers in good times creates durable relationships that survive severe drawdowns, effectively transforming capital from fungible money into a resilient partnership.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: Allocators should engineer transparency proactively so relationships hold when performance breaks down.
“in that way, all of a sudden, capital stops being funible because it's no longer just money. It's a partnership.” ▶ 10:29
Investors should behave in good times exactly as they would want to behave in their worst moment, using that anticipated stress as the baseline for how to communicate and treat partners.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Pre-committing to crisis-grade behavior avoids the defensive reflex that destroys relationships under stress.
“if you imagine yourself in your worst moment, that's how you behave in the good times.” ▶ 9:45
The instinctive defensive reaction of putting one's guard up under stress is usually the wrong move in investing; letting a partner see your struggles and decisions produces better outcomes.
contrarian🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Managers who open up during pain retain LPs; those who tense up lose them.
“the normal reaction for people when they're challenged is to put their guard up, is to become defensive. And a lot of times I've found that the opposite is actually most successful” ▶ 10:06
Being perceived as a top-quartile or top-decile LP partner earns tangible rewards over time, including greater access to the manager and unique co-investment or capacity opportunities.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Transparency is a two-way street; being an exceptional LP is itself an edge that compounds into deal access.
“if we can be perceived as a top quartortile or potentially even top desile partner, we will reap those rewards over time” ▶ 10:44
Extreme manager transparency can include a manager voluntarily emailing his entire portfolio in an Excel file every month, reflecting a total lack of concern about how the LP would use the holdings.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: The highest-trust managers volunteer full position-level disclosure, giving LPs real-time exposure insight.
“he he would just he still does just sends uh his entire portfolio in an Excel file to to me monthly” ▶ 11:53
Surviving the 50% Drawdown Case Study 6
A concentrated long-short manager Trinity backed in 2017 fell 14% in 2019 (then his worst year) and a further ~30% in 2020, approaching a ~50% peak-to-trough drawdown, before compounding roughly 5x from 2021 to 2026.
data_point🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: Enduring a near-50% drawdown with a trusted manager delivered a 5x recovery, quantifying the payoff of patience.
“you have a draw down that's starting to get close to 50% at the trough... from 21 to 26, it's been a 5x.” ▶ 13:13
Conviction to hold a manager through a deep drawdown rests on three factors: stability of the business, grit in the manager to keep going, and belief in the underlying holdings.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: These three tests give allocators a concrete checklist for distinguishing patience from denial during losses.
“there need to be a few factors to have patience and that's stability of business, grit in the manager to keep going and a belief in what they own.” ▶ 42:40
The decisive reason to hold the drawdown manager was not just belief in him but that his short-side skill and holdings served a distinct diversifying role no other portfolio position filled, which proved out as his short book thrived in 2022.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Portfolio role, not just manager affection, justifies fighting to retain an underperformer.
“It was they served a distinct role in the portfolio and that was worth fighting for.” ▶ 17:34
This manager deliberately capped his fund, closing to outside capital around $300 million because he never wanted to exceed roughly $500-600 million, and would proactively return capital when his desired holdings no longer fit his AUM.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Managers who cap AUM to protect strategy integrity signal alignment prized by long-term LPs.
“this manager is the type of manager that closed outside capital when he was about at 300 million in assets because he didn't ever really want to be over 5600 million” ▶ 12:05
In hindsight the allocator's error during the drawdown was under-adding, not over-holding; the team believed 2020 was the time to add but felt paralyzed, and later wished they had added more.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Even conviction-holders freeze at the trough; recognizing paralysis is itself an edge for adding into pain.
“In hindsight, I really wish we would have added more, but um in those moments, you feel challenged and you feel paralyzed sometimes.” ▶ 14:05
About half of the drawdown manager's capital redeemed during his worst year, yet the LP retained conviction because the AUM was challenged while the business itself was not.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Distinguishing AUM stress from business viability is key to holding through mass redemptions.
“it was clear that even though the AUM was challenged, the business was not.” ▶ 14:26
Manager Selection and Sourcing Discipline 6
Trinity's portfolio skews toward fundamental, transparent, concentrated managers specifically so the office can easily explain to its many stakeholders where capital sits and in what businesses.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: Stakeholder communication needs can rationally drive an allocator toward transparent, concentrated managers.
“it pushed us towards managers that were transparent and managers that really often concentrated in specific holdings, all to be able to more easily speak to our stakeholders” ▶ 8:35
The best investors can take a hundred data points and identify the one to three that actually matter, a signal-filtering skill Trinity actively tries to hone in its own team.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Prioritize hiring and developing the ability to isolate decisive variables over exhaustive data gathering.
“The best investors we work with can take a 100red data points and tell you the one to three that they think matter.” ▶ 37:30
There is diminishing value in evaluating the 30th fund doing the same strategy; allocators should recognize an indifference point and redirect time toward portfolio construction.
contrarian🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Exhaustive comp-set analysis has a stopping point; over-sourcing wastes scarce time.
“I'm not sure there's a lot of value in looking at the 30th fund that does the same thing that you're looking at” ▶ 37:47
Hours spent by an allocator with a manager correlate strongly with an eventual investment, creating an institutional-momentum bias that allocators must consciously check and be willing to walk away from.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Sunk time creates commitment inertia; disciplined allocators keep the option to turn away at any point.
“there's this correlation of time... that's just this bias that we always have to check.” ▶ 39:24
If an allocator lacks conviction from the very start of an investment, they should not make it, because that conviction is what enables holding through a manager's inevitable early rough patch.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Underwriting conviction upfront is the prerequisite for surviving early drawdowns without capitulating.
“if you don't have that conviction from the start, you shouldn't make the investment.” ▶ 40:19
A manager repeatedly deferring an eager LP's investment request in favor of deepening the personal relationship first signals the trust-building priority that ultimately matters most in long-duration partnerships.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: The most relationship-driven managers gate access on mutual knowledge, not eagerness or dollars.
“he says, "Don't you think we should get to know each other better more?"” ▶ 25:14
Transparency's Edge Cases and IR as a Weapon 8
A manager firing an analyst for repeatedly lying and then voluntarily disclosing the embarrassing full story to LPs demonstrated an ethics-driven compass, which the allocator ultimately read as greater transparency rather than a people-management deficiency.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Willingness to share ugly internal truths builds LP empathy and long-term support rather than eroding it.
“he's just more transparent because we are it's framed to us that all these firms are perfect and professional... But inside of them are humans and humans are complicated” ▶ 22:58
A manager's first rough patch is best experienced early in a fund's life (though not the first year), because the self-knowledge and team muscle built in that moment shape how the firm communicates and survives future stress.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Battle-tested early managers who kept their culture become durable holdings; smooth early runs mask untested fragility.
“it would be for a PM to have their rough patch or one of their rough patches early in the fund's life. hopefully not in the first year” ▶ 19:41
A manager who had a great 10-year run then faltered, tensed up, stopped sharing with LPs, and took non-LP-friendly liquidity actions became untenable to hold, because doubt among the team and investors destroys patience instantly.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Guarded, non-LP-friendly behavior under stress is a decisive exit trigger regardless of past track record.
“at the moment that the institutional base, the team, the investor starts to doubt the relationship, the patience just disappears.” ▶ 20:38
Treating investor relations as an ancillary rather than front-line function wastes a critical resource; firms that empower IR (e.g., a COO who began in IR) optimize their whole team and reap real returns.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Allocators can read a manager's ego and durability through how seriously the firm invests in IR.
“when you view your IRBD as an ancillary function you're missing a really big resource” ▶ 33:14
A cyclically-focused equity fund delivered only mid-single-digit returns for its first five years while markets compounded at high-single to low-double digits, yet outperformed its own sectors and later ran 6x from 2020 because it was battle-tested early and kept its culture.
data_point🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Sector-adjusted outperformance during a muted absolute run can precede a large recovery for a resilient firm.
“The run since 2020 has been a 6x... it's because they were battle tested early and built a business there and they haven't lost that culture.” ▶ 32:27
A firm's willingness to let individual analysts spend unscripted time alone with investors at annual meetings signals authentic trust in their people and is a positive due-diligence tell.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Unscripted analyst access is a low-cost signal of genuine culture and internal trust.
“there's no script, there's no prompt... They just show that they trust their people.” ▶ 31:20
Transparency need not mean disclosing every short; effective managers communicate a short book at the level of themes or sectors so LPs can understand and support it without exposing individual positions.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Thematic-level disclosure lets LPs back a short book without compromising the manager's edge.
“it's making sure you communicate that at a level like uh say themes or sectors or anything so that someone can understand and support it” ▶ 30:24
Trinity actively guards against a bias toward smaller managers by deliberately diversifying across firm age and size, having built a strong high-transparency relationship with a well-known spin-out that now manages over $30 billion.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: Large funds can sustain transparency through group dinners and robust IR teams, so allocators shouldn't reflexively avoid scale.
“they sit at over 30 billion in assets today... What the firm's done as it's grown is figure out a way to keep that kind of connectivity and transparency.” ▶ 26:44
Portfolio Construction and Tilt Management 6
More than half of an institutional allocator's job is being a portfolio manager, working to make the money-weighted return exceed the time-weighted return by adding to what's not working and trimming what is.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Edge comes as much from position sizing and rebalancing discipline as from picking the best managers.
“working every day to make sure your money weighted return is better than your time weighted return.” ▶ 35:03
Managers supply unique betas but have no idea what the aggregate portfolio looks like, so managing the top-down tilts those betas create is the allocator's responsibility, not the managers'.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Bottom-up manager selection must be reconciled into deliberately managed aggregate tilts.
“how they work together is your responsibility not theirs.” ▶ 38:13
Allocators should be wary of making too many top-down macro calls because they lack the tools and expertise, so tilt management should be marginal adjustments referenced against a benchmark rather than active macro bets.
contrarian🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Discipline against over-active top-down calls protects allocators from playing a game they can't win.
“the thing you definitely want to check is people in our seat making too many top- down calls because we do not have the tools to do that.” ▶ 50:02
High manager transparency gives Trinity near-daily P&L and mid-month exposure visibility, which is especially valuable in volatile months to know real-time positioning rather than stale month-end data, even though it shouldn't change long-term actions.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: mediumTrinity Wall Street
So what: Position-level transparency yields real-time aggregate risk visibility that improves tilt management timing.
“it's very nice to know where you are in real time, not where you were at last month's end.” ▶ 49:13
Benchmark-relative investing has been especially hard recently because of index concentration, forcing allocators to actively decide how to address underweights or overweights to the benchmark's top holdings.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Index concentration makes benchmark-aware public books structurally harder to outperform.
“benchmarks have been especially especially hard in in in recent years because of the concentration.”
A 5% position and a 5-basis-point position often get the same airtime at investment committee, so Trinity deliberately reframes decisions in basis points rather than raw dollars to allocate attention proportionally.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: mediumTrinity Wall Street
So what: Framing sizing in percent terms disciplines committee attention against dollar-anchoring bias.
“a 5% position and a five basis point position can often get the same amount of airtime at an internal investment committee meeting. And and that's not necessarily the way it should be.” ▶ 41:28
Exit Discipline and Governance 6
Allocators exit too late in most cases; when something turns and fails to improve, hindsight shows they should have left earlier, and exit decisions should therefore happen faster than add decisions.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Structural bias toward holding means allocators should bias exits earlier and faster.
“In hindsight, we exit too late in most cases” ▶ 42:22
Getting only six to eight of ten manager-stress decisions right can still be highly rewarding, because the winners are typically caught near the drawdown trough and deliver far larger upside than the losers cost.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: The asymmetric payoff of trough-timed holds means a sub-perfect hit rate still compounds favorably.
“if we can get six to seven to eight of those calls right, I think we end up being rewarded significantly” ▶ 46:21
Consistency between mean-reversion and momentum orientation is critical, because oscillating between the two philosophies sets an allocator up to always miss the turning point.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Pick a mean-reversion or momentum discipline and hold it; style-switching guarantees mistimed exits.
“when you start to go between the two, you really set yourself up to always miss the bus stop” ▶ 46:07
Exit red flags beyond performance include increasing portfolio turnover, style drift, erratic behavior, distance to high watermark, direction of AUM, and any improper or illegal conduct which is an immediate termination.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: A concrete red-flag checklist distinguishes recoverable stress from a manager doubting himself into a spiral.
“watching for style drift, watching for erratic behavior. All kinds of things that are telltel signs of of someone truly doubting themselves.” ▶ 42:55
At Trinity the board delegated exit and trim decisions to not require approval before add decisions, on the logic that if you lose faith you should be out and such decisions must happen fast.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: Governance should grant staff faster discretion on exits than on additions given the speed exits demand.
“The first thing that was passed on to not need approval was exit and trim decisions.” ▶ 47:14
When a manager is struggling Trinity conducts a formal written review, pulled forward from its periodic schedule, forcing a documented sell/hold/buy decision and testing whether holding is justified by asking why they aren't adding.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall Street
So what: A forced written sell/hold/buy review counters inertia and aligns the team during moments of pain.
“if we're not adding here, why are we holding this?” ▶ 48:02
Private Markets, Liquidity and Marks Skepticism 7
Private funds are far less reversible than public investments yet ironically offer LPs less access, often showing up saying they close in a month for a 10-to-15-year lockup, so the strategy is to spend time with them when they are not raising.
framework🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Build private relationships during non-fundraising windows since the commitment is long and irreversible.
“private is a lot less reversible... they generally show up and say, "Hey, we're closing in a month."” ▶ 40:39
Private markets have gotten a pass that public markets never receive, and with public markets vastly outperforming the broad private market on a Cambridge index basis, allocators are having difficult conversations about the future path of private allocations.
contrarian🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highCambridge Associates
So what: The performance and scrutiny gap is prompting genuine reconsideration of private-market allocation sizing.
“private markets have, and this is dangerous to generalize, but gotten a pass in a way that the public markets don't.” ▶ 53:50
Private allocations are not essential and should only be held if their expected return exceeds public markets or if they provide a real diversifier, not one manufactured by smoothed quarterly marks or 'laundering of capital.'
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Privates must clear an explicit excess-return or genuine-diversification hurdle rather than earning inclusion by default.
“It can be a diversifier as well, but it needs to be a real diversifier and not just because of uh quarterly marks or some other kind of laundering of capital.” ▶ 54:25
With private markets unable to produce liquidity while public markets sit near all-time highs, the inability to sell now raises real questions about the validity of broad private-market marks.
causal🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: The liquidity freeze at market highs implies carried marks may be overstated across the private universe.
“if you can't sell now, what does that say about marks on a broad basis?” ▶ 55:08
The euphoric 2020-2021 private vintages, when funds returned to market at 12 months instead of the usual third year, may have damaged future returns, while the coming wave of record-size IPOs at roughly 2x historical volume will be a critical dynamic to watch.
prediction🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Over-fast 2020-21 fundraising and an unprecedented IPO wave frame the near-term risk and opportunity for privates.
“how the public market digests IPO volume 2x uh anything we've seen in history.” ▶ 53:30
The contrarian view is that today's private-market stress will eventually create amazing vintages and a great opportunity to partner with groups facing less demand than they are used to and with new funds doing it the right way.
contrarian🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: medium
So what: Reduced-demand and new managers in a stressed private market may offer the best future entry points.
“at some point there'll be a great opportunity to partner with groups that maybe have a little less demand than they're used to” ▶ 55:34
Trinity is relatively well-positioned on liquidity because it began building its private portfolio in 2016, giving it a meaningful but not overly illiquid private allocation as industry-wide liquidity has turned rough.
data_point🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: mediumTrinity Wall Street
So what: A younger, more measured private program cushions an endowment against the current liquidity squeeze.
“We're in an okay position because we started building our private portfolio in 2016.” ▶ 52:08
Trinity's Origins and the Allocator's Mindset 3
Trinity Wall Street's endowment is a relatively new investment office built inside a 320-year-old institution, launched in 2016 by Meredith Jenkins with a fully-invested inherited portfolio to rotate.
anecdote🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: highTrinity Wall StreetMeredith Jenkins
So what: Context: a young endowment office had to build culture and rotate an inherited book from scratch around transparency principles.
“Trinity was this new endowment inside of a 320-year-old institution... we started really building Trinity out in 2016.” ▶ 6:29
The best investors know themselves and spend their careers trying to stay authentic amid constant positive and negative feedback and noise, mirroring how audiences sense authenticity in music.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Self-knowledge and authenticity are underrated durable edges in an investing career full of noise.
“the best investors I meet know themselves and they spend their whole life trying to be that authentic version.” ▶ 5:00
The most important advice for a young allocator is to become comfortable with not knowing, because the belief that a spreadsheet or AI co-pilot holds the answer is not real.
thesis🗣 Nicholas Csicskoconviction: high
So what: Embracing irreducible uncertainty, rather than seeking false precision or AI answers, makes a better investor.
“to think that there's a spreadsheet or a co-pilot answer is just not a thing.” ▶ 58:18

Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy 1 episode(s) · 46 insights

The Two Harvard Dropouts Who raised $800M to take on NVIDIA93 min46 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Etched founders Gavin Uberti and Rob Wachen explain how their inference-only chip and full-rack system (Sohu) target radically higher throughput than NVIDIA by exploiting inference-specific constraints, low-voltage design, and cluster-scale memory, arguing token production will become the biggest market in the world and that the largest companies will be those producing most of the global token supply.
Inference-specific chip architecture bets vs GPUs · 9Inference as the largest future market · 7Vertical integration and production-as-product · 6Software strategy: kernels-first, betting on AI-written kernels · 3Competitive dynamics vs NVIDIA and hyperscaler chips · 5Supply chain, TSMC and power constraints · 3Fundraising, contrarian conviction and near-death moments · 4Talent, recruiting and execution philosophy · 6Hardware-shaped model futures · 3
Inference-specific chip architecture bets vs GPUs 9
Etched claims the entire semiconductor and data center stack is over-engineered with general-purpose buffer, so designing for a single inference use case lets you relax constraints and get compounding gains (20% here, 50% there, 2x elsewhere) into a system radically better for inference.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedEDA toolssemiconductorsinference
So what: The differentiation thesis is workload-specialization, not incremental process gains, implying incumbents' general-purpose designs are structurally handicapped for inference.
≈ “the entire semiconductors and data center industry is built on buffer... when you have a specific use case you're really trying to design for, you can change the constraints a lot.” paraphrase ▶ 2:27
AI data centers never run below ~80C, so Etched removes the default EDA assumption that chips must hit full clock speed at 0C, unlocking system-wide design changes competitors leave on the table.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedEDA tools
So what: Illustrates concretely how relaxing generic sign-off corners yields free performance for a purpose-built inference chip.
“I've never seen an AI data center with ice in it... they're never really going to be running below 80 C anyway.” ▶ 3:22
Etched splits inference into prefill (loading text into KV-cache/model memory) and decode (generating tokens), and uses PD disaggregation to transfer KV caches from a prefill cluster to a decode cluster, optimizing each stage separately.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedKV cacheprefilldecode
So what: Separating prefill (flops-bound) from decode (memory-bound) is the structural basis for their two core technical bets.
“Prefill is reading in a huge volume of text and decode is then using that data to generate output tokens... we call PD disagregation.” ▶ 6:46
GPUs deliver only 20-50% model flops utilization (MFU) and provably cannot hit 100% because higher utilization draws more power and forces thermal throttling that lowers clock speed, so adding flops without solving thermals yields no real gains.
causal🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highGPUsNVIDIAMFU
So what: Frames thermal management, not raw flops, as the true binding constraint on inference throughput.
“on GPUs you often get somewhere between 20 and 50%... you have a thermal issue where as you increase the flop utilization... the chip will self-regulate and actually lower its clock speed.” ▶ 8:44
Etched built a new power-delivery mechanism called low voltage inference that runs its first-gen chip at under half the voltage of any other AI chip, exploiting that power scales quadratically with voltage (Dennard scaling).
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedlow voltage inferenceDennard scalingGPUs
So what: Halving voltage cuts power to a quarter, letting them cram far more flops in the same silicon area without thermal throttling; they predict all future AI chips become low-voltage.
“we were able to create a new mechanism of running at much lower voltages... running at under half the voltage of any other AI chip.” ▶ 10:06
Etched argues Bitcoin miners already run at under a quarter of GPU voltage, proving low-voltage operation is physically possible and that industry experts wrongly claimed you can't run below GPU voltages.
contrarian🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highBitcoin minersGPUsEtched
So what: Existence proof from adjacent chip industries undermines the incumbent consensus that constrained their competitors.
“Bitcoin miners run at under a quarter of the voltage of GPUs. So this is obviously physically possible.” ▶ 9:55
Etched's second core bet, cluster scale memory, pools SRAM and HBM across the full scale-up cluster via a custom interconnect built above Ethernet layer 2, cutting chip-to-chip latency by more than 5x versus GPUs.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedSRAMHBMEthernetinterconnect
So what: The right question is cluster-wide memory bandwidth, not per-chip bandwidth; lower inter-chip latency lets them serve large models at far higher tokens/sec per user.
“You should be asking how much memory bandwidth is on your full scaleup cluster... What we call cluster scale memory.” ▶ 10:42
Blackwell chips take roughly 4,000 nanoseconds for point-to-point chip communication, so scaling to 8x tensor parallelism yields far less than an 8x tokens/sec improvement; the speed-of-light limit is only 2-3 nanoseconds, leaving huge room for improvement.
data_point🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highBlackwellNVIDIAinterconnect
So what: Inter-chip latency is a 1000x+ headroom lever that is under-appreciated relative to raw compute, and central to serving models at 10,000-20,000 tokens/sec.
“on blackwell chips, it can be about 4,000 nonds to go point to point... You can do it in just a handful like 2 three nconds.” ▶ 11:17
Every existing GPU, TPU and AI chip was architected before ChatGPT and is retrofit to modern models, so purpose-built inference chips will look fundamentally different in flops organization, voltage domains, power planes, packaging, board design and interconnect.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highGPUsTPUsChatGPTEtched
So what: The core investment case: a clean-sheet, post-ChatGPT architecture can beat incumbents burdened by legacy general-purpose design.
“It's not that surprising given all these architectures were built before chat GPT.” ▶ 12:00
Inference as the largest future market 7
Etched believes inference will become the biggest market in the world and whoever produces the most tokens will be the most valuable company, because every software company's COGS and every business's opex will increasingly be inference.
thesis🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highinferenceEtchedcoding agents
So what: The thesis reframes AI value capture around token production capacity rather than model IP alone.
“We know inference is going to be the biggest market in the world. Whoever produces the most tokens is going to be the most valuable company in the world.” ▶ 0:00
Only a few million people worldwide use paid AI plans, roughly 1/1000th of the global population, implying AI is at ~0.1% penetration and demand has orders of magnitude to grow.
data_point🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highAIinference
So what: Frames current infrastructure as radically undersized for eventual billion-user concurrency.
“there's only a few million users in the world using paid plans of AI models. So, we're at 1 1,000th of the global population actually using this stuff.” ▶ 15:32
Faster decode compresses wall-clock time on long-horizon agentic tasks, turning a year-long compute run into a month, then days, then hours, accelerating scientific discovery and iteration cycles.
causal🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highinferenceagentswall clock time
So what: Positions decode speed as the lever that unlocks entirely new agentic use cases, not just faster chat.
“a year-long compute build would now take a month and that month-long compute build will now take 3 days and that 3-day compute build will now take 7 hours.” ▶ 47:35
Etched frames the goal as bringing iPhone/car-style economies of scale to token production, arguing tokens are currently handcrafted by small general-purpose systems like Renaissance-era screws.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedtokensiPhone
So what: Mass-production economics, not just per-chip performance, is positioned as the defensible moat.
“a general purpose system, relatively small one, is kind of handcrafting these tokens uh like they made screws back in like the Renaissance.” ▶ 17:02
Etched predicts inference will become a majority of global GDP and that productivity will be measured as agents per megawatt or agents per gigawatt rather than GDP per capita.
prediction🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: mediuminferenceGDPagents
So what: Extreme long-run TAM framing that motivates the capital intensity of building token factories.
“I firmly believe we are on a global march of inference becoming majority of global GDP.” ▶ 1:24:38
Etched predicts 2026 is the second-to-last year that a majority of the knowledge workforce is human, with more agents than humans doing knowledge work by 2027.
prediction🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: mediumagentsknowledge work
So what: Aggressive near-term timeline for agent adoption that underpins their demand projections.
“I think in 2027, you're going to see there's going to be more agents doing knowledge work than humans.” ▶ 1:25:06
Etched expects trillion-dollar individual data centers to be inevitable, analogous to fabs scaling past $40B, functioning as mega token factories serving one or a handful of models to massive user bases.
prediction🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highdata centersfabstokens
So what: Signals a belief that inference infrastructure will consolidate into enormous single-purpose builds, favoring scale-optimized systems.
≈ “You think we'll see a trillion dollar individual data center? Absolutely. It is a matter of time.” paraphrase
Vertical integration and production-as-product 6
Etched builds a full inference solution (chip, board, power delivery, interconnect, rack and production) rather than just a chip, and claims to be the only startup building its own rack as well as its own chips.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedrackinterconnect
So what: Extreme vertical integration is positioned as both a performance and speed advantage that differentiates them from chip-only startups.
“We're actually I think we're the only startup right now that's building its own rack as well as its own chips.” ▶ 26:25
Etched recruited Brian Lerer, who started NVIDIA's HGX and DGX systems representing roughly 80% of NVIDIA revenue (tens of billions per quarter), to build its rack team.
data_point🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highBrian LererNVIDIAHGXDGXEtched
So what: Signals ability to poach the specific person who built the incumbent's highest-revenue rack systems, de-risking their rack ambitions.
“we brought over Brian Lerer uh who built all of Nvidia's HGX and DGX systems which is like 80% of the revenue.” ▶ 26:37
Etched practices 'pre-fetching' the schedule: it shipped racks with networking/CPU/storage to customer data centers without chips, ran a dozen models on 700+ FPGAs, and built cold plates from a thermal-mock chip so everything was ready before silicon arrived.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedFPGAscold platesdata centers
So what: Massively parallelizing the schedule with capital is their method for compressing time-to-market.
“we took over 700 FPGAs and put the entire full reticle chip on an FPGA cluster and ran a dozen different models with our full inference stack on them before the chips came back.” ▶ 39:00
Etched went from receiving silicon to running inference in a rack in 40 days, versus a famous AI chip company that publicly took 10 months, because it had pre-built the software, rack and production line.
data_point🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedinference
So what: Concrete, quantified evidence of execution velocity advantage over a named-category competitor.
“There was another very famous AI chip company uh that took 10 months to go from getting their silicon back to having them running inference in Iraq... We were able to do it in 40 days.” ▶ 39:26
Etched runs 24/7 development with day and night shifts, has over half the company living next to the office, operates a 2 megawatt data center on its floor, and a factory in Taiwan with a few dozen people cloning its test stations.
anecdote🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedTaiwan
So what: Operational intensity and geographic co-location are treated as deliberate levers for schedule compression.
“we have a 2 megawatt data center on this floor. Uh and we did 24/7 development cycles.” ▶ 27:08
Etched sees natural business boundaries at the chip layer on the bottom and the model layer on top, and intends to build everything in between while declining to build its own data centers because that would not add token capacity today.
thesis🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtcheddata centerschipsmodels
So what: Clarifies their vertical scope and discipline: integrate only where it adds token capacity or is required for scale.
“We think the natural boundaries are on the chip side, on the bottom, and the model layer at the top. and we'll fill the whole gap between.” ▶ 55:34
Software strategy: kernels-first, betting on AI-written kernels 3
Etched deliberately skipped building a general graph compiler and chose kernels-first programming, betting a sub-100 set of models would dominate and that improving coding models would eventually write all kernels superhumanly.
thesis🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedgraph compilersCUDAPyTorchONNX
So what: Skating to where AI-written kernels make hand-tuned performance the winning long-term architecture while saving compiler engineering time.
“we envisioned a world where there was going to be under a hundred models that actually mattered... we were going to build primitives using physics that were going to accelerate these as much as humanly possible.” ▶ 52:25
Etched designs its profiling and debugging tools primarily for AI models to use rather than humans, and had OpenAI's Codex get GPU code (GPOSS) running from scratch overnight based only on its docs.
anecdote🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedCodexOpenAI
So what: Tooling built for AI consumers is a forward bet that model-driven kernel generation will dominate developer workflows.
“we had codeex actually get GPOSS running from scratch just based off of our docs uh completely by itself... it did it I think overnight.” ▶ 51:53
Etched's kernels-first, compiler-averse philosophy was initially dismissed but resonated with high-frequency trading firms who also write their own kernels, and the company has hired dozens of people from HFT as a result.
anecdote🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedhigh frequency trading
So what: HFT talent and validation is a non-obvious recruiting and credibility channel tied to their software thesis.
≈ “the only people that took us seriously were in high frequency trading uh because they all hate compilers too... dozens of people from high frequency trading join the team.” paraphrase
Competitive dynamics vs NVIDIA and hyperscaler chips 5
Etched argues hyperscaler and lab in-house chips (Google TPU, Meta MTIA, Microsoft Maia, OpenAI's Jalapeno) are not existential to their parents, whereas the best chip in the world is inevitably built by a company that only builds that chip, as NVIDIA proves.
contrarian🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highNVIDIAGoogle TPUMeta MTIAMicrosoft MaiaOpenAI JalapenoEtched
So what: Positions Etched's focus and existential stakes as a structural edge over captive silicon efforts at diversified giants.
“Google won't fail if TPUs fail... Meta won't fail if MTIA fails. Microsoft won't fail if Maya fails and OpenAI won't fail if Jalapeno fails. Ultimately this is our product.” ▶ 56:18
Etched claims in-house lab/hyperscaler chips have lower FP8xFP8 flop density than the NVIDIA B300 because they only need to build a similar-enough product to avoid the NVIDIA tax, not to lead.
data_point🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: mediumNVIDIA B300hyperscalersFP8
So what: Suggests captive chips are cost-avoidance plays, not performance leaders, leaving room for a specialist to win on raw performance.
“The flop density for say FB8 times FB8 is lower than the black B300... they just have to go and build a similar enough product and not pay the Nvidia tax.” ▶ 56:52
Etched positions its hardware as delivering roughly an order of magnitude more concurrency at a given interactivity (tokens/sec speed), directly improving tokens per watt and tokens per dollar for large mixture-of-experts models.
thesis🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedinteractivitymixture of experts
So what: The core commercial claim customers evaluate: how many users served at a required speed within a fixed power budget.
“our hardware is going to generally be able to get you an order of magnitude more concurrency at a given level of interactivity.” ▶ 45:05
Etched frames chip supply as positive-sum by deliberately building its first product on a different supply chain than NVIDIA Rubin (4nm vs 3nm, different HBM), so deployment is often '2 gigawatts' of GPU plus Etched rather than a substitution.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedNVIDIA RubinTSMCHBM4nm3nm
So what: Avoiding node/memory contention with NVIDIA lets customers add Etched capacity without cannibalizing GPU allocation, easing adoption.
“for our firstg product we built it on a different supply chain than the Rubins... we're on 4nometer Ruben's on 3 nanometer.” ▶ 1:18:35
Etched notes Colossus charges about $12/hour for Blackwell GPUs because it is the only place to buy 20,000 at once, illustrating that scarcity of large contiguous compute and power drives pricing.
data_point🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: mediumColossusBlackwellNVIDIA
So what: Scale and power availability, not just chip specs, are pricing levers in the current supply-constrained market.
“why is Colossus charging $12 an hour for black holes? It's because they're the only place you can buy 20,000 of them at once.” ▶ 42:43
Supply chain, TSMC and power constraints 3
Etched argues TSMC's true value is service, not just technology: TSMC will run yield-improvement experiments on its own dime and move its whole line when a customer's recommendation works, unlike other industries.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highTSMCEtched
So what: Deep supplier partnership, not transactional buying, is framed as a decisive competitive factor in chips.
“the real value is all in the service. The TSMC customer service is way way better than I have seen at any other company in any other industry.” ▶ 41:48
Etched frames power availability and time-to-power as a core constraint, noting it is much easier to scale from 100MW to a gigawatt than from a gigawatt to 10, driving a push for maximum tokens per megawatt.
causal🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedpowerPUE
So what: Energy efficiency (tokens per megawatt) becomes a primary product metric because building new large power sites is the hard bottleneck.
≈ “it's much easier to go from a 100 megawatt to a gigawatt than from a gigawatt to 10 and 10 to 100... we are pushing the limits of what's possible on these timelines.” paraphrase ▶ 43:11
Etched warns that if you have the most performant product but cannot produce it you are 'just a podcast', so supply chain must be considered early in design decisions.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedsupply chain
So what: Producibility is elevated to a first-order design constraint, reinforcing production-as-product.
“if you have the most performant product and you can't produce it, you know, then you're just a podcast.” ▶ 1:19:02
Fundraising, contrarian conviction and near-death moments 4
Etched raised roughly $103M in its Series A (early 2024) after every major Valley investor initially passed, at a time when the biggest semiconductor Series A rounds were around $40-50M, and has since raised nearly a half-dozen rounds totaling $800M.
data_point🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedSeries A
So what: Quantifies the funding gap they overcame and the doubling-down by early backers that funded rapid scaling.
“it's like 103 million. It's like these are all like soft commits and we all look at each other and we say we're going to take it and um that was the series A.” ▶ 1:07:28
Etched faced a near-death cash crisis in early 2024 with only ~$15M in the bank while needing a $40-50M physical design vendor agreement and a full-cluster build projected to cost ~$100M over 12 months.
anecdote🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedphysical design
So what: Illustrates the extreme capital intensity of hardware relative to software and how close the company came to failing.
“this was going to cost a lot more than the $15 million we had in the bank... we think we're going to spend $100 million in the next 12 months.” ▶ 1:03:49
TSMC agreed to provide Etched emulators on favorable multi-year payment terms before the company had raised its $100M, effectively extending a loan of belief, after Gavin impressed a senior TSMC VP at a semiconductor conference dinner.
anecdote🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highTSMCEtched
So what: Supplier conviction, secured through a chance relationship, was a critical enabler that de-risked early capital needs.
“TSMC was willing to work with us back before we'd raised any of that hundred million dollars... let us get uh some of their emulators on extremely favorable terms where we pay over many years.” ▶ 1:07:58
Being a contrarian pre-product hardware bet self-selects for truth-seeking talent and investors, so Etched worries that announcing more product specs could erode this filtering effect that currently attracts believers over opportunists.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: mediumEtched
So what: Their obscurity has been a recruiting and capital moat; consensus arrival could dilute talent quality.
≈ “being such a contrarian bet kind of self-selects... we may lose some of this if we're not very careful.” paraphrase
Talent, recruiting and execution philosophy 6
Etched uses 'project-based recruiting': mapping the hardest technical problems ever solved across industries, identifying the zero-to-one person who actually did the work, and pursuing them across ~20 conversations because acceptance rates rise sharply with persistence.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtched
So what: A systematic method for landing world's-best specialists that is central to solving otherwise-impossible problems.
“We created this system we call project-based recruiting where we map out all of the hardest technical problems across all industries that anyone has ever had to solve.” ▶ 28:16
Etched pairs 'legends' (world-number-one experts who know what good looks like) with naive first-principles young talent, arguing the combination working together, not merely coexisting, is what produces breakthroughs.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedBrian LererSanford
So what: A replicable team-composition model for hardware startups facing unprecedented problems.
“It's really the legends plus the raw, you know, some naivee raw first principles type talent, but it's... not just that you have both in the company. It's that they're working together.” ▶ 31:34
Etched's core operating philosophy is 'velocity velocity velocity, you win by shipping', deliberately choosing to have almost no external communications and to run lean rather than scaling to 20,000 employees like incumbents.
thesis🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedNVIDIA
So what: Product velocity over communications/headcount is the stated competitive doctrine, analogous to their two-person robotics championship approach.
“you win by shipping. You're not going to go out and win by having the best outreach or the best communications... you win by having the best product.” ▶ 25:35
Etched shipped a dozen top engineers (including Gavin for 4.5 months) to Bangalore for six months to run 24-hour development cycles with the US team when a vendor fell a year behind, solving what would otherwise have been a year-long delay.
anecdote🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedBangalore
So what: Personally flooding the binding constraint (Musk-style) is treated as a central, repeatable tenet of hardware execution.
“We went out and shipped a dozen of our top engineers across the world to Bangalore for 6 months. I was there as well.” ▶ 34:44
Etched solved a chip-killing clock-domain-crossing bug by inventing a drift-based phase-locking mechanism that aligns two clock signals to within 50 picoseconds two billion times per second, in a dark two-week sprint after some employees quit calling it unsolvable.
anecdote🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtched
So what: Concrete proof of the team's ability to solve seemingly impossible problems, which the investor cites as a rare binary company trait.
“we had to go line up two clock signals on our chip to within 50 picosconds... And do it on every chip two billion times a second... We we had people quit.” ▶ 57:55
Etched runs many parallel experiments expecting most to fail, needing only one to work, exemplified by chip bring-up with ~30 board experiments where only three worked but all three were decisive.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtched
So what: A high-variance, parallelized experimentation culture is how they de-risk unprecedented hardware problems.
“our chip bring up with I think 30 different board experiments and and three of them worked and all three of them are worth their weight in gold... I only got to get lucky once.” ▶ 1:00:04
Hardware-shaped model futures 3
Etched argues machines don't think like people (airplanes don't fly like birds): for neurons memory is cheap and math expensive, but for chips math is cheap and memory expensive, and math will keep getting cheaper faster than memory due to DRAM limits.
framework🗣 Gavin Uberticonviction: highEtchedDRAMmixture of experts
So what: Implies future models should lean into massive compute (more/larger experts, many parallel copies) rather than memory, shaping both model and hardware design.
≈ “machines don't think like people think. You look at airplanes for example that airplanes don't fly like birds fly... math gets cheaper at a rate that is faster than memory gets cheaper.” paraphrase ▶ 1:19:59
Etched sees 'dynamism', controlling compute and memory per-token and per-user and dynamically routing data between chips, as a key model trend that legacy hardware handles inefficiently, creating an opening for hardware built to accelerate dynamic computation.
thesis🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedmixture of expertsattention
So what: Hardware-model co-design around dynamism (MoE, variable context) is a differentiation vector legacy chips can't match.
“a theme in models right now is this focus on something called dynamism which is this ability to control the level of computation and memory spent at a per token or per user level.” ▶ 1:21:33
Etched envisions large MoE models run as 'distributed brains' cut into many experts across many servers so that high user volume keeps each part of the brain busy, driving cost per token dramatically lower through concurrency economies of scale.
framework🗣 Rob Wachenconviction: highEtchedmixture of expertsdata centers
So what: Concurrency-driven utilization is the mechanism by which their cluster-scale architecture lowers per-token cost at massive scale.
“you're going to end up with these giant scale distributed brains... a big data center with a bunch of chips, a huge amount of flops, and a huge amount of scale up interconnect.” ▶ 1:26:48

Peter H. Diamandis 1 episode(s) · 45 insights

US Government Blocks GPT-5.6, Alibaba's AI Theft, and Why OpenAI Is Stalling Their IPO | #267138 min45 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Moonshots roundtable (Diamandis, Alex Wissner-Gross, Dave Blundin, Emad Mostaque) argues the US executive branch has begun gating frontier AI (throttling OpenAI's GPT-5.6 and Anthropic's Mythos to a handful of vetted customers), that Chinese open-weight models are converging on frontier parity by year-end, that OpenAI is stalling its sub-trillion IPO because it doesn't need capital and regulatory overhead is crushing, and that photonic compute plus quantum could be the next discontinuity.
US government gating of frontier AI · 6China convergence, harnesses and open-weight models · 4Cyber offense/defense and model theft · 6OpenAI IPO stall and lab financing · 6Neuralink telepathy and BCI · 3Musk universe, video generation and world models · 6Quantum, photonics and the next discontinuity · 5Longevity: orexin and short-sleep drugs · 3IP, UBI and space compute policy · 6
US government gating of frontier AI 6
The US executive branch has placed a national-security hold on commercial AI, forcing OpenAI to release GPT-5.6 to only ~20 select companies and Anthropic to release Mythos 5 to ~100 select companies, with the government approving access customer-by-customer.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGPT-5.6Mythos 5Trump administrationWhite House
So what: Regulatory gatekeeping of frontier model access is a new structural risk/moat for US labs; watch how customer allow-lists shape enterprise adoption and valuations.
“Bottom line, the US government is now in the release loop for the most capable models, customer by customer, selecting who gets access to the latest models.” ▶ 7:26
The US government is functioning as a synchronization mechanism forcing the OpenAI/Anthropic duopoly to release capabilities at roughly the same time and level, effectively pushing GPT-5.6 to near-parity with Anthropic's Mythos preview.
framework🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGPT-5.6Mythos
So what: If the state is the coordination mechanism collapsing the labs' race dynamic, relative-capability advantages between the two US leaders may compress, reshaping competitive positioning.
“Turns out that the same old coordination mechanism that we've had for thousands of years, government, localized geographic monopoly on force, is more than capable... of being that coordination mechanism.” ▶ 11:40
There is a real risk that AGI-level capability is achieved internally inside the two US frontier labs while external users (even American ones) are held at or behind parity with Chinese models, creating a capability event-horizon split.
thesis🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicChina
So what: A widening internal-vs-external capability gap could create perverse incentives (talent flocking to labs for access) and mask the true frontier from public markets and competitors.
≈ “there is very much the risk that AGI is achieved internally but externally for all of the users the users are American users and western users of American frontier models are stuck at par or or worse behind par with Chinese models” paraphrase
The panel expects the US to require KYC, prompt retention, and a patriotism/citizenship-based license to access frontier (as opposed to merely competent) models, and to ban corporate use of Chinese open-weight models.
prediction🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumUS governmentChinaDavid SacksHoward Lutnick
So what: A licensing/KYC regime would create compliance moats and slow diffusion; onshore inference vendors and identity/verification layers become investable.
“Just like you have a driving license, you will need to have a license where you basically say that you're patriotic to America and convince the frontier model of that to get your license.” ▶ 25:36
Export controls on frontier models are effectively protectionism masquerading as security, and if China's open-weight models keep converging the US risks companies worldwide adopting on-prem Chinese models instead.
contrarian🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumChinaUS governmentDavid Sacks
So what: Global demand could route to unrestricted Chinese open-weight models, undermining US lab revenue outside the allow-listed US market.
“superficially it looks like perfectionis protectionism may be masquerading as export control and there is I think a very real risk that the US falls behind” ▶ 22:12
The next US policy step is expected to be conditioning global access to top American AI on other countries (Europe, South America, East Asia) adopting a new US-led rulebook, akin to a 'new NATO' for AI being drafted by David Sacks.
prediction🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: mediumDavid SacksUS governmentEuropeChinaNATO
So what: A bloc-based AI access regime ('Pax Intelligencia') would geopolitically segment the AI market; watch for bilateral access deals as a leading indicator.
“We will not let you access this unless you follow this new playbook. And that's what David Saxs is busy writing right now. Like what are the new NATO?” ▶ 24:05
China convergence, harnesses and open-weight models 4
An extrapolation circulating on X shows Chinese open-weight models reaching parity with the two remaining Western frontier open-weight models on a trajectory to hit zero time-lag by Christmas 2026.
prediction🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumChinaopen-weight models
So what: If the capability gap closes by December, US export-control moats erode fast; time-sensitive for anyone underwriting sustained US lab pricing power.
“if you extrapolate it... the number is on a trajectory to go to zero by Christmas of this year” ▶ 13:15
China's GLM 5.2, said to cost only ~$25 million of compute, tops the difficult Frontier SWE coding benchmark under the right multi-model harness and outperforms GPT-5.5 in Intelligent Internet's tests, showing harnesses now matter more than base-model weights.
data_point🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: highGLM 5.2GPT-5.5Frontier SWEIntelligent InternetSakanaBlitzySWE-Bench Pro
So what: Cheap Chinese models plus orchestration can leapfrog gated US frontier models, undercutting the case that gating protects US advantage.
“GLM 5.2 is maybe $25 million worth of code of compute... now you've seen actually with the right harness open models already can be at the top” ▶ 15:49
Because existing released models (GPT-5.5, Opus 4.8) can be wrapped in a strong harness to exceed the capability of gated models like Mythos or GPT-5.6, the government's throttle is already too late and the cat is out of the bag.
causal🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: highGPT-5.5Opus 4.8MythosGPT-5.6Blitzy
So what: Regulatory gating of new weights may be circumventable via scaffolding on already-public models, limiting policy efficacy and favoring harness/orchestration vendors.
“You can already take 5.5 or Opus 4.8 and put enough of a brilliant harness around it to make it better than Mythos or better than um 5.6 GPT 5.6. Therefore... the cat's out of the bag” ▶ 17:05
Chinese labs may have reached a recursive self-improvement loop where GLM 5.2's gains over 5.1 came from iterative data self-improvement, meaning China may no longer need Western model outputs to stay near the frontier.
thesis🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumGLM 5.2ChinaAlibabaMercor
So what: If Chinese self-improvement is self-sustaining, cutting off distillation/API access won't halt their progress, undercutting the strategic rationale for controls.
“you can't guarantee that if today China could never use any of the US models again, the existing data set they have, the techniques they have are not good enough to keep up with frontier capability” ▶ 1:24:37
Cyber offense/defense and model theft 6
Anthropic's Mythos model, running red-team exercises under Project Glass Wing with US intelligence, reportedly broke into nearly all classified US government systems in hours, which triggered the administration to disable Mythos 5 and Fable 5 for foreign nationals ~12 days later.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highAnthropicMythosFableProject Glass WingSenator Mark WarnerUS intelligence
So what: Demonstrated offensive cyber capability is the stated trigger for the export hold; cyber-hardening and defensive-AI vendors gain tailwind.
“This tool broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours.” ▶ 26:37
OpenAI's defensive GPT-5.5 cyber model 'Daybreak' scored a record 85.6 on the Cyber Gym benchmark, the highest single-model score, and is being steered toward defense over offense with hints of poisoning attacker use-cases.
data_point🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: highOpenAIGPT-5.5 cyberDaybreakCyber GymSam Altman
So what: Alignment via defense-favoring scaffolding that poisons offensive use is emerging as a product category; the trust/liability layer for auto-merging AI fixes is a control point worth owning.
“The real prize isn't finding the holes. It's automatically writing and testing the fixes across web browsers all the way down to the Linux kernel, effectively turning the threat into a cure.” ▶ 34:56
Anthropic accuses Alibaba of running a distillation campaign using 28.8 million fraudulent exchanges across 25,000 fake accounts to copy Claude's capabilities, potentially the largest AI model-theft accusation ever.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highAnthropicAlibabaClaude
So what: This becomes the political trigger for cracking down on Chinese AI; expect heavy litigation over whether distillation constitutes espionage.
≈ “28.8 million fraudulent exchanges across 25,000 fake accounts to securely extract and copy Claude's capabilities... the single largest AI model theft accusition ever made” paraphrase ▶ 1:19:47
Chinese proxies reportedly resell access to Western models (Anthropic, OpenAI) at ~90% discount in exchange for harvesting users' reasoning traces, which can then be used for distillation.
causal🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIChina
So what: Reasoning-trace leakage via cheap proxies is a covert IP exfiltration channel; a reason labs may lock down API openness and privacy terms.
≈ “you pay a lot less you get access to the models via these proxies... the proxies gather those reasoning traces using you basically as a sock puppet” paraphrase ▶ 1:21:56
The base weights of frontier models (pre-system-prompt/pre-post-training) are becoming the highest-value espionage target because they are more creative and less hobbled for offensive cyber attacks by nation-state adversaries.
thesis🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumAnthropicColossusElon MuskxAI
So what: Data-center weight security becomes strategically critical; the loading of Anthropic weights onto Colossus raises the espionage stakes.
“what you want is the model that comes before the end of pre-training or before the system prompt... because they're more creative and they're not as hobbled for the attack” ▶ 38:22
The US government may soon require ISO-type certified, trustworthy models to merge code, because AI-written code can hide backdoors in a model's latent space that go undetected for days, months or years and no human reviews every line anymore.
prediction🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumUS governmentChinaPalantir
So what: A certification/trust-stamp regime for code-generating models is an emerging market; the certifying entity (US vs NATO-type) is a strategic control point.
“who on this call now actually reviews every line of code that they merge” ▶ 39:41
OpenAI IPO stall and lab financing 6
OpenAI leadership is pulling back on a near-term IPO because Altman refuses to go public below a $1 trillion valuation and the company doesn't need capital, having recently raised ~$122 billion.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highOpenAISam Altman
So what: Frontier labs staying private longer keeps the largest wealth-creation event out of reach of retail and avoids quarterly scrutiny of massive compute burn.
“Altman apparently is not interested in going public below a trillion dollars.” ▶ 42:48
SpaceX's IPO is characterized as near-perfectly priced (opened ~$150, trading ~$153 vs $135 IPO price, ~$2T valuation), so OpenAI citing SpaceX volatility as its reason to delay is dismissed as a convenient excuse.
contrarian🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: highSpaceXOpenAIElon Musk
So what: The real IPO-delay drivers are lack of capital need, governance/leadership transition and regulatory overhead, not competitor volatility.
≈ “That's pretty much a perfectly priced IPO... So you're just looking for an excuse to delay your IPO. This is a really good one to use because you're pointing at your competitor” paraphrase ▶ 44:06
OpenAI erred by focusing too early on consumer as its revenue engine and is now racing to become enterprise-focused like Anthropic, with Codex revenue reportedly skyrocketing.
thesis🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicCodexChatGPTSarah Friar
So what: The IPO likely hinges on brain-swapping Codex-style enterprise revenue into the core and showing profitability; watch enterprise/Codex revenue ramp.
“They're now trying to become anthropic faster than Anthropic can become OpenAI.” ▶ 46:44
OpenAI is forecast to lose ~$26 billion this year but its revenue has grown to ~$40-50 billion run rate, catching up to its valuation, while Anthropic is closer to needing a raise or IPO relative to its balance.
data_point🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Anthropic faces nearer-term financing pressure than OpenAI; watch for an Anthropic raise or structural moves (e.g., acquiring Sierra, Brett Taylor as CEO).
“they're going to lose $26 billion this year according to their forecast. And as Dave said, they raised 120 billion.” ▶ 52:08
SpaceX's cloud/AI business run-rate revenue has reportedly overtaken AWS and GCP within a few months, an unprecedented revenue-growth trajectory.
data_point🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: lowSpaceXAWSGCP
So what: If accurate, SpaceX is becoming a major cloud/compute player, materially changing the hyperscaler competitive landscape.
“I was actually looking at SpaceX's AI revenue from just their cloud business. It's overtaken AWS and GCP on a run rate.” ▶ 51:36
Frontier labs are reluctant to IPO because SEC/public-company overhead (disclosing hundreds of personal investments, FD-approved communications, stock lawsuits) is a distraction during a hard takeoff when the world a year out is expected to look nothing like today.
thesis🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: highOpenAISECSam AltmanDario Amodei
So what: Expect frontier labs to stay private and use secondary liquidity rather than IPOs, keeping public-market AI exposure scarce.
“friends don't let friends run public companies” ▶ 46:01
Neuralink telepathy and BCI 3
Elon Musk announced Neuralink may attempt the first direct brain-to-brain human telepathic communication later in 2026, framed as an I/O layer for humans to couple with AI during the singularity rather than a medical device.
prediction🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: mediumNeuralinkElon Musk
So what: First superhuman (not restorative) BCI milestone; a proof point could ignite demand for non-invasive BCI products.
“Neuralink may make an attempt later this year for the first direct humanto human telepathic communications, literally transmitting from my brain to your brain” ▶ 55:34
A recent Cell paper found bilingual humans' hippocampus stores concepts like an encoder-only transformer vector-embedding space, with translation-equivalent words placed adjacently, suggesting human cognition may be less complex than assumed and telepathy easier than expected.
data_point🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: highNeuralinkCell (journal)
So what: If human latent spaces mirror AI embeddings, high-bandwidth and non-invasive brain-to-brain interfaces become far more tractable, expanding the BCI TAM.
“the hippo campus of the humans bilingual humans turns out to look more or less like a vector embedding space for an encoder only transformer model. That is a shocking conclusion.” ▶ 57:45
Human I/O is extremely narrow (speech ~40-60 bits/sec, typing ~5-20 bits/sec, conscious thought ~10 bits/sec), so shared human latent spaces could allow telepathy bandwidth to jump 10,000-100,000x by using minimal words to activate common representations.
framework🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumNeuralinkStability AI
So what: The bandwidth thesis reframes BCI value from data-rate to latent-space alignment; a 2023 'MindEye' fMRI-to-image reconstruction supports shared-latent-space claims.
“the bandwidth's probably going to go up 10, 100,000 times from there because you can adapt the adapters to hit the person at the right time” ▶ 1:01:07
Musk universe, video generation and world models 6
Musk unveiled a naming architecture where Starlink moves bits, Starfall moves atoms (cargo up/down from LEO), and Starmind is SpaceX's AI orbital constellation / Dyson-swarm brand, while Starpipe signals a nascent SpaceX oil-and-gas/methane refining play.
data_point🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumSpaceXStarlinkStarfallStarmindStarpipeStargazeElon MuskPlanet
So what: Maps SpaceX's expansion into orbital compute, down-mass logistics, refining and situational awareness (a potential Planet competitor); each is a new vertical to watch.
“Starlink moves bits, starfall moves atom, star mind moves intelligence.” ▶ 1:02:42
ByteDance's Seedance/Cance 2.5 (beta, July release) generates 30-second 4K videos referencing up to 50 different inputs with cinematographic and text-prompt editing control, meaning China is running away with video generation.
data_point🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: highByteDanceCance 2.5SeedanceChinaGoogle GeminiGrok
So what: Near-perfect pixel/character control puts Hollywood-level AI film within reach and displaces media labor; US competitors (Gemini Omni ~10s, Grok) lag.
“50 inputs. This means 50 different characters and you can input video, audio, images and others... you have almost perfect pixel control” ▶ 1:09:59
China leads video generation partly because Chinese labs have cheaper, less copyright-encumbered training data (e.g., TikTok video) and because US labs are chasing far more lucrative code-generation, with codegen revenue ~10-20x video-gen revenue.
causal🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: highChinaTikTokByteDancecodegen
So what: Economics explain the US video-gen gap; Western labs re-enter only when a lucrative enterprise video use-case (real-time meeting participants) emerges.
“you're probably not too worried about being sued for copyright infringement for all of the Tik Tok or other similar video video data you're using for pre-training” ▶ 1:13:08
The video-game market (~a couple hundred billion) dwarfs Hollywood (~$50B), and whoever solves real-time, low-latency 4K generative video embedded in interactive games captures the big prize, with China's world-model progress (e.g., ByteDance) giving them an edge toward real-time 4K games next year.
thesis🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: highvideo game industryHollywoodLiquid AIChinaByteDance
So what: Real-time generative gaming is a larger prize than film; latency is the current bottleneck and Liquid AI's efficient context handling is a contender.
“the video game industry is already bigger than all other media media combined including movies” ▶ 1:15:32
Enterprise real-time interactive video (e.g., Alibaba's 'one streamer' video-to-video demo, or a generative Zoom/FaceTime participant) is a ~$30 trillion enterprise-software market that dwarfs even video games, and is where Western labs should compete.
thesis🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumAlibabaenterprise softwareZoom
So what: Real-time interactive world-model video for enterprise is framed as the largest addressable market; a strategic re-entry point for US labs.
“there's a market that's orders of magnitude larger than video games and that's enterprise use cases” ▶ 1:17:46
Liquid AI is a from-scratch foundation-model company (not built on OpenAI/Anthropic) with a more efficient non-transformer context mechanism enabling on-device edge use, and is planning a capital raise in a couple of months, with Mercedes cars from September running Liquid AI.
data_point🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: mediumLiquid AIMercedes
So what: Edge/on-prem efficient models and a Mercedes design win make Liquid AI a name to watch ahead of its raise.
“if you buy a Mercedes in September and you talk to your car, you're talking to liquid AI” ▶ 1:14:37
Quantum, photonics and the next discontinuity 5
Trump signed an executive order committing ~$2B in venture investments to US quantum via the chips/science act, with IBM getting ~$1B for its Anderon quantum chip foundry in Albany, and D-Wave, Rigetti and 'Inflection' each ~$100M and PsiQuantum a $140M equity stake.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highIBMD-WaveRigettiPsiQuantumTrump administrationquantum computing
So what: Direct government equity/funding creates catalysts for named quantum stocks; the forthcoming US sovereign wealth fund would gain quantum exposure.
“IBM received the lion share, a billion dollars of the program to co-develop their Anderon quantum chip foundry in Albany, New York.” ▶ 1:31:17
Quantum computing for science acceleration has largely underdelivered (protein folding was solved classically by AlphaFold 3, not quantum), and its economically useful quantum-advantage algorithms remain scarce.
contrarian🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumAlphaFold 3quantum computingSandbox AQ
So what: Tempers quantum-computing hype; the real near-term value may be quantum sensing and quantum networking rather than computing.
“Quantum computing was supposed to give us protein folding. Turns out protein folding problem was solved by arguably alpha fold 3 purely classically without use of any quantum computing.” ▶ 1:34:24
The convergence of super-capable AI models (that can finally program quantum computers with the right questions) and quantum supremacy could let us answer problems in microseconds that would otherwise require coating the universe in compute, potentially obviating the need for Dyson swarms.
thesis🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumquantum computingSandbox AQ
So what: A frontier-AI-plus-quantum discontinuity could disrupt nation-states and reduce the energy/compute scaling race; the US is trying to lead it.
“we might not need Dyson spheres and that is actually something” ▶ 1:36:10
Photonic (quantum-photonic, not quantum) computing is the near-term discontinuity: highly quantized neural nets can perform as well as FP32, enabling photonic compute at ~1/100th (or better) the mass per unit of computation versus Nvidia chips.
prediction🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: highNvidiaphotonicsQuantum.AIElon MuskSpaceXIntelGoogle
So what: Photonic compute at a fraction of the mass/power is a threat to Nvidia's model and ideal for orbital data centers; Blundin's new Quantum.AI and Musk's photonics acquisition are plays here.
“it's almost a certainty now that highly quantized neural nets can perform just as well as floatingoint 32 neural nets, which opens the door to massive amounts of photonic computation efficiency” ▶ 1:40:57
Elon Musk/SpaceX just acquired a photonic computing/communication company for several billion dollars, and China's Xanadu-series photonics is an area where the US is reportedly underinvested.
data_point🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumSpaceXElon MuskphotonicsChinaIntelGoogle
So what: Photonics is becoming an acquisition battleground; expect it to be integrated into Starlink/Starmind and to draw more US policy attention.
“Elon/P SpaceX just acquired for several billion dollars a phetonic computing/communication company to to merge in” ▶ 1:42:46
Longevity: orexin and short-sleep drugs 3
Eli Lilly acquired Centessa Pharmaceuticals for $6.3 billion for a drug targeting the orexin neuropeptide (the brain's master wakefulness switch), which the panel expects to become a lifestyle short-sleep drug beyond narcolepsy.
data_point🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highEli LillyCentessa Pharmaceuticalsorexinnarcolepsy
So what: Orexin agonists could be the next GLP-1-scale category; a short-sleep lifestyle drug has enormous productivity and market implications.
“Eli Liy purchased for $6.3 billion a company called Centessa Pharmaceuticals. Uh, their drug targets a neuropeptide called Areexin in the brain.” ▶ 1:47:02
Orexin agonists could be as transformative as GLP-1 receptor agonists, with Eli Lilly re-running its GLP-1 playbook (diabetes to broad HealthSpan) in neuroscience via narcolepsy, with GLP-1 revenue already comparable to or larger than OpenAI/Anthropic token revenue on similar timescales.
thesis🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: highEli LillyGLP-1orexinOpenAIAnthropic
So what: A receptor-agonist platform playbook applied to sleep/neuroscience could create another trillion-dollar pharma category; watch Eli Lilly's neuro pipeline.
“I think this is potentially as transformative as the GLP-1 receptor agonists. This is Eli Liy seemingly playing the the same playbook over again.” ▶ 1:47:50
Diamandis says an undisclosed stealth portfolio company is the number-one player in the short-sleep space with data showing roughly double the performance of the Centessa drug.
anecdote🗣 Peter Diamandisconviction: highorexinCentessa Pharmaceuticals
So what: Signals a competitive stealth asset potentially worth more than Centessa's $6.3B if the claimed 2x performance holds.
“one of my portfolio companies, which is still under stealth, is the number one player in this area... it's, you know, double the performance of the Cintessa drug” ▶ 1:46:56
IP, UBI and space compute policy 6
The panel splits on whether AI destroys IP protection: one view is AI makes IP meaningless because anything can be reinvented/remixed and one-shotted within a few years; the counter-view is AI supercharges IP defense (better patent drafting and litigation), so IP may be strengthened not eliminated.
framework🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumintellectual propertypatents
So what: IP-litigation-as-a-service and AI patent tooling could boom even as copying gets easier; investors should watch both offense and defense of IP.
“it might just be utterly supercharged in terms of its ability to protect” ▶ 1:30:25
UBI funded purely by dividends from US golden shares in AI companies is mathematically impossible: at 5% yield you'd need companies worth ~$10 trillion and to own half of them just to get halfway, and a $3k/month UBI for US residents alone would cost ~$12 trillion/year versus a ~$7.4 trillion US budget.
data_point🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: highUBIUS government
So what: Dividend-based UBI doesn't pencil out; monetary-creation models are more plausible, reframing how AI-era redistribution could be financed.
“It's about 10 trillion and you'd have to own like half of them to get to like halfway there.” ▶ 1:53:10
Argentina's proposed non-human AI corporation regime could attract millions or billions of autonomous AI-person companies (not frontier model labs), though many would run on Chinese base models unless the US restricts Argentina's ability to import them.
prediction🗣 Alex Wissner-Grossconviction: mediumArgentinaMileiChina
So what: A jurisdiction for autonomous AI corporations is emerging; US export policy could extend to third countries to block Chinese-model-powered AI firms.
“I can totally see many millions, billions of those types of non-human AI companies migrating to Argentina.” ▶ 1:55:09
