Low Earth orbit (under ~500km) is the sweet spot for orbital data centers because residual atmosphere self-cleans the environment, while beyond that the Kessler debris effect threatens them; the moon is favored for security, defensibility and in-situ raw materials (regolith: silicon, oxygen, nickel, iron) to build more data centers.
framework🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: mediumSpaceXlow Earth orbitthe moonKessler effect
So what: Frames the physical/economic logic of space compute placement; lunar mining and mass drivers become part of the compute build-out roadmap.
“low Earth orbit is great for data centers but then you get outside 500 km altitude and you get the Kesler effect problem and all kinds of debris flying around” ▶ 1:59:27
Heavy Nvidia data centers (a couple tons per NVL72 with power) are likely to be deployed in oceans in the near term (Microsoft's Project Natic proved lower failure rates over two years) while photonic compute at a fraction of the mass is built for launch, so ocean and space data centers will both happen, in that order.
prediction🗣 Dave Blundinconviction: mediumNvidiaMicrosoftProject NatickphotonicsPantalassa
So what: Near-term compute goes subsea (cheap cooling, no launch cost) while photonic breakthroughs enable orbital compute; ocean-cooling and photonics are both investable trends.
“people will want to put the NVIDIA data centers in the ocean and not launch them because they're very heavy” ▶ 2:13:58
Europe is only slowly waking to AI competitiveness; there is a good chance the EU AI Act gets repealed after the Fable/Mythos episode shocked decision-makers, and reforms like EU Inc (company setup in under a day) are emerging.
prediction🗣 Emad Mostaqueconviction: mediumEuropean UnionEU AI ActEU IncFable
So what: Potential EU AI Act repeal and faster incorporation could shift European AI investability; the highest-leverage EU opportunity is agentic workflows into legacy industries, not frontier models.
“So I think there's a good chance the EU AI act gets repealed.” ▶ 2:10:36

ChinaTalk · Jordan Schneider 1 episode(s) · 45 insights

Taiwan’s War on Renewables: The Political Economy of Energy Poverty75 min45 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Taiwan imports ~97% of its energy and has spent 40 years dismantling nuclear while botching a renewables rollout, leaving it structurally addicted to seaborne LNG and acutely vulnerable to a Chinese blockade; artificially cheap, state-monopoly electricity pricing, corruption, and failed local-content mandates have starved the grid just as TSMC and AI data-center demand surges, though President Lai's March 2026 nuclear-restart reversal signals a possible turn.
Blockade Vulnerability & Import Dependence · 6Tai Power Monopoly & Distorted Electricity Pricing · 7Offshore Wind Boondoggle & Local-Content Failure · 7Corruption & Permitting Dysfunction · 6Battery Storage Market Failure · 4Grid Constraints on TSMC & AI Data Centers · 4Nuclear Reversal & Political Economy · 5Geopolitical & Investment Signals · 6
Blockade Vulnerability & Import Dependence 6
Taiwan imports roughly 97% of its energy, the highest rate of any major economy, and holds only about 11 days of LNG reserves and roughly 42 days of coal, meaning a sustained naval blockade would cut power within weeks.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTaiwanChinaLNGcoal
So what: Taiwan's grid is a single point of failure that China can attack without firing a shot, making energy the softest target in any coercion scenario.
“97% of Taiwan's energy comes from imported sources, the highest rate of any major economy.” ▶ 0:24
CSIS wargaming found that once Taiwan's coal reserves (roughly 40 days combined) are exhausted under blockade, total electricity production would fall to about 20% of pre-blockade levels, at which point all manufacturing ceases.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highCSISTaiwanTSMC
So what: A blockade need not sink ships to halt Taiwan's chip output; strangling fuel imports collapses the fab economy within ~40 days.
“wargaming by CSIS found that Taiwan's total electricity production would fall to about 20% of pre-blockade levels, at which point all manufacturing would cease.” ▶ 14:46
China could disrupt Taiwan's gas supply below the threshold of an act of war by using its coast guard/maritime vessels to impose fabricated permit requirements on energy tankers, rather than launching a formal blockade or firing missiles.
framework🗣 Angelicaconviction: highChinaPLA NavyTaiwanLNG
So what: Gray-zone energy coercion is a more likely and deniable path than kinetic blockade, so Taiwan's LNG-heavy grid is exposed even short of war.
≈ “they have the means to say oh we have new renewable energy mandate. So any energy vessels coming into our territorial waters have to get this permit and if you don't have a permit then you're out of line.” paraphrase
Cutting Taiwan's gas supply would require blockading only three LNG import terminals, whereas distributed renewables are far harder to disable because killing offshore wind means severing undersea cables in the ADIZ and killing rooftop solar would require bombing apartment buildings.
causal🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTaiwanLNGoffshore windsolarPLA Navy
So what: Distributed renewables are a wartime resilience asset, reframing the energy debate as a defense issue, not just a climate one.
“To cut Taiwan's gas supply, China would only need to blockade three LG terminals.” ▶ 12:54
Taiwan is currently avoiding an LNG price crisis during the 2026 Iran war and Hormuz closure only because Chinese natural-gas demand destruction and idle coal capacity freed up excess spot cargoes, an ironic dependence on Beijing for supply.
contrarian🗣 Angelicaconviction: mediumChinaQatarLNGIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Taiwan's spot-market cushion is a lucky byproduct of Chinese economic conditions, not a durable hedge, and could vanish if Chinese demand recovers.
“I think Taiwan should send a thank you note and a fruit basket to Beijing right now because the reason why we are not having an energy crisis right now is because of China.” ▶ 1:04
Taiwan gets roughly a third of its LNG from Qatar and was contracted for 35% from that source, so the Iran war and damage to Qatar's largest LNG complex forced it onto a spot market where Asian gas prices surged over 140%.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highQatarTaiwanLNGIran
So what: Concentration of LNG sourcing in a single Gulf supplier magnifies Taiwan's exposure to Middle East chokepoint risk on top of China risk.
“Taiwan, which gets roughly a third of its LG fromQatar, is paying the price.” ▶ 0:57
Tai Power Monopoly & Distorted Electricity Pricing 7
Tai Power controls 100% of Taiwan's fossil-fuel and nuclear generation plus all transmission, distribution, and retail, owning everything except renewables, so government fuel bailouts overwhelmingly subsidize fossil generation over clean energy.
framework🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTai PowerTaiwanrenewablesLNGcoal
So what: The monopoly structure hardwires a fossil-fuel bias into subsidies, structurally disadvantaging every merchant renewable and storage developer.
“the only thing they don't own is renewables... So these bailouts are overwhelmingly subsidizing fossil fuels.” ▶ 15:55
By mid-2025 Tai Power had accumulated 418 billion NTD (about 13 billion USD) in losses because household electricity prices were frozen at 3.78 NTD/kWh (~12 cents US) even as LNG prices soared 150% after the Iran war.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTai PowerLNGIran
So what: A state utility bleeding taxpayer money to suppress prices signals no near-term path to market liberalization or profitable merchant power.
“By mid 2025, Tai Power had accumulated 418 billion NTD in losses, which is about 13 billion USD.” ▶ 17:24
Taiwan keeps electricity prices artificially low primarily as monetary policy, because electricity is a key CPI input and holding CPI below 2% removes pressure to raise interest rates.
causal🗣 Jason Funconviction: highTai PowerTaiwanCPI
So what: Energy pricing is hostage to macro policy, so market-based reform faces entrenched central-bank/political resistance, not just utility inertia.
“The reason the government wants the electricity price to be kept so is monetary policy. Because electricity price is one of the important factors in CPI.” ▶ 17:45
Artificially low conventional prices (averaging ~3 NTD/kWh) versus renewable prices of 4.5 to 5 NTD/kWh ensure renewables cannot compete on cost, so absent mandates or subsidies consumers default to fossil power.
causal🗣 Tang Luconviction: highTai Powerrenewables
So what: Suppressed fossil prices are the binding barrier to voluntary renewable uptake, meaning decarbonization stalls without price reform.
“renewable energy prices like 4.5 to 5 new Taiwan per kilowatt hour compared to the conventional price averaging three new Taiwan.” ▶ 18:18
On May 9th, 2025, Taiwan's legislature formally rolled back the 2016 requirement to unbundle Tai Power, legislation sponsored by DPP lawmakers and passed with cross-party support and cheered by Tai Power's union.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTai PowerDPPKMTTPP
So what: The reversal entrenches the monopoly indefinitely, closing off the market structure needed for merchant renewables and storage arbitrage.
“On May 9th, 2025, Taiwan's legislative UN formally rolled back the requirement to break up Tai Power.” ▶ 16:14
Tai Power discriminates by industry when raising prices, immediately hitting high-margin sectors like semiconductors and AI data centers while granting lower-margin industries a delay, which discourages efficiency innovation and slows economic development.
causal🗣 Jason Funconviction: mediumTai PowersemiconductorsAI data centers
So what: Selective pricing burdens Taiwan's most productive export sectors and blunts price signals that would otherwise drive efficiency investment.
“These are the ones that make more money. They will be directly impacted by this price increase... you can enjoy a delay of that electricity price increase.” ▶ 18:56
A think-tank view holds that unbundling Tai Power is not the binding constraint; the real hurdles are limited land, administrative bottlenecks, and grid infrastructure, and the renewable market is already largely driven by independent power producers.
contrarian🗣 Tang Luconviction: mediumTai PowerIPPsrenewables
So what: Even fixing the monopoly would not unlock renewables if land, permitting, and grid limits remain, tempering the case for structural reform alone.
“unbundling uh TPC is not a prerequisite to solving our current development because the primary hurdles as I mentioned is about limited land mass administrative bottlenecks and green infrastructure.” ▶ 33:28
Offshore Wind Boondoggle & Local-Content Failure 7
Taiwan's Round 3.1 and 3.2 offshore wind auctions awarded points for local-content commitments even though Taiwan has no wind-turbine supply chain, so made-in-Taiwan components cost two to three times imported parts and destroyed project profitability.
causal🗣 Angelicaconviction: highTaiwanoffshore windwind turbines
So what: Localization mandates without domestic scale are a template for how industrial policy can inflate costs and kill the very projects they aim to build.
“made in Taiwan components ended up costing sometimes two three times the components um made outside of Taiwan dragged the whole uh profitability picture down.” ▶ 22:21
An EU challenge at the WTO filed in July 2024, settled by Taiwan in November 2024, forced Round 3.3 to drop localization requirements entirely, stranding developers who won 3.1/3.2 under now-impossible commitments the government admits cannot be met.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highEUWTOTaiwanoffshore wind
So what: Policy whiplash traps earlier winners in unfulfillable contracts while later rounds get better terms, poisoning developer trust and freezing buildout.
“round 3.3 would drop localization requirements entirely, prompted by an EU challenge at the World Trade Organization filed in July 2024.” ▶ 23:49
Major international developers exited Taiwan: RWE announced its withdrawal in November 2023 and JERA sold its Formosa 3 stake to Corio and TotalEnergies in 2023, leaving Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners as a key remaining developer still progressing on 3.1/3.2 but with no turbines yet generating power.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Jason Funconviction: highRWEJERAFormosa 3CorioTotalEnergiesCopenhagen Infrastructure Partners
So what: The exodus of blue-chip developers signals to capital that Taiwan offshore wind carries policy and execution risk few will underwrite.
“The German utility RWE announced in November 2023 that it was pulling out of Taiwan. Jera, the Japanese giant, sold its stake in Formosa 3 to Coro and Total Energies in 2023.” ▶ 25:13
The flagship 640 MW Yunlin wind farm reached full operation only in early 2025 after years of delays, and its 70 billion NTD financing (signed 2019 with 19 banks) fell into emergency restructuring, forcing sponsors to inject roughly three times their original equity.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highYunlinoffshore windTaiwan
So what: Triple equity overruns on the flagship project quantify how badly Taiwan wind economics broke, warning off future project finance.
“saw its 70 billion new Taiwan dollar financing deal, originally signed in 2019 with 19 banks, fall into emergency restructuring. sponsors had to put in roughly three times their original equity commitments” ▶ 25:37
By late 2024 Taiwan had installed only 2.8 GW of offshore wind against an original 5.7 GW target, as localization costs compounded post-Ukraine commodity spikes and rising interest rates that broke wind projects globally.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Angelicaconviction: highTaiwanoffshore wind
So what: The ~50% shortfall against target shows how far behind schedule Taiwan's flagship renewable is, deepening reliance on imported gas.
“by late 2024, Taiwan had installed just 2.8 8 g of offshore wind, far below its original 5.7 gawatt target.” ▶ 27:49
Taiwan blocked Chinese firms from its offshore wind industry but then favored made-in-Taiwan components over parts from democratic partners like Europe and Japan, forgoing a chance to give allied developers scale against Chinese overcapacity.
contrarian🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: mediumTaiwanChinaEuropeJapanoffshore wind
So what: Protectionism aimed at building a local supply chain squandered a strategic opening to deepen allied clean-energy supply chains excluding China.
“the DPP government privileged made in Taiwan components over parts made by democratic partners, and these projects have been phenomenally expensive.” ▶ 27:40
Offshore wind advocates argue the shortfall reflects rapid demand growth outpacing infrastructure, not technology failure, noting renewables' share tripled from 4.2% (2015) to 12.7% and 2024 actual generation of 288.9 billion kWh exceeded the 257.5 billion kWh projection by 31.4 billion kWh.
contrarian🗣 Tang Luconviction: mediumTaiwanrenewables
So what: If demand growth, not tech, is the constraint, Taiwan's renewable buildout may be less of a dead end than headlines suggest, but the gap keeps widening.
“there is an increase of 31.4 billion kilows over the original estimates equivalent uh to the annual operator of actually 4.5 new gas fire units which is really big.” ▶ 28:39
Corruption & Permitting Dysfunction 6
Solar developers split large projects into smaller ones to dodge a 2020 ban on converting small farmland to solar; the Lyang Energy case indicted 15 people including its chairman and an Energy Bureau solar director over forged documents, with estimated illegal profits of 9.1 billion NTD (~300 million USD).
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highLyang EnergyTaiwansolarMinistry of Agriculture
So what: Systemic circumvention of land rules has fused renewables with corruption in public perception, poisoning the political license to build.
“The estimated illegal profits ran to 9.1 billion New Taiwan dollars, close to 300 million US. And that's just one company.” ▶ 30:20
Local officials extract bribes for solar permits: Kohou Township's former mayor Lin Huling demanded a bribe from Poseidon Solar (negotiated from 8 million to 4 million NTD, ~125,000 USD) to expedite permits and eliminate protests; permits cleared in two weeks, he got 10 years, and the project became a deserted wasteland.
anecdote🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highPoseidon Solar EnergyYunlin CountysolarTaiwan
So what: Local extortion (the 'green energy cockroaches') is a direct tax on renewable developers and abandons scarce sited land.
“The original ask was an $8 million new Taiwan bribe negotiated down to $4 million new Taiwan... in exchange for expediting permits and quote eliminating public protests.” ▶ 38:26
Chung Yao-lin, Tsai Ing-wen's young point-person for offshore wind (dubbed 'little Ing-wen' and Tsai's 'renewable whisperer'), was detained August 2025 and indicted December 26, 2025 on bribery/money laundering charges over 1.98 million NTD in bribes and 6 million NTD in unexplained assets, with prosecutors seeking 14 years.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highChung Yao-linTsai Ing-wenTai PowerTungwayoffshore wind
So what: Prosecution of the wind program's architect signals both entrenched corruption and a political purge that chills further development.
“He was basically the government's point person for the entire offshore wind industry under Tying Wen... Prosecutors are seeking 14 years.” ▶ 39:07
A source argues President Lai's anti-corruption 'war on green cockroaches' is really an intra-DPP purge targeting officials tied to predecessor Tsai Ing-wen rather than reforming the permitting system, with the renewable industry as collateral damage rooted in the 2019 DPP primary rivalry.
thesis🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: mediumPresident LaiTsai Ing-wenDPPrenewables
So what: If crackdowns are factional rather than systemic, corruption incentives persist and the renewable industry keeps suffering reputational and political damage.
“President lie has declared war on the green cockroaches but critics note that he's mainly targeting officials associated with his predecessor Tyanguen not reforming the permitting system.” ▶ 40:14
Rooftop solar permitting in Taiwan averages over a year, worse than California's ~six months and far worse than Australia's ~one-to-six weeks, and officials deliberately slow-walk approvals for months out of fear of corruption-related repercussions rather than genuine technical review.
causal🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: highTaiwanCaliforniaAustraliasolar
So what: Permitting friction, worsened by anti-renewable optics, is a self-reinforcing bottleneck that no amount of subsidy fixes without process standardization.
“in Taiwan. If you want to do rooftop solar, it is well over a year. It is on average a year or maybe even over a year. So, it is even worse in California.” ▶ 43:12
Renewable corruption is often driven by relatively unseen mid-level bureaucrats rather than high officials, and anti-renewable interest groups (nimbys, nuclear or gas advocates) have weaponized and exaggerated corruption narratives to attack the sector.
thesis🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: mediumTaiwanrenewables
So what: The corruption story is partly real and partly a political weapon, so investors should discount sensationalist accounts but not dismiss governance risk.
“Often times it's the midlevel you know like bureaucrat that no one knows about that kind of um is the mastermind behind all of this” ▶ 37:20
Battery Storage Market Failure 4
Taiwan built a meaningful battery fleet but for the wrong market: because only Tai Power could sell conventional electricity and batteries couldn't prove their stored energy was purely renewable, storage operators were locked out of energy arbitrage and confined to frequency regulation, ancillary services, and reserve capacity.
causal🗣 Jason Fun / Narratorconviction: highTai PowerTaiwanbattery storage
So what: The monopoly's rules foreclosed the primary revenue model for grid batteries, misallocating capital into markets too small to sustain.
“When the battery energy storage system cannot prove that all the energy they store is from renewables, they are not allowed to do power trading in Taiwan.” ▶ 31:19
As too many battery operators piled into Taiwan's small, fixed ancillary-services and reserve markets, auction prices collapsed to zero, so essentially all Taiwanese batteries now suffer from zero-price auctions.
data_point🗣 Jason Funconviction: highTaiwanbattery storage
So what: Zero clearing prices mean stranded storage assets and no viable business case for merchant batteries under the current market design.
“When the supply exceeds the demand, the auction price will drop to zero. All the batteries in Taiwan now suffer from this zero price thing.” ▶ 32:22
Arbitrage is a non-starter because Tai Power charges the same price at peak and overnight for 98% of households and only 6-7 NTD/kWh more at peak for industrial users, so without hourly price swings batteries cannot profit and non-intermittent gas cannot be replaced by renewables.
causal🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTai PowerTaiwanbattery storageLNG
So what: Flat pricing structurally blocks storage-enabled renewables integration, locking in gas dependence and perpetual Tai Power bailouts.
“Tai Power charges the same price for electricity consumed at peak times versus in the middle of the night for 98% of households, which makes arbitrage a non-starter.” ▶ 32:47
The only battery economics that work in Taiwan are behind-the-meter storage near industrial customers like semiconductor fabs, and in March 2026 the Ministry of Economic Affairs announced a subsidy up to 5 million NTD per MWh for industrial behind-the-meter storage, with Chinese-made batteries excluded.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highMinistry of Economic AffairsTaiwansemiconductorsbattery storageChina
So what: Policymakers are subsidizing private bill-management rather than building a merchant storage market, signaling where non-Chinese battery vendors can find demand.
“the Ministry of Economic Affairs announced a subsidy of up to 5 million NTD per megawatt hour for industrial users installing behind the meter storage.” ▶ 35:07
Grid Constraints on TSMC & AI Data Centers 4
Only about 16% of Taiwan's electricity comes from domestic renewable or nuclear power while TSMC alone consumes 8-10% of the grid, leaving ~84% dependent on seaborne fossil imports.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Angelicaconviction: highTSMCTaiwanrenewables
So what: TSMC's outsized single-company power draw against a mostly imported grid concentrates both economic and geopolitical risk in one point of failure.
“Only about 16% of Taiwan's electricity comes from domestic renewable or nuclear power. And TSMC alone consumes 8 to 10% of Taiwan's electricity.” ▶ 12:37
Taiwan's grid is so tight that you cannot site even a 5 MW data center in northern Taiwan (the industrial heartland), and an 80 MW project in the south had to be split into four ~20 MW phases across different townships due to power shortages.
anecdote🗣 Angelicaconviction: highTaiwanAI data centersGoogleAmazon
So what: Power scarcity is actively capping Taiwan's ability to capture hyperscaler data-center investment, leaving money on the table for competitors in Japan/US.
“the fact that you can't even site a five megawatt data center in North Taiwan, which is the industrial heartland, is pathetic.” ▶ 47:25
Hyperscalers like Google and Amazon would build extensive data centers in Taiwan given its central, ideal conditions but are constrained purely by electricity, and the chip sector itself is similarly power-constrained.
thesis🗣 Angelicaconviction: highGoogleAmazonTaiwanTSMCAI data centers
So what: Electricity, not talent or geography, is now the gating factor for Taiwan's AI/compute economy, making grid buildout an economic-growth imperative.
“Google, Amazon, all these tech giants, they would love to build infinite data centers in Taiwan if they could... but they are constrained by electricity.” ▶ 48:09
Taiwan has no market or pricing mechanism to curb electricity demand growth, so when chip manufacturers demand more power the government's reflex is always to add supply rather than deploy efficiency or demand-response, leaving low-hanging efficiency gains untapped because prices are kept dirt cheap.
framework🗣 Yu-Suconviction: mediumTaiwanTai Powersemiconductors
So what: Demand-side reform (peak pricing, efficiency) may be a more feasible lever than supply expansion, but flat cheap prices remove any incentive to pursue it.
“every time there's an expansion need from the especially the chip manufacturing industry... the government's first respond is to come up with more but they really don't have this way to use any for example efficiency measure.” ▶ 59:49
Nuclear Reversal & Political Economy 5
On March 22, 2026, President Lai announced that decommissioned plants 2 (Guosheng) and 3 (Maanshan) meet conditions to restart, citing AI-driven demand and decarbonization, with Maanshan potentially restarting by end of 2028, a stunning reversal less than a year after Taiwan achieved its 'non-nuclear homeland' goal.
prediction🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highPresident LaiTai PowerNuclear Safety CommissionTaiwan
So what: A DPP president reversing 40 years of anti-nuclear branding marks a structural policy shift that could add base-load supply and reshape the energy debate.
“Lie publicly announced that two decommissioned nuclear plants, plant number two, Guang and plant number three, Maans, meet conditions to restart.” ▶ 49:27
Restarting the two reactors would cover only about 10% of Taiwan's current electricity needs, but a single reactor's ~6% already exceeds every solar panel on the island combined, and completing the never-finished fourth plant could raise nuclear's share to 16-18%.
data_point🗣 Angelicaconviction: mediumTaiwannuclearsolarLongmen
So what: Nuclear offers concentrated, dispatchable output that dwarfs current solar, but two restarts alone are useful rather than transformative for the supply gap.
“the 6% from one reactor is already more electricity than every solar panel on the island combined.” ▶ 51:01
The never-completed Longmen (fourth) plant is an advanced boiling water reactor designed by GE with reactor units from Hitachi and Toshiba, turbines from Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, and construction by Shimizu, but was mothballed in 2014 and permanently blocked by a 2021 referendum after opponents smeared it as a dangerous multi-supplier 'Franken reactor.'
data_point🗣 Narrator / Angelicaconviction: highLongmenGEHitachiToshibaMitsubishi Heavy IndustriesShimizu
So what: A finished large ABWR asset sits idle; reviving it is technically the biggest lever but politically the hardest given the entrenched smear narrative.
“Long was supposed to be the next generation reactor, an advanced boiling water reactor designed by GE with Hitachi and Toshiba building the actual reactor units.” ▶ 51:24
By unilaterally reversing the nuclear shutdown, President Lai paradoxically removed his own negotiating leverage, since the opposition now knows he actually favors nuclear, so pro-nuclear carrots are no longer enough to trade for renewable reforms in a nuclear-for-renewables quid pro quo.
framework🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: mediumPresident LaiDPPKMTTPP
So what: The political-economy deadlock means an 'all of the above' grand bargain remains unlikely, so reforms will stay incremental and crisis-driven.
“by reversing the nuclear shutdown unilaterally, lie has paradoxically removed his own leverage.” ▶ 56:18
Taiwan's energy debate posits nuclear and renewables as mutually exclusive rival teams (DPP pro-renewables/anti-nuclear; KMT and TPP pro-nuclear but increasingly anti-renewables), so climate pressure fails to drive cross-party cooperation and the 'all of the above' constituency is unrepresented in the legislature.
framework🗣 Yu-Su / Narratorconviction: highDPPKMTTPPTaiwan
So what: Polarization structurally blocks the pragmatic energy mix Taiwan needs, so gridlock, not technology, is the core investment risk.
“nuclear and renewables are on opposite teams and posited as mutually exclusive energy paths instead of compliments.” ▶ 8:23
Geopolitical & Investment Signals 6
In February 2026 Taiwan signed a reciprocal trade agreement with the Trump administration committing to over 40 billion USD in US LNG and crude oil purchases through 2029, which will raise US LNG to roughly one-third of Taiwan's total supply.
data_point🗣 Narrator (Lily Ottinger)conviction: highTaiwanUnited StatesLNGTrump administration
So what: Taiwan is diversifying LNG toward the US for geopolitical alignment, creating a durable multi-billion-dollar US energy export flow but deepening overall gas dependence.
“Taiwan signed a reciprocal trade agreement with the Trump administration, committing to over $40 billion in US LG and crude oil purchases through 2029.” ▶ 1:03:05
The US government's priority in energy cooperation with Taiwan is selling LNG and oil rather than transferring clean-energy technology, though Taiwan could benefit from applying fracking-derived techniques to geothermal as companies like Fervo are doing.
thesis🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: mediumUnited StatesTaiwanFervogeothermalLNG
So what: Expect US-Taiwan energy ties to center on fossil exports; geothermal/tech collaboration is a secondary, uncertain opportunity for allied firms.
“the primary priority of the US government or the current administration is to sell Taiwan more LG or more you know like oil.” ▶ 1:02:26
A proposed 'Pax Electricana' would offshore Taiwan's most energy-intensive heavy industry, having partners like Australia perform the iron-making step (which consumes ~85% of steelmaking energy) using cheap clean power before shipping semi-processed iron to Taiwan.
thesis🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: exploratoryAustraliaTaiwansteelJapanSouth KoreaCanadaChileMexico
So what: Energy-arbitrage offshoring of heavy industry is a speculative structural idea, but carries obvious risk of moving strategic supply offshore amid invasion threats.
“The iron making step actually accounts for uh 85% of the energy consumption of the entire process.” ▶ 58:01
Taiwan's energy failure risks a major electoral backlash against the DPP in this year's local elections and the 2028 presidential race driven by competence frustration rather than cross-strait sentiment, potentially reviving the pro-Beijing-leaning KMT despite public dislike of its China ties.
prediction🗣 Angelicaconviction: mediumDPPKMTTaiwan
So what: Energy poverty is a live domestic political risk that could shift Taiwan's government and, by extension, its cross-strait posture, a geopolitical wildcard for investors.
“there's a level of sick and tired with the DPP in Taiwan that's got nothing to do with crossstrait relations. It's got to do with their incompetence.” ▶ 1:05:12
Taiwan ranks second globally (after the Netherlands) in rooftop solar density on flat land despite solar being under 10% of its electricity mix, meaning much of the easiest solar potential has already been exhausted.
data_point🗣 Ricky Huangconviction: mediumTaiwanNetherlandssolarTaiwan Climate Action Network
So what: High existing rooftop density means future solar growth will be harder and costlier, capping how much more distributed solar can offset import dependence.
“Taiwan has higher rooftop solar density than any country we typically associate with high solar capacity, but it's actually number two globally after the Netherlands.” ▶ 46:05
The core lesson framed for the West is that Taiwan's renewable failure is a case study in the ugly side of industrial policy, where overpromising aggressive targets combined with excessive money relative to project pipeline made corruption an inevitable product.
framework🗣 Angelicaconviction: highTaiwanrenewablesDPP
So what: Investors and policymakers should read Taiwan as a cautionary template: aggressive green targets plus local-content mandates plus subsidy floods breed cost overruns and graft.
“when the amount of money is excessive to the amount of pipeline and bandwidth, um, corruption is just the inevitable product of that scenario.” ▶ 20:35

Bloomberg Technology 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

Rocket Lab Targets SpaceX's Starlink Dominance in New Deal | Bloomberg Tech 6/29/202644 min40 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Rocket Lab CEO Peter Beck details a roughly $8B cash-and-stock acquisition of Iridium ($54/share) to become a vertically integrated, self-launching space applications company challenging SpaceX/Starlink via Iridium's unique defense-grade spectrum and global direct-to-device network. Surrounding segments cover a record 2026 IPO/fundraising boom led by SpaceX and Alphabet, South Korea's massive chip/AI capex, and US export-control drama around Anthropic's Mythos Five model.
Rocket Lab-Iridium: Vertical Integration Thesis · 7Spectrum, Constellation Refresh & SpaceX Competitive Dynamics · 7AI Trade Sustainability & Capex-vs-ROI Divide · 6South Korea Chip & AI Capex Megabuild · 3Record IPO & Fundraising Boom · 5AI Export Controls & Anthropic Model Access · 4Open-Source AI & Robotics (Hugging Face) · 4Media M&A & Corporate AI Adoption · 4
Rocket Lab-Iridium: Vertical Integration Thesis 7
Rocket Lab is acquiring Iridium at $54/share in a cash-and-stock deal valuing it at roughly $8 billion enterprise value to add a satellite communications applications business on top of its launch and spacecraft manufacturing legs.
data_point🗣 Ed Ludlowconviction: highRocket LabIridiumSpaceXStarlink
So what: Sets the deal terms and valuation anchor for evaluating Rocket Lab's move into the applications layer.
“$54 A SHARE IN A CASH AND STOCK TRANSACTION, VALUING IT AN ENTERPRISE VALUE OF ABOUT $8 BILLION.” ▶ 1:07
Beck argues the winning large space companies of the future will blur the line between launch, manufacturing, and applications, and that combining Rocket Lab and Iridium creates a rare self-launching company that owns rockets, builds its own satellites, and runs applications.
thesis🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highRocket LabIridium
So what: Frames the strategic rationale: full-stack vertical integration is Rocket Lab's moat versus point-solution rivals.
“THE LARGE SPACE COMPANIES OF THE FUTURE ARE GOING TO BE BLURRY ABOUT WHAT A SPACE COMPANY IS” ▶ 1:44
Owning the two most expensive, longest-lead-time inputs (launch and spacecraft manufacturing) lets Rocket Lab collapse those costs internally, giving it superior ability to innovate and execute new business plans versus competitors who must buy those inputs.
causal🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highRocket LabIridium
So what: The economic edge is cost internalization on capex-heavy items, not just strategic optionality.
“INSTEAD OF SCRATCHING OUT BUSINESS PLANS ON A WHITEBOARD WE SPEND BILLIONS OF DOLLARS ON SPACECRAFT MANUFACTURING AND THOSE COSTS EVAPORATE” ▶ 5:01
Beck characterizes the deal as buying an established, profitable business with valuable spectrum rather than a speculative greenfield build, avoiding the multi-year gap between building constellations and seeing first-dollar revenue.
thesis🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highRocket LabIridium
So what: Positions the acquisition as de-risked cash-generative entry into applications rather than a moonshot.
“WE ARE NOT ACQUIRING A FIELD OF DREAMS AND STARTING FROM SCRATCH. WE ARE ACQUIRING A VALUABLE SPECTRUM AND PROFITABLE BUSINESS” ▶ 7:27
Beck signals Rocket Lab intends to keep growing and doing more acquisitions after Iridium, calling this only its first foray into applications.
prediction🗣 Peter Beckconviction: mediumRocket LabIridium
So what: Investors should expect further M&A, not a one-off, as Rocket Lab builds out its applications layer.
“OUR INTENTION IS TO NOT STOP WITH HIS ACQUISITION AND CONTINUE TO GROW FOR SURE” ▶ 7:37
Beck says the deal will be financed initially with a debt bridge that Rocket Lab intends to take out, noting Iridium carries some existing debt but throws off a lot of cash.
data_point🗣 Peter Beckconviction: mediumRocket LabIridium
So what: Flags near-term leverage and refinancing risk that could pressure Rocket Lab's balance sheet.
“IRIDIUM HAS HAD SOME DEBT AND IT IS THROWING OFF A LOT OF CASH BUT WE HAVE A DEBT BRIDGE WE INTEND TO TAKE OUT.” ▶ 5:31
Beck expects the acquisition to clear regulators easily, calling it straightforward and not controversial.
prediction🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highRocket LabIridium
So what: Low regulatory-close risk supports a tighter merger-arb spread on Iridium shares.
“FROM A REGULATORY STANDPOINT, THIS IS STRAIGHTFORWARD. IT IS A STRONG DEAL AND NOT CONTROVERSIAL AT ALL.” ▶ 9:26
Spectrum, Constellation Refresh & SpaceX Competitive Dynamics 7
Beck contends spectrum is the decisive asset in satellite communications: you can build all the spacecraft you want, but without spectrum to utilize them for communications it is worthless.
framework🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highIridiumRocket Lab
So what: Reframes the deal's core value as Iridium's scarce spectrum rights, not its hardware.
“YOU CAN [BUILD] ALL THE SPACECRAFT YOU WANT BUT IF YOU DO NOT HAVE THE SPECTRUM TO UTILIZE THEM ESPECIALLY FOR COMMUNICATIONS, IT IS ALL FOR NOTHING” ▶ 2:44
Rather than compete head-on with Starlink on bandwidth, speed, and latency, Rocket Lab is targeting a niche defined by Iridium's spectrum being uniquely effective in harsh conditions and its fully global coverage for mariners, pilots, and defense forces.
contrarian🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highIridiumStarlinkSpaceXRocket Lab
So what: Rocket Lab is not fighting Starlink on consumer broadband but defending a resilient, global, defense-grade communications niche.
“IT IS COMPLETELY GLOBAL. SO NO MATTER WHERE YOU GO ON THE PLANET, WHEREVER, YOU ARE CONNECTED THAT IS SOMETHING THAT ONLY IRIDIUM HAS.” ▶ 8:58
Ed Ludlow notes the market consensus that Starlink leads on bandwidth, speed, and latency, framing Rocket Lab as needing to define a narrower total addressable market rather than chasing the whole opportunity.
contrarian🗣 Ed Ludlowconviction: mediumStarlinkSpaceXRocket Lab
So what: Acknowledges Starlink's technical lead, implying Rocket Lab must win on differentiation, not raw performance.
“A LOT OF FOLKS ARE POINTED AT THE IDEA THAT STARLINK HAS A LEAD IN TERMS OF BANDWIDTH, SPEED, LATENCY” ▶ 7:48
Iridium's direct-to-device system is rolling out this year, giving Rocket Lab an immediate application offering to compete against SpaceX and Amazon in direct-to-device connectivity.
data_point🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highIridiumSpaceXAmazonRocket Lab
So what: Direct-to-device is the near-term product wedge, and it is already launching rather than years away.
“IRIDIUM HAS A DIRECT TO DEVICE SYSTEM THAT THEY ARE ROLLING OUT THIS YEAR AND THIS IS THE BEGINNING FOR US.” ▶ 7:10
Beck says launch is the scarce Achilles' heel of space applications, and Rocket Lab's control of Electron (second-most-frequently launched rocket) plus Neutron coming online by year-end de-risks constellation development.
causal🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highRocket LabElectronNeutronIridium
So what: Owning launch capacity is the differentiator that lets Rocket Lab experiment and refresh constellations on demand.
“THE ACHILLES' HEEL IN SPACE APPLICATIONS AND INFRASTRUCTURE NOW IS LAUNCH. LAUNCH IS A VALUABLE AND RARE ASSET” ▶ 6:06
The Iridium constellation is relatively fresh with several years of life left, so it does not require immediate launch, giving Rocket Lab time to refresh it while using Electron and Neutron to experiment with new technologies almost immediately.
data_point🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highIridiumElectronNeutronRocket Lab
So what: No urgent replacement capex is required, reducing near-term cash strain from the deal.
“THE IRIDIUM CONSTELLATION IS A RELATIVELY FRESH CONSTELLATION SO DOES NOT REQUIRE IMMEDIATE LAUNCH.” ▶ 6:26
Rocket Lab's Electron just set a world record for fastest call-up-to-launch at 16 hours and 42 minutes, and Neutron is slated to come online by the end of the year.
data_point🗣 Peter Beckconviction: highElectronNeutronRocket Lab
So what: Responsive-launch capability is a tangible operational edge for defense and rapid-deployment customers.
“JUST BROKE THE WORLD RECORD FOR THE FASTEST TIME THAT SOMEONE CALLS UP A ROCKET LAUNCHES IN 16 HOURS, 42 MINUTES” ▶ 9:49
AI Trade Sustainability & Capex-vs-ROI Divide 6
A co-CIO guest argues markets are rewarding AI recipients while penalizing those funding the buildout, and this dynamic is unsustainable unless the investments generate real value over a 3-to-10-year horizon.
thesis🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumAIcapex
So what: Frames the AI trade's durability as a question of eventual ROI, with a widening gap between spenders and beneficiaries.
“THERE WILL BE A POINT WHERE IT HAS TO DELIVER RETURNS AND WE ARE AT THE POINT WHERE YOU ARE STARTING TO SEE THAT ACROSS THE CORPORATE WORLD” ▶ 13:55
Emerging-market equities, historically uncorrelated, have become beneficiaries of the AI trade and are now linked to global tech sectors, a shift the guest says is changing EM behavior this year.
causal🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumemerging marketsKOSPIAI
So what: EM diversification benefits are eroding as EM equities increasingly track the AI/tech complex.
“HISTORICALLY EMERGING-MARKET EQUITIES HAD BEEN UNCORRELATED. THAT'S GOING TO CHANGE FOR THIS YEAR.” ▶ 14:32
Despite large gains, the guest says tech valuations remain fairly attractive because earnings growth has been so strong, combining momentum with quantitative factors to keep a case for staying in tech stocks like Meta and Alphabet.
thesis🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumMetaAlphabettech stocks
So what: Earnings growth, not just multiple expansion, underpins the bull case, tempering bubble fears.
“EVEN THOUGH WE HAVE HAD A LOT OF GROWTH, THE VALUATIONS ARE STILL FAIRLY ATTRACTIVE BECAUSE EARNINGS HAVE BEEN SO STRONG.” ▶ 14:54
The guest argues the value-versus-growth distinction has broken down because tech (Micron, Intel) now sits high in value indices like the Russell 1000 Value, having delivered both price and earnings growth.
framework🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumMicronIntelRussell 1000 Value
So what: Style-box investing is less useful when tech dominates both value and growth buckets.
“THOSE DISTINCTIONS BETWEEN VALUE AND GROWTH DON'T WORK SO WELL ANYMORE” ▶ 15:43
The persistent valuation discount of US versus non-US equities is partly attributable to corporate governance gaps in nations like Korea and Japan, which the guest says have been improving dramatically.
causal🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumKoreaJapancorporate governance
So what: Governance reform in Korea/Japan is a potential re-rating catalyst narrowing the US valuation gap.
“SOME OF IT WILL BE LINKED TO CORPORATE GOVERNANCE, WHERE THERE IS STILL MORE THAT CAN BE DONE IN SOME OF THOSE NATIONS LIKE KOREA AND JAPAN” ▶ 15:58
The guest says AI-related bond-market funding is currently healthy and not overstretched because many issuers have solid long-term cash flow, and bond markets can give a truer outlook picture than hype-driven equity markets.
contrarian🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumbond marketSpaceXNvidiaAI
So what: Credit markets are not yet flashing the warning signs that equity valuations might imply.
“RIGHT NOW THE AMOUNT OF FUNDING AND BOND MARKETS IS HEALTHY AND BY NO MEANS OVERSTRETCHED.” ▶ 17:47
South Korea Chip & AI Capex Megabuild 3
South Korea laid out plans including SK Hynix building four chip fabs in a roughly $518 billion project in the southwest, aiming to double production capability over the next five years.
data_point🗣 Avril Hongconviction: highSouth KoreaSK Hynix
So what: Signals a step-change in Korean fab capacity that could reshape global memory/chip supply.
“SK HYNIX IS SET TO BUILD FOUR CHIP FABS IN A ROUGHLY $518 BILLION PROJECT IN THE SOUTHWEST.” ▶ 11:24
Korea plans to spend about $650 billion on AI data centers by 2035, with regional specialization by technology and industry to spread AI-boom benefits more evenly across the country.
data_point🗣 Avril Hongconviction: highSouth KoreaAI data centers
So what: Massive state-backed data-center demand supports sustained chip and infrastructure orders.
“PLANS TO SPEND ABOUT $650 BILLION IN AI DATA CENTERS BY 2035” ▶ 11:42
Korea's investment figures are more or less in line with what SK Hynix and Samsung have already committed for 2027, framed with urgency to move faster than rivals as competition pressures profit sustainability.
causal🗣 Avril Hongconviction: mediumSouth KoreaSK HynixSamsung
So what: The plan formalizes existing corporate capex into national strategy amid margin-pressure concerns.
“YOU COULD ALMOST HEAR THE SENSE OF URGENCY FOR KOREA TO MAINTAIN A COMPETITIVE EDGE.” ▶ 12:16
Record IPO & Fundraising Boom 5
A wave of AI-buildout-tied fundraising has pushed US IPOs and share sales to a record roughly $251 billion in 2026 so far, beating 2021, led by blockbuster offerings from SpaceX and Alphabet.
data_point🗣 Bailey Lipschultzconviction: highSpaceXAlphabetIPO market
So what: Establishes 2026 as an unprecedented equity-issuance year, driven by AI capital demand.
“NORTH OF 251 BILLION DOLLARS RAISED... WE ARE LOOKING AT NUMBERS WE HAVE NEVER SEEN BEATING 2021.” ▶ 24:56
The $251 billion figure understates true issuance because Alphabet's raise was an ATM (at-the-market) offering not fully captured, implying the real number is larger.
data_point🗣 Bailey Lipschultzconviction: mediumAlphabetIPO market
So what: Headline issuance records understate the actual scale of capital being pulled into the market.
≈ “THAT DOES NOT FULLY ENCAPSULATE THAT GOOGLE RAISE BECAUSE IT IS AN ATM... THAT NUMBER SHOULD BE LARGER.” paraphrase
SK Hynix is expected in coming weeks to potentially do a roughly $30 billion US fundraise, which would be the second-largest IPO ever and kick off a strong July.
prediction🗣 Bailey Lipschultzconviction: mediumSK HynixIPO market
So what: A near-term $30B listing would extend the issuance record and test market appetite.
“SK HYNIX EXPECTED IN THE COMING WEEKS TO POTENTIALLY BE $30 BILLION FUND RAISE HERE IN THE U.S.” ▶ 25:14
Bankers expect the third quarter to be critical for issuance, with Anthropic potentially coming to market in the fall and a possible pivot by hyperscalers from debt toward equity to fund the AI/data-center buildout.
prediction🗣 Bailey Lipschultzconviction: mediumAnthropichyperscalersIPO market
So what: A shift from debt to equity funding among hyperscalers could pressure the broader equity supply.
“THEY EXPECT THE THIRD QUARTER TO BE CRITICAL AS WE GET TOWARD POTENTIALLY ANTHROPIC COMING IN THE FALL.” ▶ 27:11
The pipeline of capital waiting for IPOs is so large that if it all comes through it would be one of the biggest years ever, but investors may sell existing holdings to make room, creating downward pressure on markets.
causal🗣 Co-Chief Investment Officer (Seven IM)conviction: mediumIPO marketprivate markets
So what: Heavy new supply can rotate capital out of existing positions, a technical headwind for current holdings.
“THAT'S A BIT OF A DOWNWARD PRESSURE ON MARKETS AND IT KEEPS IN MIND THAT INVESTORS WILL SCRUTINIZE SOME OF THESE DEALS.” ▶ 17:03
AI Export Controls & Anthropic Model Access 4
Anthropic won US approval to restore limited access to its Mythos Five model after the government abruptly barred foreign-national access over cybersecurity concerns and Anthropic responded by disabling all access; Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's letter said the model could go to certain trusted partners while the Fable Five model was not mentioned.
data_point🗣 Ed Ludlowconviction: highAnthropicMythos FiveFable FiveHoward LutnickUS Commerce Department
So what: Frontier-model access is now subject to case-by-case export-control negotiation, a new operational risk for AI labs.
“COMMERCE SECRETARY HOWARD LUTNICK WROTE THE MODEL COULD BE RELEASED TO CERTAIN TRUSTED PARTNERS. THE FABLE FIVE MODEL WAS NOT MENTIONED.” ▶ 28:22
Payton says no more than about 100 federal departments, agencies, and private-sector companies will get access to the model without foreign-employee constraints, with more expected to come.
data_point🗣 Teresa Paytonconviction: mediumAnthropicMythos FiveUS government
So what: Defines a narrow, curated approved-user set, limiting the model's near-term commercial reach.
“THERE WILL NOT BE MORE THAN 100... FEDERAL DEPARTMENTS AND AGENCIES AND PRIVATE SECTOR COMPANIES WHICH WILL HAVE ACCESS TO THIS MODEL” ▶ 29:20
Payton warns that without a published, certain, and fair export-control framework, markets and global partners will react to uncertainty by demanding their own domestic and open-source models installed within their countries.
causal🗣 Teresa Paytonconviction: highUS governmentAI regulationopen-source
So what: Regulatory uncertainty accelerates sovereign-AI fragmentation and erodes US frontier-lab reach abroad.
“YOU'RE STARTING TO SEE GLOBAL PARTNERS SAY WE NEED OUR OWN MODELS, WE NEED TO INSTALL OPEN-SOURCE MODELS HERE WITHIN OUR COUNTRY” ▶ 31:50
Payton notes OpenAI was also recently told to limit model access, and that capable open-source (sometimes free) models from China and elsewhere are narrowing the gap, so US leadership depends on getting breakthroughs to users as fast as possible.
contrarian🗣 Teresa Paytonconviction: mediumOpenAIChinaopen-sourceAnthropic
So what: Overly tight controls risk ceding ground to fast-improving open-source and Chinese models.
“THE REST OF THE WORLD, INCLUDING CHINA, IS INVESTING AGGRESSIVELY AND THERE ARE CAPABLE OPEN-SOURCE, IN SOME CASES FREE MODELS NARROWING THE GAP” ▶ 30:30
Open-Source AI & Robotics (Hugging Face) 4
Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue argues frontier labs' being labeled too dangerous is fair given their years of such marketing (citing GPT-2 deemed too dangerous 5-6 years ago), and these fast-growing giants can absorb regulation, but constraints should not be spread to startups, academia, and small players who lack the same legal and policy capabilities.
thesis🗣 Clem Delangueconviction: highHugging FaceGPT-2frontier labsopen-source
So what: Advocates a bifurcated regulatory regime that spares the open-source ecosystem from frontier-lab rules.
“ONE THING WE NEED TO BE CAREFUL OF IS TO SPREAD THESE CONSTRAINTS AND REGULATIONS TO THE REST OF THE ECOSYSTEM, LIKE STARTUPS” ▶ 35:11
Delangue says the most dangerous capabilities are concentrated in a few frontier labs behind closed doors, while open-source models are mostly smaller, specialized, more transparent (easily evaluable versus opaque APIs), and broadly beneficial, justifying different treatment.
framework🗣 Clem Delangueconviction: highHugging Faceopen-sourcefrontier labs
So what: Transparency and diffuse provenance are the core arguments for lighter open-source regulation.
“OPEN-SOURCE IS AN ECOSYSTEM OF SMALLER COMPANIES... VERSUS FRONTIER LABS IS A COUPLE COMPANIES THAT ARE CONCENTRATING A LOT OF POWER BEHIND CLOSED DOORS.” ▶ 37:46
Delangue says the open-source ecosystem is booming, with a million new models and datasets introduced on Hugging Face in the past quarter and rising usage by American startups and small companies, enabling firms to own AI rather than rent it via APIs.
data_point🗣 Clem Delangueconviction: highHugging Faceopen-sourceneo-cloud
So what: Quantifies open-source momentum as a durable competitive alternative to closed API models.
“A MILLION NEW MODELS IN DATA SETS HAVE BEEN INTRODUCED IN HUGGING FACE IN JUST THE PAST QUARTER.” ▶ 38:35
Delangue argues open-source transparency matters even more in robotics/physical AI, and Hugging Face has shipped over 10,000 units of its robot worldwide in the past few months as evidence of demand for open, transparent robotics solutions.
data_point🗣 Clem Delangueconviction: highHugging Faceroboticsphysical AINvidia
So what: Open-source is positioned as the trust-and-control model for consumer/physical AI, with early shipment traction.
“WE HAVE SHIPPED OVER 10,000 OF THEM AROUND THE WORLD OVER THE PAST FEW MONTHS” ▶ 40:31
Media M&A & Corporate AI Adoption 4
Comcast plans to split into two public companies, spinning off NBCUniversal and Sky as a standalone media entity while Comcast doubles down on broadband, wireless, and cable, a move analysts see as setting the stage for more cable/media M&A.
data_point🗣 Ed Ludlowconviction: highComcastNBCUniversalSky
So what: The separation creates cleaner M&A targets and could catalyze further consolidation in cable/media.
“COMCAST IS GOING TO SPIN OFF NBC UNIVERSAL AND SKY, BASICALLY SEPARATING INTO TWO SEPARATE PUBLIC COMPANIES.” ▶ 23:05
Microsoft is set to shed about $570 billion in market value in June, down as much as 20% at one point, on track for its worst monthly performance in 26 years.
data_point🗣 Ed Ludlowconviction: highMicrosoft
So what: Even mega-cap AI leaders are seeing severe drawdowns, signaling rotation or repricing within the AI trade.
“$570 BILLION DOLLARS IS THE AMOUNT OF MARKET VALUE MICROSOFT IS SET TO SHED IN THE MONTH OF JUNE” ▶ 23:53
Bob Iger and Joshua Kushner (via Thrive/Thrive Eternal) are discussing a bid for a majority stake in an NBA expansion team in Las Vegas, extending Thrive's push into iconic franchises and cultural assets after an earlier smaller investment in the Giants.
anecdote🗣 Natosha (Bloomberg reporter)conviction: mediumBob IgerJoshua KushnerThrive CapitalNBA
So what: Signals continued institutional/VC capital flowing into sports franchises as investable cultural assets.
“THE WHOLE IDEA BEHIND THRIVE ETERNAL IS THEY WANT TO INVEST IN ICONIC FRANCHISES, CULTURAL ASSETS” ▶ 20:47
AI notetakers are proliferating in corporate meetings, creating privacy, security, legal, and interpersonal-etiquette challenges as tools with hundreds of millions of users auto-join calls, sometimes without the human even present.
thesis🗣 Issie Lapowskyconviction: mediumAI notetakersZoomGoogle Meet
So what: Ubiquitous AI notetaking creates unresolved consent and compliance norms that enterprises must actively manage.
“THERE ARE LEGAL IMPLICATIONS OF HAVING EVERY SINGLE WORD RECORDED AND SHARED AND SAVED, BUT THERE ARE ALSO INTERPERSONAL CONSEQUENCES.” ▶ 42:42

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

Are The Rich Starting To Stumble? | Danielle DiMartino Booth61 min40 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Danielle DiMartino Booth argues the US already ran through a labor-market recession in 2025 masked by K-shaped averages, and the key new risk is that even top-decile earners' confidence and spending are finally cracking. She expects negative headline CPI prints, no 2026 rate hike (against consensus), a clearing commercial-real-estate/private-credit bust, and advises harvesting gains, holding cash at ~4%, and stepping back into precious metals.
Hidden Labor-Market Recession Beneath the Averages · 3Defensive Capex vs. Real Investment · 3Rising Bankruptcy Cycle and Consumer Fragility · 4The Top of the K Starts to Crack · 5Disinflation, the Fed and Kevin Warsh · 6CRE Clearing, Shadow Banking and Private Credit Risk · 7Speculative Excess and the AI Capex Cliff · 6Positioning: Harvest Gains, Cash, Gold and the Midterms · 6
Hidden Labor-Market Recession Beneath the Averages 3
The Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (the hard data underlying non-farm payrolls, covering 95%+ of US employers) showed net job losses in Q1, Q2 and Q3 of 2025, which meets and exceeds the definition of a recession.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highQuarterly Census of Employment and WagesUS labor marketnon-farm payrolls
So what: The 'resilient' headline economy masks a labor-market recession already recorded in the most reliable data.
“that from US employers told us that in the first, second, and third quarters of 2025, there were net job losses. So that that goes beyond the definition of a recession.” ▶ 3:53
The US economy lost roughly 600,000 full-time jobs on a rolling 12-month basis, with recent job creation skewed toward part-time and temporary leisure/hospitality roles tied to the World Cup.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highUS labor marketleisure and hospitalityWorld Cup
So what: Headline job gains are low-quality and temporary, overstating true labor demand.
“We have a lot of leisure and hospitality jobs created because of the World Cup. Um, but they're part-time in nature and they're temporary in nature” ▶ 4:32
Investors only ever trade off the first non-farm payroll print, so subsequent revisions to hard data are effectively irrelevant to market participants even when they reveal recessionary job losses.
framework🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highnon-farm payrollsinvestors
So what: A real economic downturn can persist in the data without moving markets, delaying repricing.
“the audience is investors are only ever going to trade off of the first non-farm payroll print. What happens subsequent to that is completely irrelevant to their day jobs.” ▶ 6:12
Defensive Capex vs. Real Investment 3
Despite bonus-depreciation incentives in the One Big Beautiful Bill, companies outside AI are spending on rebuilding and over-stocking inventory (defensively) rather than on capex/building things (offensively), which the bill was supposed to stimulate.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highOne Big Beautiful BillISMS&P Globalregional Fed manufacturing surveysAI
So what: The promised capex-driven growth boost is not showing up outside AI, undercutting bullish GDP forecasts.
“Companies are spending money, but they're spending money on inventory... They're spending it defensively versus offensively” ▶ 8:37
Companies initially deferred capex because of the Iran war and missile risk, then held off further amid threats of fresh tariffs, driving panic buying of inventory ahead of feared cost spikes rather than durable investment.
causal🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumIrantariffsinventory
So what: Geopolitical and trade uncertainty is diverting corporate cash from productive investment into precautionary stockpiling.
“Initially, they were holding off because missiles went flying and because of the Iran war.” ▶ 7:51
The One Big Beautiful Bill's promised average tax-refund boost of $1,000+ actually came in around $326 and was more than consumed by higher gas prices before those prices later fell.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highOne Big Beautiful Billtax refundsgasoline prices
So what: The fiscal boost to household spending power was far smaller than advertised and was eaten by energy costs.
“at the margin a delta of $1,000 or more on average with tax refunds. That ended up being about $326 that was more than consumed by higher gas prices” ▶ 6:41
Rising Bankruptcy Cycle and Consumer Fragility 4
Bankruptcies are running about 40% year-over-year higher and QI Research's proprietary PPI for bankruptcy attorneys looks like a hockey stick, indicating an accelerating insolvency cycle.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highQI Researchbankruptcy courts
So what: A steep rise in insolvencies signals deeper stress than headline growth suggests.
“bankruptcies running about 40% um year-over-year above where they were... it's a hockey stick.” ▶ 9:20
The corporate bankruptcy wave of 2023-2024 is now feeding a lagged rise in consumer bankruptcies, up about 10% year-over-year as failing companies lay off workers and liquidate rather than secure bridge financing.
causal🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highconsumer bankruptciescorporate bankruptcies
So what: The 'caboose' of consumer distress is now arriving, pressuring household spending over coming quarters.
“now we're seeing the caboose. So now we're seeing consumer bankruptcies are up about 10% year-over-year.” ▶ 9:47
As of July 1, all 42 million Americans with student loans (about one in six borrowers coming off forbearance in place since March 2020) become obligated to repay, creating a payment shock that is contributing to rising bankruptcies.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highstudent loansstudent loan borrowers
So what: A large, delayed debt-servicing obligation is hitting consumers just as cushions are thinning.
≈ “we finally have all 42 million Americans obligated to repay their student loans. And so there'll be this kind of kicker price shock” paraphrase
Consumers keep spending regardless, piling gigantic sums onto revolving credit with buy-now-pay-later 'humming along in the background', so credit stress alone won't stop consumption.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highconsumer creditbuy now pay laterrevolving credit
So what: Watch confidence and wealth effects, not credit usage, for the signal that spending finally rolls over.
“The American consumer never stops spending... we've seen consumer credit just go haywire.” ▶ 11:29
The Top of the K Starts to Crack 5
Within both Conference Board and University of Michigan surveys, it is the top income earners whose confidence is now falling most quickly, an inversion of the usual pattern where high earners are impervious.
contrarian🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highConference BoardUniversity of Michigantop income earners
So what: If the top leg of the K weakens, the averages that have propped up the economy could break.
“it's those top earners whose confidence is coming down the most quickly.” ▶ 0:09
Per BEA/Fed flow-of-funds decile data, only the 80-90th percentile cohort still has a small cash cushion, while nearly all remaining cash sits with the top 90-100% decile.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highBureau of Economic AnalysisFed flow of funds
So what: Spending power is extremely concentrated, so a hit to the very top has outsized macro consequences.
≈ “the 80 to 90%... they're the only cohort that has like a little bit of a cash cushion left and then all of the other cash is sitting with that 90 to 100% top desile.” paraphrase
Carnival Cruise Lines missed revenue last week, a red flag for baby-boomer spending because boomers dominate cruise demand and such a miss hasn't occurred since the pandemic.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumCarnival Cruise Linesbaby boomers
So what: A discretionary-spend miss among affluent retirees is an early tell that top-tier consumption is softening.
“Carnival Cruise Lines just told us last week that whoops, we missed our revenue. Um that to me is a red flag for baby boomers” ▶ 15:08
Americans over age 70 now own about a third of the US stock market and seniors hold their highest-ever equity exposure, so a 20-25% market correction would trigger a severe negative wealth effect that freezes their spending.
causal🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumbaby boomersUS stock marketseniors
So what: Boomer net worth propping up the economy is fragile and highly correlated to a single equity-market drawdown.
“people over the age of 70 now own a third of the US stock market” ▶ 14:49
Median retirement savings for the average boomer is a little under $250,000, far too little to last 20 years, especially while supporting adult children, so the widely cited $90 trillion in boomer net worth is extremely unevenly distributed.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highbaby boomersretirement savings
So what: The 'boomer wealth backstop' narrative overstates broad household resilience because the wealth sits with a thin top slice.
“The median retirement savings for the average boomer is like a little under 250,000... It's not going to last you 20 years.” ▶ 21:18
Disinflation, the Fed and Kevin Warsh 6
DiMartino Booth predicts headline CPI will soon print negative month-over-month, driven by falling oil, home-price declines spreading beyond the Sun Belt, services disinflation and wage growth back to 2019 levels on the Atlanta Fed tracker.
prediction🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highCPIoilAtlanta Fed wage trackershelter
So what: Imminent negative CPI prints would upend the market's rate-hike narrative and reprice rate expectations.
≈ “we're going to see negative prints on headline CPI. It's just it's going to happen.” paraphrase
She expects no Fed rate hike in 2026, explicitly betting against the market's consensus that pricing a first hike around September, and shared that other guests interviewed hold the same view.
prediction🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highFederal Reserveinterest rates
So what: A contrarian no-hike (potentially cut) call versus a hawkish market sets up a large repricing if correct.
“I don't think we're going to see a rate hike this year, Adam.” ▶ 40:33
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is deliberately killing forward guidance, cut the FOMC statement to its shortest since Greenspan, may skip press conferences, and is running Fed communications far quieter than the Powell era.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveFOMCAlan Greenspan
So what: Less Fed communication removes a key market crutch and raises volatility around data surprises.
“chopped the FOM statement in half... he even went so far as to say, we might not even have press conferences if we don't have anything to say.” ▶ 32:21
Warsh signaled at his first press conference that non-farm payroll data is useless until the third revision (the QCEW), which DiMartino Booth reads as him tacitly acknowledging the economy was in recession.
causal🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumKevin Warshnon-farm payrollsQuarterly Census of Employment and Wages
So what: The Fed chair himself may privately accept the recession thesis, shaping policy toward easing.
“he mentioned that payrolls data were only relevant on their third revision... That means he knows that the economy was in recession.” ▶ 33:45
Corporate margins are being squeezed because companies cannot pass along higher input costs (visible in the CPI-vs-PPI gap), with Dallas Fed survey respondents urgently pleading for lower interest rates.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highDallas Fed surveyCPIPPIcorporate margins
So what: Margin compression amid disinflation strengthens the case for rate cuts and threatens earnings.
“companies have said unequivocally and if you look at the difference between CPI and PPI, we can't pass along the higher prices.” ▶ 39:48
Logistics data show transportation costs rising over March-May while unit transportation volumes fell 13.3% over the same span, a divergence hitting margins hard per the Logistics Managers Index.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumLogistics Managers Indextransportation
So what: Rising costs on falling volumes is a direct margin drag and a sign of weakening real activity.
“Transportation costs have increased over the March to May period while unit transportation volumes have are down 13.3% over that same two-month span.” ▶ 40:05
CRE Clearing, Shadow Banking and Private Credit Risk 7
The long-awaited commercial real estate downturn is now in full swing with 'extend and pretend' over, distressed office sales at a 10-year high and stress bleeding into multifamily.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highcommercial real estateofficemultifamily
So what: A clearing CRE bust pressures bank lending standards and liquidity precisely as consumer cushions thin.
“the commercial real estate cycle that we've been waiting for forever is finally in fullblown... Extend and pretend is bye-bye.” ▶ 17:44
The Fed's balance sheet is growing again (perhaps $50-60 billion a month of overnight-market operations), which DiMartino Booth argues is de facto QE unlike prior 'not-QE' episodes when the balance sheet was shrinking.
contrarian🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumFederal ReserveQEbalance sheet
So what: Renewed balance-sheet growth suggests hidden liquidity stress and quietly supports asset prices.
“unlike the prior episodes of it not being QE when the Fed's balance sheet was shrinking, now the Fed's balance sheet is growing again.” ▶ 17:10
The shadow/non-bank financial system has for two years been about 51-52% of global assets (roughly $258 trillion as of end-2024), larger than the regulated banking system and effectively unregulated.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highshadow bankingnon-bank financial institutions
So what: A majority of global assets sits outside regulation with opaque valuations, a systemic tail risk.
“258 trillion as of the end of 2024. 51% of global assets unregulated in the shadows larger than what we consider to be out in the open” ▶ 26:11
DiMartino Booth rates her concern about private markets a 7 or 8 out of 10, reasoning that visible bankruptcies are up 40% year-over-year so unseen insolvencies and valuations in the non-bank system are likely worse, amplified by higher-for-longer rates.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highprivate creditprivate equityinterest rates
So what: Opaque private-market valuations are a leading candidate for the next credit shock.
“I would put that up at a seven or an eight given the publicly visible bankruptcy cycle being 40% higher year-over-year.” ▶ 26:54
A feedback loop exists whereby public companies value their private holdings at questionable levels, so if roughly half of Mag-7-type earnings rest on recurring 'one-time' accounting marks, both the E and the P/E could be inflated.
causal🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumMagnificent Sevenprivate equityprivate creditP/E ratio
So what: Questionable private marks may be quietly propping up public equity earnings and valuations.
“So what if the E and the PE ratio because public companies are valuing their private holdings at questionable levels? There is a feedback loop somewhere in there” ▶ 24:31
The push to sell private-market assets to retirement funds and retail investors is, in DiMartino Booth's read, largely an effort to offload junk, echoing the 1990s DLJ-era creation of private-equity retail funds by lowering minimums to $250,000.
contrarian🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highprivate equityprivate creditDonaldson Lufkin Jenretteretirement fundsretail investors
So what: Retail democratization of private assets may transfer risk from sophisticated sellers to unsophisticated buyers at the top.
“I watched this marketing created in its first iteration. Um, and I knew why it was happening then. So, I think we definitely know why it's happening now.” ▶ 28:40
Blue Owl now trades as effectively a penny stock, illustrating how public-market repricing is exposing weakness in private-credit sponsors.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumBlue Owlprivate credit
So what: Publicly listed private-credit proxies are already signaling deep stress in the space.
“Blue Owl is a penny stock right now, right?” ▶ 25:39
Speculative Excess and the AI Capex Cliff 6
A record $251 billion of equity was sold in the first half of the year, with the biggest players selling as much debt and equity as possible because the issuance window has effectively closed.
data_point🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highequity issuanceprivate equity
So what: Record insider/corporate selling into strength is a classic late-cycle distribution signal.
“we've had $251 billion dollars of equity sold in the first half of the year, breaking all prior records.” ▶ 23:17
AI and AI-related companies now make up roughly 45% of the S&P 500's market cap, meaning the broad index (not just the Nasdaq) is far more concentrated and exposed than during the 1999-2000 bubble.

≈ “AI and AI related companies are like 45% of the S&P market cap right now. Not the NASDAQ... but the S&P” paraphrase
Adam Taggart argues the biggest catalyst for a market correction would be anything that compromises AI capex expectations (projected ~$800B this year, over $1T next), citing physical constraints like capacitors, permitted land, power, skilled engineers and copper, plus local data-center moratoriums.
thesis🗣 Adam Taggartconviction: mediumAI capexdata centerscopperpower gridReno

≈ “the biggest contender for what delivers the wakeup call... it's going to be something that compromises expectations in that capex buildout.” paraphrase ▶ 47:29
The reported postponement of OpenAI's IPO to 2027 and OpenAI pulling its IPO were, in DiMartino Booth's view, meaningful red flags that redefined 'first mover advantage', yet markets shrugged them off within a day.

“When the New York Times reported that OpenAI was postponing its IPO until 2027, I'm like, hello.” ▶ 48:16
Speculation is at extremes with margin debt at all-time highs, 11 leveraged/inverse ETFs trading the day after the SpaceX IPO, and retail investors now driving markets as their share of trading has doubled over 15 years.

Retail investors control an outsized share of the market but 'sling around little actual money', trading options on leveraged ETFs on margin, so their dominance is highly leveraged and fragile.
framework🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highretail investorsleveraged ETFsoptions
So what: Market leadership resting on thin, highly leveraged retail positions can unwind violently.
≈ “they're buying options on leverage ETFs on Mar... it's ridiculous that they control so much of the market, but yet they're throwing around so little actual money.” paraphrase ▶ 52:13
Positioning: Harvest Gains, Cash, Gold and the Midterms 6
DiMartino Booth says for the first time in a long time it is time to step back into precious metals, arguing much of gold's consolidation is behind us and speculative 'tourists' have been shaken out.
prediction🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: mediumgoldprecious metals

“I just said last week for the first time in a long time, I think it's time to step back in.” ▶ 42:25
One of the easiest trades right now is locking in about 4.1% on a two-year Treasury, effectively getting paid to hold cash while waiting for opportunities.
thesis🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: hightwo-year Treasurycashmoney market funds
So what: Attractive risk-free yield makes patience cheap and supports a defensive posture.
≈ “you can get 4.1% on a two-year. I mean, just go lock that in. Lock and load.” paraphrase
Her core strategy is 'let your profits ride, but never let it be said that you lost a penny' — harvest gains over time so you end up playing with house money, and hedge rather than chase risk.
framework🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highrisk managementhedging
So what: A disciplined gain-harvesting and hedging framework for late-cycle markets where flows still dominate.
“let your profits ride, but never let it be said that you lost a penny.” ▶ 53:29
Recent rotations out of tech into defensives are month/quarter-end rebalancing and flows, not genuine changes of mind, so 'you cannot fight the flows' but should be hedged.
framework🗣 Danielle DiMartino Boothconviction: highMagnificent Seventechdefensive sectors
