So what: Checklist for state-failure risk (fragmentation, ungovernability, tax breakdown, separatism) can screen systems; a system passing all of them fails only to external shock, reframing 'decline and fall' narratives.
“if you're looking at the kinds of factors that cause states to fail, fragment, you know, become ungovernable, these never happen.” ▶ 3:26:37
The only reason the empire eventually collapsed was foreign invasion it could not cope with; the internal fundamentals (taxation, governance, representation) were sound enough to have sustained it for another thousand years.
thesis🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highEastern Roman EmpireOttoman Turks
So what: Frames geographic/geopolitical exposure, not internal decay, as the binding constraint on longevity — relevant when judging whether an entity's problems are terminal-structural or externally imposed.
The states with the longest continuity (British monarchy, Portugal, Japan) share being 'tucked away at the edges', whereas the Eastern Roman Empire survived a millennium while sitting in the most contested corridor of empire-building and new religions.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highBritish monarchyPortugalJapan
So what: Longevity is partly a function of exposure/location; a durable institution in a high-competition arena is a stronger signal of quality than one protected by isolation.
“This is the most dangerous neighborhood, right, that you can possibly live in. So no wonder it didn't survive. Like it would've been extraordinary if it had.” ▶ 3:27:32
Legitimacy, accountability & the 'monarchic republic' governance model 5
Because no one held a hereditary right to the throne, ~46% of Constantinople's emperors were violently overthrown and there were ~120 civil wars over 1,000 years, creating a permanent accountability mechanism that incentivized rulers to actually deliver for subjects.
data_point🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highConstantinopleEastern Roman Empire
So what: A vivid model of accountability-through-contestability: absent formal succession rules, the constant threat of removal substituted for elections and disciplined incumbents — analogous to takeover threat disciplining management.
“Something like 46% of the emperors of Constantinople are overthrown through violence. 46%.” ▶ 37:16
Governance ran on a 'perpetual referendum' — emperors read public mood via mass acclamations in the Hippodrome and other gatherings, and would reverse policy (e.g. Alexius III canceling the 'German tax' after an uproar) when crowds turned hostile.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highHippodromeAlexius IIIConstantinople
So what: Even absolute-looking regimes can be policy-responsive when they lack succession legitimacy; a lesson that continuous consent-testing, not just elections, disciplines rulers.
“institutionally, the emperor might look absolute... In practice, no. No. The ones that did that didn't end well.” ▶ 43:28
The Machiavellian lesson from the empire is that rhetoric and action must match: the state's persona (accountable, hardworking, ruling for subjects' benefit) actually corresponded to behavior most of the time, and this credible consistency was a primary glue holding society together.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highMachiavelli
So what: Applies directly to institutional/leadership trust: a narrow gap between stated mission and observed action builds durable consensus; a wide gap (cited re: modern US foreign policy) erodes legitimacy.
“People and states and, you know, powerful people can say anything they want to. At the end of the day, what matters is what they actually do.” ▶ 31:09
The Eastern Roman Empire was not a military dictatorship despite emperors controlling the armies, because it very rarely used the army as an instrument of domestic social control — a restraint the speaker calls striking across a thousand years.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highEastern Roman Empire
So what: Turning the army on your own population is a high-variance, regime-ending move (compared to the Arab Spring / Mubarak); durable states secure compliance via consent, not force.
“Look at how many modern regimes tried to use their own armies against their people who are in a moment of rising up... It can backfire on you badly.” ▶ 2:29:05
The empire is best described as a 'monarchic republic' — a Roman res publica (common affairs of a political community) governed by a monarchical executive expected to serve the polity, not a dynastic conquest-empire.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highres publicaRomania
So what: A useful category for hybrid regimes today (strong executive + republican legitimating ideology); the ideological baseline constrains even autocratic power.
Taxation as the engine of the state 4
If one factor explains the functioning, success, and cohesion of the Eastern Roman state, it is taxation — the census-based fiscal system was the single institution binding every community, even supposedly isolated villages, into the state.
thesis🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highDiocletianEastern Roman Empire
So what: Fiscal capacity (ability to census and tax territory) is the master variable for state strength and cohesion; loss of tax base is the mechanism through which territorial losses become terminal.
“If you asked me to put my finger on one factor, it would be that.” ▶ 1:09:25
Loss of provinces triggered a self-reinforcing collapse spiral: lost territory meant lost tax revenue, which meant fewer affordable armies, which meant inability to stop the next invader — the mechanism that felled the Western Empire.
causal🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highWestern Roman EmpireVandalsNorth Africa
So what: A doom-loop template: revenue base erosion compounds military weakness; watch for the point where a system can no longer fund its own defense/core function after losing key assets.
“without those resources, they couldn't, you know, maintain, mobilize, pay for their armies. Then they had fewer armies to handle the next wave of barbarians” ▶ 1:58:21
Diocletian ended Italy's centuries-old tax exemption and imposed universal, census-based taxation, replacing a system where 'the conquerors don't pay taxes' with a broad, relatively progressive flat structure.
data_point🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highDiocletianItalyGalerius
So what: Broadening the tax base and removing privileged carve-outs (tax havens) was a key stabilization reform — a recurring lever in fiscal consolidation.
“So Italy was this big tax haven, right? Diocletian... these guys are from Illyria. They're from the Balkans. Like why is Italy tax-free exactly, you know?” ▶ 1:08:17
The tax system operated not via a code but via petition-driven exemptions granted to monasteries, elites, and villages, which accreted into unmanageable complexity, forcing emperors to periodically cancel all exemptions and restart — the speaker draws an explicit parallel to the modern US tax code and IRS.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: mediumIRSUnited States
So what: Exemption-based systems inevitably bloat and invite corruption; complexity creep is a structural tendency of any large fiscal bureaucracy, requiring periodic hard resets.
“it gets so complicated and that's how it works.” ▶ 2:11:42
Citizenship, identity & social mobility as cohesion tools 4
The 212 AD Edict of Caracalla extended full, enforceable Roman citizenship to nearly all free inhabitants and 'they meant it' — within a generation emperors and top officials were provincials, an inclusion that no modern empire (e.g. Britain in India) ever matched.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highCaracallaConstitutio AntoninianaRoman Empire
So what: Genuine, enforced inclusion of the periphery into elite opportunity structures 'sapped discontent' and reduced rebellion risk — a template for how expanding stakeholder rights buys long-run stability.
“not only did they extend citizenship to everybody, but they meant it. This is something that had teeth.” ▶ 51:55
The society retained high physical and class mobility (a poor Balkan peasant like Justinian could rise to emperor via the army), and legal 'binding to the land' was a tax-enforcement mechanism rather than a rigid caste system.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highJustinianJustin IIllyria
So what: Open mobility channels (army as ladder) act as a pressure-release valve reducing revolt incentives; a marker of resilient social systems.
Cohesion ultimately rested on two things: a credible ruling-for-your-benefit social contract, plus a tightly unified Roman-and-Orthodox identity in a world of enemies who were neither — subjects held together because 'the alternatives were worse.'
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highOrthodoxyRoman identity
So what: A two-factor durability model (credible governance + strong differentiated identity vs. worse outside options); applicable to why populations tolerate imperfect institutions.
“The alternatives were worse.” ▶ 3:30:08
The claim of 'isolated peasant communities' cut off from the state is a myth; every arable parcel was censused, taxes were collected roughly three times a year in coin, kind, services and recruits, and the church calendar and coinage further enmeshed everyone.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highEastern Roman Empire
So what: State penetration was far deeper than assumed even in pre-modern conditions — a caution against underestimating institutional reach, and a marker of administrative capacity.
“There's no proof for these communities at all... It's not like you can hide a village, right?” ▶ 2:17:51
Geography, strategy & military technology 4
Constantinople's site was the decisive strategic asset: positioned between the Danube and Euphrates frontiers and at the Europe-Asia break point, it let the state fall back on either its European or Asian provinces and functioned as an impregnable fortified command center.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highConstantinopleBosporusDanubeEuphrates
So what: Chokepoint geography can be a structural, enduring advantage; the ability to redeploy resources across theaters from a defensible core is a resilience feature worth identifying in any system.
Greek fire functioned as a jealously guarded, nuclear-tier state secret — its napalm-like compound (deployed via pressurized nozzles and even hand grenades, effective against Arab and later Viking fleets) was so well-protected that its exact composition is still debated today.
anecdote🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highGreek fireVikingsArab Caliphate
So what: A durable technological moat, protected by extreme secrecy, gave asymmetric defensive advantage — an early case study in how a guarded proprietary technology can preserve a strategic position for centuries.
“it's a state secret. They didn't want this getting out, so they kept it pretty well. The fact that we're debating it will tell you that they were pretty good at keeping secrets.” ▶ 2:58:34
The 11th-century crisis was caused mainly by a simultaneous three-front external assault (Norman knights, Pecheneg and Seljuk nomadic horsemen), not by internal moral decay; no medieval or ancient state could survive three very different enemies at once.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highNormansPechenegsSeljuk TurksMacedonian dynasty
So what: Reframes a classic 'decline' story as an external-shock story; a caution against reflexively attributing setbacks to internal rot when correlated external stressors coincide.
The Seljuk incursion into Asia Minor caused a lasting ecological/demographic transformation, converting agricultural land into pasturage, backed by manpower reserves no prior enemy possessed — permanently degrading the region's tax productivity.
causal🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highSeljuk TurksAsia Minor
So what: Some invasions inflict irreversible structural (land-use, demographic) damage that permanently shrinks the revenue base, unlike transient raids — a distinction between recoverable and terminal losses.
“they're transforming or terraforming, Agricultural land into pasturage.” ▶ 3:13:22
Religion co-opted by the state & the source-bias lesson 3
Rome captured Christianity rather than the reverse: the religion (perhaps only ~10% of the population under Constantine) was co-opted into and subordinated to the pre-existing imperial system, unlike Islam which had to build its own state from scratch.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highChristianityConstantineIslam
So what: Distinguishes systems where a movement is absorbed by incumbent institutions vs. where it must construct its own — a lens for analyzing how disruptive ideologies/technologies get institutionalized.
The iconoclasm conflict, traditionally cast as a society-shaking crisis, was in fact of interest to only ~100-200 elites (emperors, bishops, monks) who happened to write the surviving sources; the general population was largely indifferent and switched positions with each imperial policy change.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highiconoclasmOrthodoxy
So what: A sharp source-bias warning: the people who write the records over-represent their own obsessions, systematically distorting perceived importance — directly applicable to reading any biased data source (media, filings, sell-side).
“these are the people who write the texts... in my view, this is one of those controversies that's only a few people really, really cared about it, and unfortunately, they're the ones who wrote the sources.” ▶ 3:05:38
Political stability at the top can mask normal life below: the Crisis of the Third Century saw 26 emperors murdered in 50 years, yet Egyptian papyrus records show most ordinary people did not experience a crisis.
data_point🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highCrisis of the Third CenturyEgypt
So what: Headline elite turmoil is a poor proxy for real economic conditions on the ground; look at granular activity data (ancient equivalent of high-frequency data) rather than leadership drama.
≈ “That's just the emperors... what are the lives of most people?” paraphrase ▶ 59:25
Leadership evaluation & modern parallels 5
Kaldellis rates leaders low on conquest and high on durable institution-building and subject welfare: Justinian's reconquests overstretched resources, stripped the east (allowing Persia to sack Antioch), and left provinces like Italy that could not pay for themselves.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: highJustinianAntiochPersiaItaly
So what: Expansion is not value-creating per se; the test is sustainable, self-funding institution-building and long-term stakeholder benefit — a direct analog to disciplined vs. empire-building capital allocation.
“I don't think you get a pass for conquering North Africa by surrendering Syria.” ▶ 2:42:34
The explicit modern lesson: investing in institutions that visibly work for the majority (and explaining the cost to them) serves the ruling class's own long-term interest; the Romans 'built things to last' beyond roads, including the Church.
thesis🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: mediumUnited StatesRoman Empire
So what: Long-horizon institution-building over short-term extraction is framed as the durable strategy for any ruling/managing class — a governance principle for evaluating leadership quality.
“they build things to last... I think institutions that explain, you know, to people why they exist, is... Yeah, I think we could we could use some of that.” ▶ 3:35:20
A wide, persistent gap between lofty stated goals and actual military/foreign-policy action (as with US wars in Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq) is corrosive to credibility, whereas the Roman army mostly did what emperors said it did — protect subjects.
causal🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: mediumUnited StatesVietnam WarAfghanistanIraq Warmilitary-industrial complex
So what: Credibility is an asset that erodes when rhetoric and action diverge; the combination of lofty goals plus war-profit incentives structurally widens that gap in the US case.
“I don't think most of the world even believes, you know, what American leaders say their goals are, especially when it comes to, like, war.” ▶ 3:37:34
Human nature is broadly stable across history (love, hate, ambition, incompetence recur in any group of 20-30 people), while culture merely 'sets the dials'; occasionally uniquely skilled individuals at the right moment genuinely turn the tides.
framework🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: mediumThucydidesSteve Jobs
So what: Supports using historical human behavior as a stable base rate for forecasting, while leaving room for outlier individuals — a balanced view against both pure structuralism and pure great-man theory.
“the dials go from one to 10 most people” ▶ 3:42:27
Perceived societal hopelessness often decorrelates from objective conditions (upbeat 1980s under nuclear threat vs. gloomy post-Cold-War 1990s); by most measures human misery is objectively decreasing and current challenges are largely solvable if elites can be reined in.
contrarian🗣 Anthony Kaldellisconviction: mediumCold War
So what: Sentiment is a poor read on fundamentals — a reminder to separate mood/narrative from objective trend data when assessing risk.
≈ “the pervasive sense of hopelessness or... Despair doesn't always match with, like, if you look at the situation objectively.” paraphrase

Animal Spirits · Carlson & Batnick 1 episode(s) · 29 insights

Invest in the Robot Revolution or Be Crushed by It | LFTC42 min29 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Andrew Kang, CEO of Robo Strategy (NASDAQ: BOT), argues humanoid/physical-AI robotics is investable NOW (3-7 year horizon, not decades) because LLM-scale intelligence gains are transferring to robots, and pitches BOT as a MicroStrategy-style closed-end 'publicly-listed VC' offering retail access to private robotics leaders (Figure AI, Apptronik, Dyna Robotics) at a NAV premium.
Robotics Adoption Timeline & Market Sizing · 5Why 'This Time Is Different': ChatGPT as the Unlock · 4Labor Displacement, UBI & Policy · 4BOT Structure: MicroStrategy-Style Closed-End VC · 5NAV, Private-Market Valuation & Liquidity Nuance · 4Portfolio Construction & Company-Level Bets · 7
Robotics Adoption Timeline & Market Sizing 5
Humanoid and quadruped robot shipments grew 250% in the prior year to nearly 53,000 units, and Goldman Sachs projects 250,000+ humanoid robots shipped by 2030 with a $38B TAM by 2035 (a figure Goldman recently revised up sixfold).
data_point🗣 Josh Brownconviction: highGoldman Sachshumanoid robotsquadruped robots
So what: The steep shipment growth and Goldman's sixfold upward TAM revision frame robotics as an inflection-point theme, not a distant moonshot.
“Global shipments of humanoid and quadriped robots grew 250% last year to nearly 53,000 units.” ▶ 1:36
The addressable market for humanoids should be framed as the entire ~$50 trillion annual value of global physical labor, plus unfilled labor gaps and new environments (e.g. space) where human labor is uneconomical.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIhumanoid robotsphysical labor market
So what: Sizing the opportunity against total physical-labor GDP implies orders-of-magnitude upside versus current industrial-automation TAMs.
“your market size is essentially the entire physical labor market which is something like $50 trillion” ▶ 6:43
Kang expects general intelligence for robotics to be solved in roughly 3-5-7 years (not 50-100), and believes robot intelligence will be 'there' in the next one or two years, making manufacturing capacity the true bottleneck.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIphysical AIhumanoid robots
So what: If intelligence is nearly solved, investors should watch manufacturing scale-up as the gating variable for adoption and returns.
“you could potentially solve general intelligence for robotics in a in a really reasonable amount of time... something more like three to five to seven years” ▶ 4:35
Manufacturing—not intelligence—is the main bottleneck, and building factories capable of producing 1-10 million robots/year will take time but be solved within about three to four years.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotsmanufacturing
So what: Identifies factory capacity as the rate-limiter, favoring companies with a credible path to mass production.
“we can only produce so many robots per year right now... that'll be the bottle, but we're going to solve it in the next, I would say, three, four years” ▶ 13:01
Regular people will encounter humanoids first on the internet or in specific hubs like Silicon Valley, with everyday physical contact still a few years out.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumSilicon Valleyhumanoid robots
So what: Sets expectations that mainstream physical exposure lags investment exposure, so the trade precedes visible ubiquity.
“in regular contact, I would say it's probably a few years out from now... you're going to see it more so on the internet or if you live in specific places like Silicon Valley a lot sooner” ▶ 10:18
Why 'This Time Is Different': ChatGPT as the Unlock 4
Kang's conviction flipped in late 2023/January 2024 after seeing Figure AI's robots walking in a lab, because ChatGPT's launch signaled intelligence—historically the limiting factor for robotics—was about to accelerate dramatically.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIChatGPTOpenAI
So what: Ties the robotics thesis directly to the LLM breakout, explaining why a crypto/DeFi investor pivoted fully to physical AI.
“the reason why we didn't have it work was because intelligence was always the gaining factor. And I think it was kind of a little bit more clear that intelligence development... was really going to accelerate” ▶ 4:24
The GPT-3 to GPT-5.x jump over three-to-four years demonstrates a steepening slope of AI progress that can be extrapolated to physical-AI robot intelligence following a similar trajectory.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highGPT-3GPT-5physical AI
So what: Uses the observed LLM curve as the base rate for underwriting robot-intelligence timelines.
“we saw that jump from GPT3 to GPT 5.5 5.6 six right in the last three to four years and that was a huge jump... you can just easily extrapolate to that similar path of development happening for physical AI as well” ▶ 8:46
Humanoids 'productize physical labor' the same way LLMs productized knowledge work, and a robot doing three humans' work at ~$2/hour versus $35-40/hour for a US worker makes the adoption incentive nearly impossible to ignore.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highhumanoid robotsLLMsphysical labor market
So what: The stark unit-economics gap is the core driver of forced adoption once supply exists.
≈ “a robot doing the work of three humans at roughly $2 an hour versus $35 to $40 for a US worker” paraphrase
Figure AI's roughly eight-day continuous autonomous livestream (robots sorting/flipping packages, swapping shifts to recharge) was a 'ChatGPT moment' proving the work was not human-teleoperated or a cherry-picked clip.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIhumanoid robotslogistics
So what: Sustained autonomous operation is the credibility signal that separates real capability from staged demos.
≈ “it went on for eight days straight. And I think that was like a little bit of a mini chat moment where people started to realize this is real. It wasn't controlled by a human” paraphrase
Labor Displacement, UBI & Policy 4
Kang contends AI/robotics will eventually replace both physical AND cognitive labor, so the 'automation creates new jobs' analogy may break because prior waves shifted humans into more cognitive work—which AI will also do.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumAIroboticslabor market
So what: Undercuts the techno-optimist reassurance and implies structural, not transitional, displacement risk.
“the reason why we found new jobs is because we found jobs to do that required more... complex cognitive thinking... AI is not going to stop. it's going to be able to think in all the ways that humans can” ▶ 16:45
Universal basic income may become a necessity rather than a choice, and Kang urges building displacement plans now because when the transition hits it will happen fast and late reactions leave people in serious trouble.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumUBISam AltmanElon Musk
So what: Frames policy preparation as urgent tail-risk management, distinguishing Kang from optimists who stay quiet on displacement.
≈ “you can't fight technology... we need to start working on a plan now um for when that moment does come because when it comes it's going to happen real fast” paraphrase ▶ 14:46
Governments are already moving toward strategic industrial investment (Intel/semiconductors, rare-earth processing financing) and robotics is 'clearly next,' now a major US priority reflected in bills moving through Congress.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumIntelsemiconductorsrare earthsUS governmentCongress
So what: Anticipated government subsidies/investment could be a tailwind for domestic robotics valuations and supply chains.
≈ “They've um invested in semiconductor companies in the US like Intel. They provided financing for the rare earths processing companies and I think it's pretty clear that robotics is next.” paraphrase
A Nordic-style sovereign-wealth/dividend model (or Musk's 'embodied labor / universal high income') is floated as a way to distribute robotics-driven prosperity broadly, but only works if AI safety/alignment frameworks are gotten right.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: exploratoryElon MuskSam AltmanNordic countriesAI alignment
So what: Highlights that broad-benefit outcomes are conditional, not automatic, adding governance risk to the bull case.
“if we get like figure out the AI safety AI alignment piece, then there could be some pretty good outcomes for humanity” ▶ 18:33
BOT Structure: MicroStrategy-Style Closed-End VC 5
Robo Strategy (NASDAQ: BOT) is a publicly-listed venture capital company structured as a closed-end fund—not an ETF or private VC—that invests predominantly in private robotics companies to give retail investors access to the private-market innovation.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highRobo StrategyBOTNASDAQclosed-end fund
So what: The vehicle's edge is pre-IPO robotics exposure otherwise unavailable to retail through public ETFs.
“Robo Strategy is essentially a publicly listed venture capital company. It is exclusively focused on investing in leading robotics companies and predominantly private robotics companies.” ▶ 22:25
BOT applies a MicroStrategy-style flywheel: issue shares at a premium to NAV and deploy proceeds into more robotics equity, aiming to grow exposure per share (e.g. figure-shares per BOT share) over time, analogous to Strategy growing Bitcoin-per-share ~5-10x since 2020.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highMicroStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoinBOTRobo Strategy
So what: Accretive premium-to-NAV issuance is the core value-creation engine investors must underwrite—and the key risk if the premium collapses.
“We're increasing the exposure to the companies that people see and want in the portfolio in the same way Micro Strategy did that for Bitcoin... they increased their Bitcoin per share... something between five to 10x” ▶ 39:14
BOT explicitly targets 'multiples arbitrage'—buying private robotics companies valued at private-market multiples and having public markets re-rate them higher, the same tactic public roll-ups like TransDigm and Constellation Software use (buying at 3-8x, held at 15-40x).
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highTransDigmConstellation Softwareprivate equityventure capital
So what: The public-vs-private valuation gap is the structural alpha claim; investors should test whether it persists as more capital chases private robotics.
≈ “in private equity world... they call it multiples arbitrage... and we're just applying to venture capital, which is, I think, the first time people have done it in the public equity markets” paraphrase ▶ 27:52
Kang raised three PIPE fundraises in three weeks totaling ~$100M from long-term-oriented institutional investors, all priced at roughly 3-4x the publicly stated NAV (close to each day's market price).
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highRobo StrategyBOTPIPEinstitutional investors
So what: Institutions paying 3-4x NAV is presented as a confidence signal, but also underscores how far the market price sits above stated asset value.
≈ “we've announced three pipe fundraises over the course of the past three weeks totaling close to around 100 million... they were all done at multiples to the publicly stated NAV... around 3 to 4x” paraphrase
Kang argues share issuances in BOT should be read as accretive signals of new investment opportunities rather than dilution, since management (the largest shareholders) will only issue when the deal plus expected returns substantially outweigh dilution cost.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumBOTRobo Strategy
So what: Reframes dilution optics for retail holders, but relies on management discipline and continued premium-to-NAV pricing.
“the management team is not going to want to do dilution or issue shares if we don't believe it's massively accreative” ▶ 38:26
NAV, Private-Market Valuation & Liquidity Nuance 4
BOT's NAV is inherently lagging because private assets are marked at the last round (or a discount) and don't reflect company developments—positive or negative—until the next round, which can be six months to two years out.
framework🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highNAVprivate marketsBOT
So what: Investors must treat stated NAV as stale; the public price (premium/discount) is partly the market's attempt to reflect between-round changes.
≈ “these private assets are marked at the last round valuation or sometimes at a discount... if there's a positive development or a negative development it's not reflected until the next round” paraphrase
Kang uses Anthropic as an example of private-market mispricing: a hypothetical $50B round followed weeks later by a major Pentagon contract makes the company objectively worth more, but with no ticker the value isn't repriced until a new transaction.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumAnthropicPentagonprivate markets
So what: Illustrates the information/liquidity gap that both creates the arbitrage and makes NAV unreliable in real time.
≈ “they raise at a $50 billion valuation and then news comes out like 3 weeks later that they got this massive contract with the Pentagon. Obviously the company's worth more but there's no ticker and no one's trading it” paraphrase
Venture secondary markets are opaque and non-transparent—transactions aren't publicly reported and prices anchor to the most recent round—creating alpha for managers who can price between rounds better than the anchored crowd.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumsecondary marketsventure capital
So what: Positions manager skill in private pricing as a genuine edge, but also a source of valuation subjectivity for BOT holders.
“there's a lot of nontransparency opakquakeness in terms of where these things should actually be priced... That's a little bit of alpha actually” ▶ 30:06
BOT plans to move NAV updates from quarterly to monthly and may increasingly incorporate employee/secondary share transactions into its valuation framework, though currently that is only a small component.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: mediumBOTNAVsecondary markets
So what: More frequent, transaction-informed marks could narrow the info gap and affect how the premium/discount behaves.
“we're actually going to probably start updating it monthly on our website” ▶ 24:31
Portfolio Construction & Company-Level Bets 7
BOT's three largest positions—Figure AI, Apptronik, and Dyna Robotics—each represent roughly a quarter of net assets, a deliberately concentrated 'own the best, not 100 startups' strategy that raises single-name risk.
data_point🗣 Josh Brownconviction: highFigure AIApptronikDyna RoboticsBOT
So what: High concentration amplifies both upside and idiosyncratic blow-up risk; allocations are expected to shift as the field matures.
“your three largest positions are Figure AI, Aptronic, and Dino Robotics... each represents a quarter of the fund's net assets” ▶ 31:27
Kang deliberately bets on the highest-valuation category leaders (the 'Anthropics or OpenAIs of robotics') rather than cheaper laggards, arguing in AI the best-funded, best-talent, best-leadership companies out-competed cheaper alternatives.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIpower lawrobotics
So what: A power-law, pay-up-for-winners philosophy—implying investors shouldn't reach for cheap robotics names to 'catch up.'
“we just want to bet on the best and leading companies in the space because there is so much more upside available. you don't need to be picky” ▶ 33:25
Figure AI is framed as the 'Apple of humanoids'—best-in-class AI, hardware, and manufacturing plus a premium showroom experience—with its first consumer model pricing TBD but possibly six figures, alongside a likely leasing model for industry.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highFigure AIApplehumanoid robots
So what: Positions Figure as a premium consumer/industrial brand; leasing could drive industrial adoption ahead of home ownership.
“I would think about them as like the apple for humanoids... they have the best product” ▶ 33:46
Apptronik is a 9-10 year humanoid pioneer distinguished by original actuator (joint-motor) innovation, having built prototypes for a well-known humanoid company before making its own robots—now partnering with Jabil for manufacturing and Google DeepMind for AI.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highApptronikJabilGoogle DeepMindactuators
So what: Actuator IP plus tier-1 manufacturing/AI partnerships is the differentiation thesis for this holding.
≈ “they have what I consider some of the original innovators in actuator development. Actuators are basically the motors in the joints of the humanoid” paraphrase
Dyna Robotics is a vertically integrated bet on legless, stationary robots (two arms or on a wheelbase), on the thesis that most repetitive factory tasks don't require legs—which add significant cost—so the non-humanoid segment is a large separate market.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highDyna RoboticsFigure AIrobotics
So what: Diversifies BOT beyond bipedal humanoids into cheaper fixed/wheeled robots targeting the bulk of stationary factory work.
“their robot doesn't have legs... not every single robot needs to move around to have legs and those add a lot of cost to the robot... Most tasks in a factory, you're just kind of stationary” ▶ 36:06
Tesla is judged to be in pole position on humanoid manufacturing, reportedly building a 10-million-robot-per-year facility while most competitors produce only thousands to tens of thousands per year.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highTeslaElon Muskhumanoid robotsmanufacturing
So what: Names Tesla as the scale leader on the true bottleneck (manufacturing), a key competitive reference point for the sector.
≈ “I think they're working on a 10 million robot per year facility right now, which would be the biggest one by far” paraphrase
Top robotics companies are staying private far longer (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI not expected public until $1T+ valuations) because abundant private capital and the burden of public boards remove the incentive to IPO—so private-market access is the only early entry.
causal🗣 Andrew Kangconviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIIPO
So what: The 'stay private longer' trend is the structural justification for BOT's existence versus public robotics ETFs.
“these private companies stay private longer and longer. SpaceX IPOed at two trillion anthropic open AI not going public until at least a trillion” ▶ 40:22

Risk Reversal · Dan Nathan 1 episode(s) · 29 insights

Bull Markets Die on Euphoria. Are We Finally There?42 min29 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue the AI bull market has reached its euphoric final leg, now concentrated in memory/semiconductor stocks (Micron, SanDisk, SK Hynix) even as hyperscalers roll over, and warn that Micron's blow-off top, churning sector rotation, a failing SpaceX debt deal, and the S&P 7,200 level signal a likely short-term top and structural damage beneath the index surface.
Euphoria as the final bull-market stage · 3Blow-off top in memory and semiconductors · 5Hyperscaler and AI-leader breakdowns · 4Churn, technical damage and short-term top · 4IPO/capital-markets euphoria and retail distribution · 4No fear in cross-asset positioning · 3Oil, geopolitics and rates · 4Category disruption in consumer/retail · 2
Euphoria as the final bull-market stage 3
The AI bull market is in its euphoric final stage, with euphoria now concentrated in one last group of memory/semiconductor stocks after tech leadership broadened and then meandered.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highNvidiasemiconductorsmemory stocksLeon CoopermanJohn Templeton
So what: If euphoria marks the end, the memory-stock leadership is a late-cycle tell rather than a fresh opportunity.
“Bull markets are born on pessimism, grown on skepticism, mature in optimism, and die on euphoria.” ▶ 0:00
The current market psychology and retail-investor behavior is eerily similar to the late 1920s / early 1930s go-go period, with only the names of the leading stocks having changed.
framework🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumRCAAndrew Ross SorkinFederal Reserve
So what: A 1929-style behavioral parallel argues for caution even if the 'it's different this time' crowd is loud.
“only the names have changed. The behavior and some of the things around the behavior is exactly the same.” ▶ 9:22
The market can be in a secular AI bull market (Munster's 'second inning') yet still see many individual AI stocks sell off dramatically, so early-innings framing does not protect against large single-name drawdowns.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGene MunsterDeepwaterPalantirNvidia
So what: Being right on the long-term AI story doesn't prevent 50-70% drawdowns in richly valued names.
“we could be in a secular bull market, but you could still have, you know, many of the stocks, you know, sell off dramatically.” ▶ 10:23
Blow-off top in memory and semiconductors 5
Micron made an all-time high on strong earnings and guidance, and Nathan/Adami flag it as a potential blow-off top since bull runs often end on the best of news.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highMicronMU
So what: Peak fundamentals plus peak price is a classic exhaustion setup, not a buy signal.
“sometimes bull runs end on the best of news, and you can't argue that that wasn't pretty damn good news in the form of the earnings release.” ▶ 4:10
Memory stocks (Micron, SanDisk, Western Digital, Seagate) have been 'ripping like nothing we've ever seen before' as the last late-to-the-game semiconductor group to rally, after Nvidia, then Intel and AMD.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMicronSanDiskWestern DigitalSeagateNvidiaIntelAMD
So what: The final rotation into memory names marks broad participation exhaustion in the semi trade.
“the memory stocks, Micron, SanDisk, uh Western Dig, um and Seagate, you know, they've just been ripping.” ▶ 6:18
The SOX semiconductor index is up roughly 107% on the year and doubled in the last few months / during Q2, an extreme move.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highSOXsemiconductors
So what: A doubling in one quarter quantifies the euphoria they are warning about.
“the socks is up 107% on the year. We know that the socks doubled in the last few months.” ▶ 17:24
Buying Micron at these levels is historically the wrong period to buy because it requires assuming the market is under-pricing future revenue, earnings and margins right when history says not to.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMicronMU
So what: Late-cycle memory buyers are betting against mean reversion in a cyclical business.
“history has shown us that this is the exact sort of period where you should not be buying.” ▶ 21:23
Bulls justify memory names as no longer cyclical (a secular change), arguing Micron could still be ~50% undervalued on P/E and growth rates, whereas Adami expects cyclicality to return in spades once competition and pricing power erode.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumMicronMU
So what: The whole thesis turns on whether memory has escaped cyclicality; if not, valuations unwind hard.
“Micron even with the move that it's seen is still probably 50% 50 undervalued. And they can do the math around it to show you.” ▶ 22:07
Hyperscaler and AI-leader breakdowns 4
Microsoft is down about 35% from its all-time high (roughly $555 in August 2025) and June is its worst month since 2000, having round-tripped a bounce from ~355 in March to ~450 and back down.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMicrosoftMSFT
So what: The former largest-cap company breaking down signals structural damage masked by the index.
“Microsoft is down 35% Uh um you know, from its all-time highs. I think June, this is the worst month it's had since the year 2000” ▶ 5:46
All the hyperscalers spending collectively trillions on data-center CapEx have rolled over, and software, internet and security names have been disrupted, leaving semis as the last standing leadership.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highhyperscalerssoftwareinternetsecuritysemiconductors
So what: Narrow leadership resting on one final group is fragile and reversible.
“if all the hyperscalers are rolling over, they're the ones that are spending collectively, you know, trillions of dollars to do this” ▶ 5:55
Palantir round-tripped from a run toward $200 down to nearly $100 last week (roughly cut in half in six to seven months) despite revenue growth from ~$3B four years ago toward $8B and a Street estimate near $20B in three years.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highPalantirPLTR
So what: Even a top 'early-innings' story with government customers halved, showing valuation risk trumps narrative.
“Well, the stock basically kissed $100 guy last week, okay? And it's now rallied a little bit.” ▶ 10:41
Google has been anointed the universal AI long winner on vertical integration (GCP, TPUs, models, distribution) and being less compute-constrained than rivals, rallying ~50% from late-March lows to recent highs in about three months.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumGoogleGOOGLGCPTPUs
So what: Consensus crowding into Google as the AI winner is itself a euphoria signal to watch.
“Google was a universal sort of long as far as AI. I think investors over the last year have just kind of anointed this one the winner.” ▶ 19:38
Churn, technical damage and short-term top 4
The 7,200 level in the S&P 500 must be defended into quarter-end to stave off an outside month to the downside.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highS&P 500
So what: A specific technical line whose break would confirm a monthly bearish reversal.
≈ “that 7,200 level in the S&P 500 ... that's the level that needs to be defended in order to stave off what will be an outside month to the downside.” paraphrase
The rapid churn of money rotating from one sector to another on a daily and even hourly basis is unhealthy 'churn before the storm' that typically signifies a short-term top.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highsemiconductorssoftwareinternet
So what: Frantic rotation without net progress is a distribution tell, not healthy breadth.
“I don't think that's healthy at all and I think that's sort of the not calm before the storm, but I think it's sort of the churn before the storm” ▶ 18:45
Sharp single-day foreign-market drops (Kospi/SK Hynix/Samsung down ~9% over two days, Nikkei down 4%) are being ignored but matter because such moves rarely happen and could happen in the US just as easily.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumKospiSK HynixSamsungNikkeiJapan
So what: Overseas volatility in memory-heavy indices is an early warning US investors are dismissing.
“There were two days where the Kospi was down 9%.” ▶ 17:58
Index strength in the S&P and Nasdaq is masking significant technical damage in individual names, causing investors to under-focus on stories being told under the surface.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highS&P 500Nasdaq
So what: Headline indices give false comfort while leadership deteriorates beneath them.
“the broader market ... sort of clouds over a lot of the damage that's been being done” ▶ 4:26
IPO/capital-markets euphoria and retail distribution 4
Euphoria around banks is fading, with the bloom coming off recent deals as Cerebras (CBRS) and SpaceX have not traded well since pricing, and the OpenAI IPO is now likely pushed to next year.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumCerebrasCBRSSpaceXOpenAIJP MorganJamie Dimon
So what: A stalling IPO calendar removes a key fee engine and euphoria driver for bank stocks.
“the bloom is coming off the rose in the way the two specific companies are trading. Cerebrus, CBRS, and obviously SpaceX” ▶ 25:43
SpaceX priced its equity around 135, traded above 200, and is now at ~153, and its ~$25B debt offering is already showing ~$305M in paper losses days after issuance, with bonds quoting up to 0.28pp wider than the 1.75pp-over-Treasuries issue spread.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highSpaceXBloombergTreasuries
So what: Fresh deals breaking price signals investor appetite is being over-tapped ('plugging').
“Bond traders stunned as losses on SpaceX new debt keep coming.” ▶ 27:35
Designating 30% of the SpaceX IPO for retail is an alarming warning sign, reflecting the old rule that if they're selling it to you, you should ask why you'd want to buy it.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highSpaceXretail investors
So what: Heavy retail allocation is a distribution red flag late in the cycle.
“when I heard that 30% of the SpaceX IPO was designated for retail, that to me was alarming” ▶ 28:43
Companies are increasingly 'plugging' investors with securities of dramatically money-losing companies (OpenAI, Anthropic and others), which Nathan characterizes as a dumpster fire narrative persisting for six to nine months.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicSpaceX
So what: Pipeline of loss-making offerings signals late-cycle supply flooding the market.
“all these companies are trying to plug investors, okay, with with basically securities of dramatically money-losing companies.”
No fear in cross-asset positioning 3
There is no fear anywhere across assets: the 10-year yield sits at 4.375% (one-month lows), gold just above 4,000, Bitcoin around 60,000, and the VIX around 19 after briefly topping 20.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: high10-year TreasurygoldBitcoinVIX
So what: Complacency across rates, gold, crypto and vol is itself a contrarian warning.
“There's just no fear anywhere in the market like at all.” ▶ 34:48
Crypto is where fear is manifesting: Bitcoin has fallen from an all-time high near $2.5T to about $1.2T, roughly halving the risk asset, representing major value destruction that overlaps with the same investors long the semi-AI trade.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumBitcoincryptosemiconductors
So what: Shared ownership between crypto and AI longs could transmit crypto pain into equities.
“6 to 9 months ago when when Bitcoin was at its all-time highs, it was nearly two and a half trillion dollar risk asset. Now that it's 1.2” ▶ 36:08
A leveraged Bitcoin/ETH treasury company such as MicroStrategy or Bitmain is likely to blow up famously, triggering a large forced liquidation where investors shoot first and ask questions later, with the two reportedly down ~$10B combined.
prediction🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumMicroStrategyBitmainBitcoinEthereum
So what: Crypto-treasury leverage is a plausible spark for a disorderly unwind.
“one of these names ... it's going to They're going to blow up and it's going to blow up famously and there's going to be a huge liquidation” ▶ 35:50
Oil, geopolitics and rates 4
Crude oil sits below 70 with no war premium despite Iran shooting a drone at a container ship near the Strait of Hormuz days after a symbolic Versailles MOU, implying another sudden 8-10% up-move in crude is likely coming from some level.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumcrude oilIranStrait of HormuzOPECOman
So what: Mispriced geopolitical risk sets up an asymmetric long-crude spike.
“there'll be a day where crude's up 8 to 10% seemingly out of nowhere.” ▶ 31:36
Oil lacks a risk premium because OPEC is effectively no longer an entity (every-nation-for-itself, bearish for crude), global demand concerns are showing in price, and the market has learned missiles flying is not as bullish for the commodity as assumed.
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumOPECcrude oil
So what: Structural OPEC fragmentation plus demand fear can keep crude capped absent a shock.
“the fact that OPEC is no longer even an entity and it's sort of every man and woman for themselves ... is bearish for crude oil” ▶ 30:44
A stable/strong jobs report reinforces that rate cuts are off the table and hikes are possible or probable, aligning with Kevin Warsh's inflation-fighting hawkish stance.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumjobs reportFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Markets are underpricing a Fed that may hike rather than cut.
“Just reinforces the notion that rate cuts are not happening, that rate hikes are possible if not probable” ▶ 36:58
Counterintuitively, a hawkish Fed is bond-market positive because the market realizes it can't run the bond-vigilante playbook against a Fed that won't be messed with.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumFederal Reservebond market
So what: A credibly hawkish Fed could support long bonds even as it fights inflation.
“a hawkish Fed is actually bond market positive, and I know that's completely counterintuitive” ▶ 37:36
Category disruption in consumer/retail 2
Nike is a classic specialty-retail category-creation story that reversed: after an all-time high near $180-185 in fall 2021 it has fallen to about $40 (a nine-year-plus low, ~25% of its value five years ago), driven by complacency and competition rather than its new CFO.
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highNikeNKE
So what: When competition enters a dominated category, the stock unwinds fast, a template for today's leaders.
“when you create a category and dominate it, your stock goes up in a straight line. When competition comes ... it goes the other way in a fast, fast way.” ▶ 39:06
Nike and peers (Under Armour, Lululemon) are being pressured by emerging, largely private athletic-leisure brands such as Vuori, Alo and On.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumNikeUnder ArmourLululemonVuoriAloOn
So what: Private upstarts eroding incumbents show disruption risk isn't confined to tech.
“a bunch of the names ... are private. You got the Vuori, you got the Alo ... you have On” ▶ 39:40

Sourcery with Molly O'Shea 1 episode(s) · 29 insights

Benchmark's AI Bets: Cerebras, Sierra, Legora, Fireworks, Starcloud, Gumloop..57 min29 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Benchmark partner (Ev) argues AI has inverted the 'golden rules' of spreadsheet software investing, breaks down the divergent business models of AI companies via a P*Q*M taxonomy, and details Benchmark's founder-first bets (Fireworks, Sierra, Legora, Gumloop, Starcloud, Cerebras) plus the ecosystem shock from mega-rounds like Anthropic's $380B.
Death of Spreadsheet Investing / Inverted Risk Paradigm · 3AI Company Taxonomy & P*Q*M Framework · 3Inference as the Revenue Engine · 3Agents as the Biggest Business-Model Shift Since SaaS · 3Gumloop, Model Routing & the Frontier-vs-Open-Source Split · 5Frontier Lab Pricing Power Under RSI Uncertainty · 3Capital Markets: Mega-Rounds, Late-Stage Upside & VC Redefinition · 5Benchmark's Founder-First Portfolio Construction · 4
Death of Spreadsheet Investing / Inverted Risk Paradigm 3
In the AI paradigm the historical inverse relationship between scale and risk has broken: companies can exceed $1B in revenue without proven unit economics or durable product differentiation, so impairment risk is now flat or even positively correlated with scale.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highBenchmarkAI
So what: Late-stage AI revenue no longer signals de-risking; scale alone is not a safety signal for underwriters.
“you can have businesses that are well over a billion dollars in revenue um that haven't proven out their unit economics” ▶ 3:08
The AI-era winners invert nearly every SaaS golden rule: FTE/implementation-heavy distribution replaces PLG, high AI gross margins now signal nobody is using your AI features, and companies are being pushed to build capex-intensive in-house research labs to post-train models.
contrarian🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highSaaSPalantirRule of 40PLG
So what: First-principles SaaS quality screens now flag the best AI companies as low-quality, forcing a rewrite of the investing rubric.
“if you have an AI product with high gross margins, that means that no one's using your AI features” ▶ 7:32
High gross margins in AI are a warning sign because inference costs real money, and 'the Palantirification of everything' has made services-heavy implementation consultants a feature rather than a defect.
contrarian🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumPalantirSaaS
So what: Gross-margin screens must be recalibrated; low-margin AI apps can still be the most valuable.
“FTE is the new PLG” ▶ 6:58
AI Company Taxonomy & P*Q*M Framework 3
Unlike SaaS where 'software tastes like chicken' (all P&Ls converge at maturity), AI companies have radically divergent business models, capital intensities and margin profiles, so there is a real edge for investors who develop a new taxonomy of AI company types.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highVista Equity PartnersRobert SmithSaaS
So what: Category labels like 'inference company' are misleading; underwrite each AI business on its specific economic model.
“Software tastes like chicken, and that's why it's beautiful.” ▶ 10:47
Fireworks and Crusoe look like the same 'inference' category but are fundamentally different: Crusoe builds and owns data centers (acquiring power, land, permits, often for hyperscalers), while Fireworks owns no data centers and instead leases GPU capacity and monetizes software layered on top to cut cost and latency.
data_point🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highFireworksCrusoeBenchmarkGPUs
So what: Do not lump inference plays together; capital intensity and margins differ enormously within the category.
“from the outside in, you could say like, "Oh, that's just like two inference companies." But they have completely different business models” ▶ 10:26
Applying P*Q*M (price x quantity x margin) to AI apps: quantity (buyers) is roughly the same as SaaS, margin is almost definitively below 70% for ~99% of AI app companies, but price can be immensely high, with inference platforms signing nine-figure contracts even with startups.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highSaaSAI
So what: AI economics trade margin for radically higher contract value; model P uplift, not M, to size the opportunity.
“you have um you know, these inference platforms that have nine-figure contracts with with startups”
Inference as the Revenue Engine 3
Ev's operating advice to a portfolio company with huge developer usage but no business model was to 'walk under the waterfall' of inference first, because the sheer wave of inference-driven revenue is the enabler for monetization even before you know the shape of your bucket.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highFireworksinference
So what: Prioritize getting into the inference revenue stream over perfecting the model; monetization emerges downstream.
“the first thing you should do is get under the waterfall. And the waterfall's inference.” ▶ 17:16
Inference monetization removes the growth rate limit of headcount-based SaaS pricing, which is why AI companies grow 1-to-20-to-100 or 1-to-30-to-300 instead of the old 1-to-3-to-9-to-20 SaaS trajectory.
causal🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highFireworksFalBasetenModalSierra
So what: Usage/outcome-based inference pricing structurally uncaps growth, explaining hypergrowth revenue curves.
“it removes any sort of rate limit that you have on your potential to grow” ▶ 18:56
Inference monetization spans a spectrum from thin reselling/brokerage margins to heavily abstracted outcome-based pricing, exemplified by Sierra charging per completed deflection of customer support queries.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumSierrainference
So what: The degree of abstraction over raw inference determines margin capture and defensibility.
Agents as the Biggest Business-Model Shift Since SaaS 3
Agents represent the greatest product and business-model innovation since the start of SaaS/cloud because they shift buyers from purchasing licenses to purchasing 'intelligence on tap' or white-collar work output via API.
thesis🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highagentsSaaScloud
So what: Software budgets get re-anchored to labor/work value, a far larger TAM than seat licenses.
“it represents the most important both product like product / technology technology, and then also just like raw revenue and business model shift we've ever seen, probably in in in like maybe the history of technology” ▶ 22:07
Benchmark got excited about coding agents when developers reported spending ~$3,000/month each on Claude Code (~$36K per developer), turning what used to be a $50K company ACV into a per-developer figure that is still growing, potentially making coding a $20M line item for an average company and $500M/month for some.
data_point🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highClaude CodeAnthropicBenchmark
So what: Per-seat AI spend can dwarf entire legacy SaaS contracts, redefining enterprise software budgets.
“we have developers that are spending, you know, $3,000 per month themselves, like each, on Cloud Code” ▶ 21:03
When private equity firms start telling all their portfolio companies to say they sell 'agents,' the term is cooked and over-marketed, even if the underlying shift is real.
contrarian🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumagentsprivate equity
So what: Discount 'agent' branding as a signal; underwrite the actual work-replacement economics instead.
“when private equity firms are telling all their portfolio companies to say that they sell agents, like that's when you know, it's like okay, like the term is cooked” ▶ 19:38
Gumloop, Model Routing & the Frontier-vs-Open-Source Split 5
Gumloop is a bet that what happened in coding (developers offloading the bulk of work to agents) will extend to most white-collar and eventually blue-collar functions, providing a collaborative AI agent/automation canvas usable by every employee, not just developers.
thesis🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highGumloopClaude CodeCodexCognitionCursorZapier
So what: Horizontal enterprise automation for all employees is the next expansion after coding agents.
“what we've seen in code is going to happen in most white-collar job functions and eventually probably most blue-collar job functions as well” ▶ 24:37
A third-party independent routing vendor is valuable because models are 'jagged'—Gemini is the best multimodal, Claude typically best at coding (though some now think GPT 5.5 beats the latest Opus at coding), and open-source offers the best value-for-cost—and someone must stop enterprises wastefully using Opus 4.8 to check the weather.
thesis🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumGumloopGeminiClaudeGPT-5.5Opusopen-source models
So what: Model routing is a defensible layer because no single lab dominates all tasks and cost control matters at enterprise scale.
“in some of these enterprises you have employees checking the weather with Opus 4.8” ▶ 26:35
Ev's 'AI mom test': the share of everyday queries that genuinely require a frontier (or near-frontier) model has fallen to effectively 0% for non-technical users, meaning a growing proportion of tasks can be served by cheap open-source models—Cognition post-trained an open-source model on low-complexity tasks to save ~95% per query.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highCognitionopen-source modelsfrontier models
So what: A large and growing slice of inference demand can be routed off the frontier, pressuring frontier pricing power.
“there's nothing that my mom actually asks of her AI products that needs to be done by the frontier or even a nearer frontier model” ▶ 28:45
The open-source-vs-frontier debate is not zero-sum yet: per Eric Vishria, on-device inference, open-source inference, and proprietary models are all seeing parabolic demand simultaneously.
contrarian🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner, citing Eric Vishria)conviction: mediumEric Vishriaopen-source modelsAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Frame the market as multiple growing pools, not a single winner-take-all fight.
“on-device inference, yes. Open source inference, yes. Proprietary models, yes.” ▶ 31:10
Claude Code and Anthropic revenue went parabolic specifically because coding models did not become genuinely usable until Opus 4.5 last winter, marking a real capability breakthrough rather than gradual improvement.
causal🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highAnthropicClaude CodeOpus 4.5
So what: Revenue inflections in AI track discrete capability step-changes, not smooth curves.
“I don't think we had like unbelievable quality um coding models until Opus 4.5 until like last winter, which is why you saw Anthropic's revenue and why you saw Claude code go so parabolic” ▶ 30:26
Frontier Lab Pricing Power Under RSI Uncertainty 3
Frontier lab durability hinges on a binary: if recursive self-improvement produces 'geniuses in a data center' the labs keep strong pricing power and may reaccelerate growth, but if capabilities hit a ceiling and distillation makes open source ~95% as good, premium margins get severely compressed.
prediction🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIopen-source models
So what: Frontier-lab valuations are a bet on RSI continuing; a capability plateau is the key downside scenario.
“if the frontier goes that high, then you legitimately do have probably a lot of pricing power” ▶ 32:41
A capability ceiling would not kill the frontier labs because ~900M weekly ChatGPT users like the product and could not tell if the underlying model were swapped (e.g., 5.5 for 5.2); the damage is to premium pricing power, not to the product franchise.
prediction🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: mediumOpenAIChatGPTAnthropic
So what: Product/brand strength decouples lab survival from model-lead erosion; margin, not existence, is at risk.
“the vast majority of the 900 million weekly active users wouldn't know” ▶ 33:28
Anthropic revenue was last reported around $45B and is now speculated near $60B, with both Anthropic and OpenAI (alongside SpaceX and xAI) preparing to go public.
data_point🗣 Molly O'Shea / Evconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAISpaceXxAI
So what: The largest AI labs are approaching public-market liquidity at unprecedented revenue scale.
“Anthropic was like last rumored and reported around 45 billion and now people are speculating around 60 billion” ▶ 31:44
Capital Markets: Mega-Rounds, Late-Stage Upside & VC Redefinition 5
Anthropic's $380B round is categorically unprecedented: the ~$30B raised at that valuation, if Anthropic reaches a $1.5T liquid valuation, would return roughly 35x the gross dollars of the entire Snowflake pre-IPO round—i.e., '35 Snowflake pre-IPO rounds in a single round.'
data_point🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highAnthropicSnowflakeSlackDoorDashNubank
So what: The absolute dollar scale of a single AI round dwarfs a decade of best-in-class pre-IPO deals combined.
“it's 35 Snowflake pre-IPO rounds in a single round” ▶ 44:55
The impending liquidity from AI mega-IPOs is under-discussed and will shock the ecosystem faster than SpaceX did (which took 20+ years), with early visible effects already in the SF housing market where homes go for 2x asking in all-cash or lab equity.
prediction🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highAnthropicSpaceXSF housing market
So what: A concentrated, near-term wealth event will ripple into asset prices, new company formation and talent flows.
“everything's going for 2x asking price at this point. And it's all cash.” ▶ 46:05
AI has inverted the risk/upside curve so that a late-stage company can offer higher upside than a Series C, because massive markets plus companies staying private indefinitely create 'rebirth' moments—like Starlink transforming SpaceX so its S-1 P&L is now mostly consumer/B2B broadband, not launch.
contrarian🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highSpaceXStarlinkDatabricksStripe
So what: Late-stage entries at triple-digit-billion valuations can still multiply if a second business is being reborn inside the company.
“you actually have these situations where a late-stage company can have much higher upside than like a Series C” ▶ 38:30
AI changes the funding mechanism itself: a neo-lab may need $2B of compute on day one just to test a research direction, versus Airbnb's ~$500K YC seed to test product-market fit—a completely different capital market.
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highAirbnbY CombinatorGPUs
So what: Massive day-one compute costs make early-stage AI a fundamentally different, capital-intensive game.
“you need two billion dollars of compute to see whether it's going to work or not” ▶ 37:20
Firms like General Catalyst and Andreessen Horowitz are now alternative asset managers, not venture capital firms—running venture, growth, debt, health insurance and wealth-management products—and the industry has failed to update its nomenclature (Ev prefers 'venture-growth').
framework🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highGeneral CatalystAndreessen HorowitzIconiqprivate credit
So what: 'VC' is now a product line inside multi-strategy asset managers; assess these firms as diversified managers.
“venture in many ways is still the same, but it's a product now for many of these firms. It's not the firms themselves.” ▶ 42:29
Benchmark's Founder-First Portfolio Construction 4
Benchmark's edge is partnering with entrepreneurs at inception (e.g., ~$50M post) so that questions about exit multiples and capital efficiency at scale become largely irrelevant, betting that great founders are 'always in style' while business models rotate in and out of favor.
thesis🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highBenchmarkSaaS
So what: Entering at inception insulates returns from the disorienting late-stage economics debates plaguing growth investors.
“when you're backing an entrepreneur at inception at 50 posts... if you're having to answer those questions, uh you sort of already done your job” ▶ 13:40
Benchmark's AI portfolio (Legora, Sierra, LangChain, HeyGen, Starcloud, Fireworks, Cerebras, Mercor, Exa, Gumloop) looks thematically curated from outside but was built entirely 'founder-out' with zero deliberate category selection—several bets were even pivots into categories Benchmark did not originally invest in.
anecdote🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highLegoraSierraLangChainHeyGenStarcloudFireworksCerebrasMercorExaGumloopBenchmark
So what: Apparent thematic coverage can be an emergent byproduct of pure founder selection, not top-down category bets.
≈ “there was truly kind of what we talked about before. It was all founder out. It was all based on the founder.” paraphrase
Benchmark (Chetan) closed its Starcloud orbital-data-center investment weeks before Elon Musk publicly championed the category, and the bet rested on the team (Philip) already being the only company with a working GPU in space, not on a view that orbital data centers are the future.
anecdote🗣 Ev (Benchmark partner)conviction: highStarcloudBenchmarkChetan PuttaguntaElon MuskSpaceXGPUs
So what: Illustrates the founder-first thesis: back proven-execution teams ahead of category consensus, then let hype follow.
“we closed the investment weeks before Elon started like you know, professing his love and and bullishness on Orbital Data Center” ▶ 51:35
Ev names Cerebras as recently gone public and Starcloud as named in the SpaceX S-1, alongside portfolio companies Sierra (horizontal AI), Legora (vertical/legal AI), LangChain (developer), HeyGen (consumer) and Mercor (which recovered positively after a data breach).
data_point🗣 Molly O'Shea / Evconviction: mediumCerebrasStarcloudSpaceXSierraLegoraLangChainHeyGenMercor
So what: Concrete markers of portfolio maturity: an IPO, an S-1 mention, and reputational recovery post-breach.
“Then you have Cerebras that just went public, Sierra, LangChain, uh Legora, Fireworks, which we talked about a bit.” ▶ 49:24

New Era Finance Podcast 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

Michael Saylor: They Want You To Sell Bitcoin40 min28 insights2026-06-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Michael Saylor frames 2026 as the year Bitcoin became consensus 'digital capital,' argues Strategy's newly-invented digital credit market ($11B+) funded 175,000 BTC purchases during a bear market, and explains that a trivial 32-BTC sale is a fiduciary defense mechanism, not a thesis change. He attributes Bitcoin's underperformance to an AI-IPO capital black hole and expects capital to rotate back into BTC by Q3/Q4 2026.
Holy Trinity: Capital, Credit, Money · 4AI Capital Black Hole and Bitcoin Rotation · 5Drawdown Context and Long-Horizon Thesis · 5The 32-BTC Sale and Fiduciary Defense Mechanics · 5STRC Credit Durability and Break-Even Math · 6Narrative, X Dynamics, and Transparency Strategy · 3
Holy Trinity: Capital, Credit, Money 4
Saylor frames Bitcoin's investment case as a 'holy trinity' of digital capital (Bitcoin, ~$1T), digital credit (~$10-11B), and digital money (~$1B), arguing that combining all three unlocks trillions in addressable opportunity.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinStrategyMicroStrategy
So what: Saylor is signaling the next expansion leg is a Bitcoin-backed yield-bearing stablecoin layer, not just BTC accumulation.
“That's the holy trinity in my opinion because if you can put together capital, credit, and money, then there's no reason why you can't tap into trillions and trillions of dollars of opportunity.” ▶ 4:25
Saylor claims digital credit was a nonexistent asset class 12 months ago and has since grown to over $11 billion, making it one of Bitcoin's 'killer apps' and a meaningful driver of the marketplace.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoindigital creditStrategy
So what: A brand-new $11B credit market signals institutional demand for Bitcoin-collateralized yield instruments that didn't exist in 2025.
“It's now more than 11 billion dollars. 12 months ago it was zero dollar. There was nothing. The entire asset was invented in the past 12 months” ▶ 2:22
Saylor argues a Bitcoin-backed stablecoin was historically impossible in 'one hop' due to BTC's ~40 volatility, but a three-step ladder (Bitcoin 40V/40 return, to credit 5V/10-12% yield, to money 0V/5-10% yield pegged to fiat) now makes yield-bearing digital money feasible.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinTetherstablecoinsdigital money
So what: The volatility-laddering framework is the mechanical rationale for why Strategy's credit products can bridge to a Bitcoin-backed yield stablecoin.
“You couldn't go from Bitcoin directly to the stable coin because Bitcoin was too volatile.” ▶ 3:25
Saylor predicts entrepreneurs will build fiat-pegged digital money backed by Bitcoin that pays a 5-10% yield, positioning it as the next chapter after capital and credit.
prediction🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: mediumBitcoinstablecoinsfiat currencies
So what: A yield-bearing Bitcoin-backed stablecoin would compete directly with Tether while offering native yield, a potential disruptor of the stablecoin market.
“you're starting to see entrepreneurs starting to work to create digital money that's pegged to fiat currencies backed by Bitcoin” ▶ 4:04
AI Capital Black Hole and Bitcoin Rotation 5
Saylor argues Bitcoin behaves as 'risk capital squared'—amplifying both positive and negative risk sentiment—so macro conditions good or bad for risk assets are magnified in Bitcoin's price.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoininterest rates
So what: Explains why BTC can lag in a bifurcated market where only AI mega-caps get the risk-on bid.
“you can think of Bitcoin as like risk capital squared” ▶ 5:32
Saylor attributes Bitcoin's underperformance to an 'AI black hole' pulling roughly $500B of capital into AI, including ~$80B raises by Google, SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI, plus the SpaceX IPO which he calls the biggest in world history.
causal🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinAIGoogleSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIAmazonMetaNvidia
So what: Frames BTC weakness as a temporary capital-allocation cycle rather than a broken thesis, implying mean reversion.
“$500 billion dollar of capital is being slurped into the AI complex right now.” ▶ 6:49
Saylor estimates the AI capital pull has drained $10-20B out of the crypto economy, with institutional investors selling $5-10B of Bitcoin ETFs to fund hot AI deals.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: mediumBitcoinAIETFs
So what: Quantifies the outflow driving BTC's drawdown and frames it as rotation, not permanent exit.
“it has pulled 10, 15, 20 billion dollars of capital out of the crypto economy” ▶ 7:56
Saylor predicts hot money will rotate back into Bitcoin after 'IPO summer / AI summer,' with normalization to Bitcoin's benefit by Q3 and Q4 2026.
prediction🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: mediumBitcoinAI
So what: Gives a concrete timing thesis investors can test: a BTC recovery in H2 2026.
“probably the third quarter and I think by the fourth quarter things will be normalizing to our benefit” ▶ 10:43
Saylor uses the premium/discount to Bitcoin's 200-day moving average (or realized price) as a value gauge, noting BTC trades at a 100-200% premium when markets are hot and at or below that level when leverage flushes out.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoin200-day moving averagerealized price
So what: Offers a concrete valuation anchor suggesting BTC is currently undervalued near its cost basis.
“when the market's hot, it trades at 100% to 200% premium. And then when the market chills and the leverage comes out, it trades at that number or maybe at a slight discount” ▶ 9:26
Drawdown Context and Long-Horizon Thesis 5
Saylor says the current ~50% drawdown is Bitcoin's fifth major drawdown in five years since Strategy began buying in August 2020, and is less brutal than the 75%+ drawdown of a prior cycle.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinStrategy
So what: Normalizes the current selloff historically, reinforcing hold discipline over a multi-year horizon.
“five major draw downs in five years... This draw down is 50%. But, uh the draw down in was 75%.” ▶ 11:20
Saylor advises investors to judge Bitcoin only on a 4-year-plus horizon using signals like the 4-year (200-week) simple moving average, dismissing a few months of price action as noise.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoin200-week moving average
So what: Sets the analytical frame for evaluating BTC positions and dismissing short-term drawdown panic.
“you got to have a four-year time horizon or more” ▶ 12:14
Saylor argues Bitcoin's fundamentals are far stronger than two years ago: the President, VP, Fed chair, Treasury, SEC, and CFTC heads support it; accounting, tax, and regulatory rules improved; ~75% of major banks now support it; and there are 100+ ETFs holding over $100B.
thesis🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinFederal ReserveTreasurySECCFTCETFsbanks
So what: Argues the structural bull case has improved even as price fell, supporting a buy-the-dip stance.
“No, no bank was supporting Bitcoin two years ago. Now you've got 75% of the major banks starting to support Bitcoin.” ▶ 12:40
Saylor states Bitcoin dominance has recovered to roughly 68-70% from a 2021 low of 41% (down from a prior 70%), arguing the flippening never happened and Bitcoin is the unmistakably dominant monetary network.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoincrypto market cap
So what: Uses dominance recovery to dismiss altcoin competition risk to Bitcoin's monetary role.
“Bitcoin dominance was 41% in 2021... And now, uh, Bitcoin dominance has has returned to about almost 70% 68% plus” ▶ 13:23
Saylor compares Bitcoin to Amazon in 2010 and Apple in 2012—unstoppable networks conventional investors rejected for years—predicting a 'Warren Buffett moment' where mainstream capital finally capitulates and buys Bitcoin.
thesis🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinAmazonAppleWarren Buffett
So what: Frames current skepticism as the pre-capitulation phase before mainstream adoption, an accumulation argument.
“we'll have that Warren Buffett moment with Bitcoin. Not this year. Maybe not next year. It doesn't matter.” ▶ 16:32
The 32-BTC Sale and Fiduciary Defense Mechanics 5
Saylor says Strategy bought 175,000 Bitcoin in 2026—about 20% of all Bitcoin bought this year—and sold only 32 BTC, which he characterizes as two basis points (0.02%) and economically inconsequential.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoinStrategyMicroStrategy
So what: Reframes the widely-shared 'Saylor is selling' narrative as statistically immaterial noise.
≈ “our company bought 175,000 Bitcoin this year... We sold 32. 32 works out to be two basis points.” paraphrase ▶ 21:38
Saylor explains Strategy has a fiduciary duty to defend both credit investors (by selling BTC if needed to pay dividends) and equity investors (by swapping BTC for equity if shorts push the stock to a discount to NAV), and that unwillingness to defend either would collapse demand for its securities.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategySTRCBitcoin
So what: Explains why occasional small BTC sales are structurally necessary and not a bearish signal.
≈ “if a short seller shorts the stock to a 90% discount to the Bitcoin nav, they expect us to defend the stock and swap the Bitcoin for the equity so they don't get abused.” paraphrase
Saylor describes Strategy's accumulation engine as buying 20 BTC, selling 1 BTC, then using credit-raised capital to buy 20 more and sell 1 more—a cycle he calls more tax efficient and economically rational than never selling.
framework🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: The buy-20-sell-1 mechanic is why headline 'sales' coexist with net record accumulation.
≈ “we'll buy 20 Bitcoin, we'll sell one Bitcoin and we'll then the credit investors will give us enough to buy 20 more Bitcoin and we'll sell one more Bitcoin. That's actually more tax efficient” paraphrase
Saylor advises individuals to buy Bitcoin only with money they won't need for four-plus years and never sell it, selling shorter-duration assets instead when cash is needed—stating he has bought Bitcoin but never sold a single sat.
thesis🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Distinguishes his personal never-sell discipline from Strategy's corporate defense mechanics.
“If you need money to pay taxes, right, keep it in a different asset and that's fine.” ▶ 24:16
Saylor states Strategy's enterprise value grew from $600 million to $85 billion, attributing the roughly 140x increase to the credit-fueled accumulation machine working as designed.
data_point🗣 Michael Saylorconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategy
So what: Quantifies the payoff of the treasury model and its dependence on capital-market confidence.
“our company went from a $600 million enterprise value to an $85 billion enterprise value. It's because the machine works.” ▶ 27:08
STRC Credit Durability and Break-Even Math 6
