Barandes points to long-neglected alternative programs (Everett's 1956 'stochastic process interpretation', Fritz Bopp, Imre Fenyes 1940s-50s, and Nelson's 1966 stochastic mechanics from Brownian motion) as wave-function-free routes to quantum mechanics, arguing Nelsonian mechanics is 'no worse than Bohm's theory.'
data_point🗣 Jacob Barandesconviction: mediumHugh EverettEdward NelsonNelsonian stochastic mechanicsFritz BoppImre Fenyes
So what: Old, abandoned approaches can hold latent value; canvassing dead literature is a source of differentiated ideas others have written off.
Barandes argues that after 70+ years the projects of getting probability to work in Everettian/many-worlds and of extending deterministic Bohmian mechanics to quantum field theory remain unsolved, justifying a bet on a genuinely new stochastic direction well into the 21st century.
thesis🗣 Jacob Barandesconviction: highmany worldsEverettBohmian mechanicsquantum field theoryGRW
So what: A 'time-elapsed-without-progress' heuristic for judging when to abandon an incumbent approach and back a new one, applicable to persistently stuck research or product bets.
“it it's been a really long time and I'm not going to wait anymore on the hopes that that could happen.” ▶ 1:56:35
Locality, causation, and a new bound result 4
Barandes stresses that arguing quantum mechanics is 'local' requires disambiguating four distinct notions (local correlations, counterfactual locality, causal locality, signal locality); he concedes quantum mechanics is counterfactually non-local and signal-local, and only claims causal locality under his own definition of causation.
framework🗣 Jacob Barandesconviction: highBell's theoremEPRlocalitycausation
So what: Model for defusing loaded, ambiguous claims: force a term into its distinct precise senses before agreeing or disagreeing, applicable to any contested headline assertion.
≈ “I am not saying that quantum mechanics is in fact local. If anyone has gotten that impression that is not what I'm saying.” paraphrase
Barandes reports a paper (his 'CH-ization equality', just accepted in Physical Review A) showing that imposing locality on his theory's built-in notion of causation reproduces exactly the Tsirelson bound, the ~41% (factor sqrt(2)) maximum by which quantum mechanics can violate Bell's inequality.
data_point🗣 Jacob Barandesconviction: highTsirelson boundBell inequalityPhysical Review APopescuRohrlich
So what: Example of a framework earning credibility by deriving a known but previously unexplained empirical bound from first principles, the kind of independent confirmation that distinguishes a real edge from a fit.
“when you do that, what you find is you can violate Bell's uh inequality... only up until a very famous bound called the Telsine bound.” ▶ 2:29:26
Barandes rebuts Albert's claim that there are no eigenfunctions of position: while the Schrodinger representation of the Weyl algebra lacks them, other (irregular, faithful) representations do have position eigenfunctions, per Halvorson's 2004 'complementary representations' paper.
data_point🗣 Jacob Barandesconviction: highHans HalvorsonWeyl algebraeigenfunctions of position
So what: Illustrates that an apparent no-go objection can dissolve by changing mathematical representation; a reminder to check whether a 'proof of impossibility' is representation-dependent.
Albert warns that merely being stochastic is not sufficient for a theory to underwrite statistical mechanics and thermodynamics; he notes Penrose's stochastic collapse theory demonstrably fails at this even though GRW succeeds.
contrarian🗣 David Albertconviction: highGRWRoger Penrosestatistical mechanicsthermodynamics
So what: Caution against category-level reasoning ('it's stochastic, so it should work'); membership in a promising class does not guarantee a specific instance delivers the needed property.
“merely having a stochastic process... is surely not a sufficient condition for that.” ▶ 2:16:20

EconTalk · Russ Roberts 1 episode(s) · 22 insights

The Case for Sunshine (with Rowan Jacobsen) 6/15/2666 min22 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Author Rowan Jacobsen argues the decades-long public-health message that all sun exposure is dangerous is outdated: skin cancer kills few people, vitamin D pills fail in trials, and moderate sun exposure appears to lower cardiovascular and cancer mortality via mechanisms like nitric oxide, implying a large net health trade-off in favor of sun.
Skin cancer incidence vs. mortality and overdiagnosis · 5Sunscreen efficacy and the UVA generation gap · 4Vitamin D: supplement failure vs. natural sun exposure · 4Mechanisms and the mortality trade-off · 4Historical arc and epistemics of public-health advice · 5
Skin cancer incidence vs. mortality and overdiagnosis 5
Roughly 98% of skin cancer cases are basal or squamous cell carcinomas that rarely become invasive and carry negligible mortality; only about 2% are melanoma, the type with meaningful death risk.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highmelanomabasal cell carcinomasquamous cell carcinoma
So what: The headline skin-cancer scare conflates a mostly benign condition with a rare lethal one, distorting risk perception.
“those are responsible for about 98% of all skin cancer cases. Then the third important type of skin cancer is melanoma and that's more like 2%” ▶ 14:46
The single best predictor of a US skin-cancer diagnosis is the number of dermatologists in your county and the local age bracket, not sun exposure, indicating diagnosis is supply-driven.
contrarian🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highdermatologistsUnited States
So what: Rising skin-cancer 'incidence' largely reflects more looking, not more disease, a classic supplier-induced detection dynamic.
“How many dermatologists there are in your county and and the overall age bracket for people in your county.” ▶ 20:17
Melanoma mortality has stayed flat decade after decade while incidence rises steeply, which experts cite as the classic statistical signature of overdiagnosis rather than a real epidemic.
causal🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highmelanoma
So what: Flat mortality against rising incidence undercuts the narrative of a genuine, worsening sun-driven cancer crisis.
“mortality is totally flat while incidence is rising steeply. And as some scientists have pointed out, that's kind of like like the classic sign of overdiagnosis.”
In a re-read study, identical melanoma slides judged as 11 melanomas 20 years ago were judged as 18 melanomas today by the same and other dermatologists, evidencing diagnostic drift toward calling more lesions cancer.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediummelanomadermatologists
So what: Diagnostic thresholds, not biology, are inflating cancer statistics used to justify sun avoidance.
“20 years ago 11 were called were judged to be melanomas and today the exact same slides 18 of them were judged to be melanomas.” ▶ 26:13
Skin cancer kills about 10,000 Americans a year versus roughly 1 million from cardiovascular disease and 600,000 from cancer, and it does not rank in the world's top 40 causes of death.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highskin cancercardiovascular diseaseUnited States
So what: Any policy that trades a tiny skin-cancer risk against the giant killers should weight the giants heavily.
“in the US, it causes, you know, about 10,000 people a year die from skin cancer... cardiovascular disease is, you know, closer to a million people and cancer is like maybe 600,000 people.” ▶ 49:12
Sunscreen efficacy and the UVA generation gap 4
Rates of skin cancer and sunscreen use have tracked each other closely for decades, and epidemiological studies repeatedly find more sunscreen use correlated with more skin cancer.
causal🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highsunscreen industry
So what: Sunscreen has not delivered the protection its marketing implies, though reverse causation partly explains the correlation.
“rates of skin cancer and rates of sunscreen use track each other spectacularly well” ▶ 28:57
SPF measures only UVB (burn) protection, so early SPF sunscreens let large doses of UVA through while suppressing the burn signal, plausibly making them worse than nothing by extending exposure time 10-30x.
framework🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highSPFUVAUVBsunscreen industry
So what: A trusted safety metric measured the wrong thing, encouraging behavior that likely raised melanoma risk.
“by blocking sunburn and making you think you were safe from skin cancer they were allowing you to stay in the sun depending on the SPF 10 15 20 30 times longer. And all of that time gobs of UVA was pouring into your body” ▶ 35:26
US sunscreens are roughly a generation behind those sold in Europe, Asia, and Israel, with better broad-spectrum formulations expected to reach the US market starting next year.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumsunscreen industryUnited StatesEuropeAsiaIsraelFDA
So what: A regulatory lag has left US consumers with inferior sun protection and signals a coming product refresh in the US market.
“The the sunscreens are used in in Europe and uh Asia and like Israel are much better than the ones that are allowed in the US.” ▶ 33:39
Observational studies show sunscreen effects improving over time: no benefit or reverse effect for 1970s products, roughly a wash in the 1980s-90s, and net positive for modern broad-spectrum formulas.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumsunscreen industryUVAUVB
So what: Product quality, not sunscreen per se, drives outcomes, so blanket historical claims about sunscreen are misleading.
“the early ones from like the 70s show no effect and and often you know reverse effect... And now more the more modern ones it's starting to look better for sunscreen now.” ▶ 36:22
Vitamin D: supplement failure vs. natural sun exposure 4
Large rigorous placebo-controlled vitamin D trials have failed across the board: raising blood vitamin D via pills does not help cancer, cardiovascular, autoimmune, or other conditions unless the person is extremely deficient.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highvitamin DNew England Journal of Medicine
So what: The most-prescribed supplement in the world lacks trial support, exposing a gap between association and causation.
“They have completely failed across the board, where we know that unless you are extremely vitamin D deficient, raising your the amount in your blood through pill doesn't help with any of these conditions.” ▶ 41:15
New England Journal of Medicine editors have publicly urged doctors to stop prescribing vitamin D pills, yet it remains the most prescribed supplement in the world.
contrarian🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highvitamin DNew England Journal of Medicine
So what: Persistent prescribing despite negative evidence shows how sticky medical habits and placebo value keep a market alive.
“the editors of the New England Journal of Medicine have published editorials saying, "Stop prescribing vitamin D pills. It's not not helping."” ▶ 41:30
Vitamin D may be merely a biomarker of sun exposure rather than the causal agent of sunlight's health benefits, explaining why natural high-D populations are healthy while pill-raised D provides no benefit.
thesis🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumvitamin D
So what: If D is a proxy, the entire supplement-based public-health strategy targets the wrong variable.
“whether it was just sort of along for the ride. You know, it was it was an indicator of sun exposure, but it wasn't the agent that was bringing the good health” ▶ 46:00
The body's vitamin D production halves by age 70 relative to the 30s for the same sunlight, and it must be made near solar noon (roughly 1-3pm), limiting how much older adults can self-correct via sun.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumvitamin D
So what: Sun-based vitamin D strategies are age- and timing-dependent, weakening simple 'just get sun' advice for the elderly.
“by the time we're 70, we're on we're only producing half as much vitamin D for the same amount of sunlight as when we're, you know, in our 30s.” ▶ 45:11
Mechanisms and the mortality trade-off 4
Dermatologist Richard Weller showed that shining ~20-25 minutes of summer-equivalent sunlight on skin raises nitric oxide and lowers blood pressure by 5-6 points, a mechanism independent of vitamin D.
causal🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumRichard Wellernitric oxideViagra
So what: A plausible non-D mechanism links sun to cardiovascular benefit, strengthening the case that sun's value is real.
“shining it on their arm would lower their blood pressure. It raised nitric oxide in their blood... and lowered blood pressure by like five or six points.” ▶ 48:23
A Weller UK Biobank preprint tracking ~420,000 people for ~20 years found high-sun individuals about 20% more likely to die of melanoma but far less likely to die of cardiovascular disease and cancer, a roughly 75-to-1 lives-saved ratio.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumRichard WellerUK Biobankmelanomacardiovascular disease
So what: If robust, the trade-off math overwhelmingly favors more sun exposure at the population level.
“the ratio was 75 to 1 that more more light for every one extra death from melanoma that it created was saving about 75 deaths uh, from other bigger more problematic diseases.” ▶ 53:44
Of ~420,000 UK Biobank participants, ~45,000 died over the period but only 440 from melanoma and 60 from other skin cancers, quantifying how minor skin-cancer mortality is relative to total deaths.
data_point🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highUK Biobankmelanoma
So what: Skin cancer accounts for about 1% of deaths in this cohort, reframing it as a small line item in overall mortality.
“Only 440 had died of melanoma. Only 60 had died of other skin cancers out of 420,000 people.” ▶ 52:45
Both the case that sunlight causes skin cancer and the case that it prevents other diseases rest on observational data, since ethics and funding preclude randomized sun-exposure trials in either direction.
framework🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highepidemiology
So what: The evidentiary asymmetry investors and policymakers assume (proven harm vs. speculative benefit) does not actually exist.
“the reasons that we believe that... sunlight it causes skin cancer are also from observational studies.” ▶ 58:50
Historical arc and epistemics of public-health advice 5
In the late 1800s tuberculosis caused roughly one in four European deaths, and Faroe Islands doctor Finsen's discovery that concentrated sunlight cured cutaneous TB, plus sunlight curing rickets via vitamin D, launched the heliotherapy era.
anecdote🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumtuberculosisricketsFinsenFaroe Islandsheliotherapyvitamin D
So what: Sunlight was once mainstream medicine, showing how completely a health consensus can reverse.
“one out of every four deaths in Europe was due to tuberculosis in the later 1800s.” ▶ 10:03
US farming fell from about 40% of the workforce in 1900 to 3% by century's end, so the population moved indoors even as skin-cancer rates exploded, making rising sun exposure an implausible sole cause.
causal🗣 Russ Robertsconviction: highUnited Statesagriculture
So what: The dominant sun-causes-cancer story ignores that measured outdoor time fell over the same period.
“farming goes from 40% of of the workforce in 1900 to 3% by the end of the century. Uh so people aren't in the sun.”
Roberts frames observational epidemiology as an 'intellectual cesspool' where an individual raising a biomarker to match healthy populations may not gain their outcomes because unmeasured confounders (latitude, income, diet, genetics) are not controlled.
framework🗣 Russ Robertsconviction: highepidemiology
So what: A durable mental model for discounting correlation-based health and market claims where confounders dominate.
“these observational studies in epidemiology, which are um in my view a cesspool, intellectual cesspool” ▶ 42:27
Jacobsen argues LLMs now let laypeople locate foundational research directly by asking for studies and following the cited primary sources, changing how non-experts can audit expert consensus.
prediction🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: mediumLLMsClaude
So what: AI-assisted source retrieval erodes the information asymmetry that lets institutions maintain contested health narratives.
“we're now in this kind of amazing era where we have these incredibly powerful research assistants on hand all the time, you know, the LLMs that have read everything” ▶ 1:01:47
Jacobsen's actionable rule is to avoid both zero sun and any burning, since zero exposure is clearly unhealthy and a burn signals cell death, with tolerance scaling to skin tone: darker skin can take far more, red-haired fair skin is most melanoma-prone.
thesis🗣 Rowan Jacobsenconviction: highmelanoma
So what: Blanket sun-avoidance guidance written for the fairest skin over-restricts the majority who could safely get more sun.
“zero sun exposure is not healthy for people. It's also clear... that burning is bad.” ▶ 1:02:55

Mindscape · Sean Carroll 1 episode(s) · 22 insights

Solo: Theories of Dark Energy | Mindscape 359116 min22 insights2026-06-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Sean Carroll's solo deep-dive on dynamical theories of dark energy: quintessence scalar fields, phantom energy/big rip, and modified gravity (f(R)), arguing all remain theoretically unnatural and that the plain cosmological constant (w = -1) is still where the 'smart money' sits despite recent DESI/CMB hints. Direct market relevance is low; the signal is a masterclass in reasoning-under-uncertainty and a map of the observational programs (Roman, Rubin/LSST, Euclid, DESI, CMB polarization) driving cosmology funding.
Base rate on dark energy models: cosmological constant still wins · 3Quintessence and naturalness constraints · 3A live, testable prediction: rotating light polarization · 2Phantom energy, the big rip, and vacuum stability · 3Modified gravity as an alternative to dark energy and dark matter · 4Observational program and science-funding context · 3Reasoning-under-uncertainty and research methodology · 4
Base rate on dark energy models: cosmological constant still wins 3
Despite 25 years of dynamical dark energy model-building, the simple cosmological constant (equation-of-state parameter w = -1) still fits all data perfectly and remains the most probable explanation for cosmic acceleration.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmological constantdark energycosmology
So what: The expert's Bayesian prior: bet against exotic-physics headlines from any single new dataset; extraordinary claims about 'evolving dark energy' should be discounted until multiply confirmed.
“If you ask me right now I would still say that the smart money is that w is minus one that it is just the cosmological constant.” ▶ 1:20:15
Recent hints from the DESI collaboration and CMB analyses that dark energy may be evolving (and even w < -1) are, in Carroll's judgment, too premature to act on and likely reflect too-quick parameter fits rather than real signal.
contrarian🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumDESIdark energycosmic microwave background
So what: A domain expert publicly discounting the buzzy 'dark energy is changing' narrative; useful counterweight when evaluating science-media hype cycles.
“I think that their implication that W is less than minus one is actually not that believable. uh they think they did a um too quick a job in fitting the parameters.” ▶ 1:19:47
The dynamical dark energy program never solved the problems that motivated it: the cosmological constant problem persists even if vacuum energy is zero, and tracker/quintessence models failed to convincingly explain the coincidence problem.
causal🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmological constant problemcoincidence problemquintessencetracker models
So what: Illustrates how ambitious research programs can be worth pursuing yet still not pay off; a template for evaluating whether a theoretical bet has actually delivered.
“I think roughly speaking that ambition did not pan out.” ▶ 6:57
Quintessence and naturalness constraints 3
A quintessence dark-energy scalar field must have a mass near 10^-33 electron volts (numerically the Hubble constant in energy units), making it vastly more fine-tuned than even the Higgs boson's already-puzzling hierarchy problem.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highquintessenceHiggs bosonHubble constanthierarchy problem
So what: Concrete quantification of why dynamical dark energy is theoretically unattractive; grounds the skepticism in a specific unnatural number.
≈ “the quintessence field has to have a mass of about 10 ^ -33 electron volts. So it's hugely tiny compared to any known particle physics scale” paraphrase
Any new light scalar dark-energy field should generically produce detectable fifth forces and cause the fine structure constant and particle masses to drift over cosmological time, so its couplings must be suppressed by roughly a factor of 10^-5 to hide from existing lab and astrophysical constraints.
causal🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highquintessencefifth forcefine structure constant
So what: Explains what precision experiments (torsion pendulums, fine-structure-constant drift) are actually probing and why null results keep constraining new-physics models.
“in order to hide from the experiments and to be empirically phenomenologically viable, this new scalar field has to be have all of its couplings suppressed by something like a factor of 10 the minus 5.” ▶ 50:09
Imposing an approximate shift symmetry (a pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone-boson construction) makes an anomalously light quintessence field 'technically natural,' simultaneously protecting its tiny mass and suppressing its couplings to other fields.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highpseudo-Nambu-Goldstone bosonshift symmetryquintessenceGerard 't Hooft
So what: A reusable mental model: unnaturally small parameters can be rescued by a broken symmetry ('technical naturalness'), a pattern that recurs across particle physics.
A live, testable prediction: rotating light polarization 2
A slowly rolling pseudoscalar dark-energy field couples to electromagnetism via E-dot-B and should rotate the polarization plane of light from distant galaxies and the CMB by about one degree, a value that was below existing five-degree limits and testable within a lifetime.
prediction🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highpseudoscalar fieldcosmic microwave backgroundelectromagnetismcosmic birefringence
So what: A concrete, still-open prediction that could yield the first direct detection of dark energy; watch CMB polarization experiments building purpose-designed detectors.
“it will rotate the plane of polarization of light from distant galaxies and the microwave background... it's about 1 degree is your general prediction.” ▶ 58:41
Ichiro Komatsu and collaborators report a not-yet-statistically-significant hint in CMB data that polarizations are rotating slightly, and new radio telescopes are being built with detectors specifically to look for this cosmic birefringence.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumIchiro Komatsucosmic microwave backgroundcosmic birefringenceradio telescopes
So what: Identifies a specific emerging experimental frontier and the researcher to track; a positive result would be a landmark and drive further instrumentation funding.
“he says that in the data there is a sign that it looks like the polarizations are rotating by a little bit a tiny bit and it's like you know not quite statistically significant yet but it's getting there.” ▶ 1:01:47
Phantom energy, the big rip, and vacuum stability 3
Phantom energy (w < -1) requires a scalar field with negative kinetic energy, whose particle excitations have negative mass; Carroll, Hoffman, and Trodden showed this makes the vacuum catastrophically unstable because empty space can decay into unlimited positive- and negative-mass particles.
causal🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highphantom energybig ripghost particlesMark TroddenMark Hoffman
So what: A worked example of using vacuum-stability arguments to rule out a whole class of models; the same logic underlies many 'this new physics can't work' conclusions.
“empty space with zero energy can decay into a bunch of positive mass particles and negative mass particles. The vacuum would be catastrophically unstable in this way of doing things.” ▶ 1:15:39
If w is genuinely less than -1, the universe's scale factor blows up to infinity at a finite future time, a scenario Caldwell and Kamionkowski named the 'big rip.'
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumbig ripRobert CaldwellMarc Kamionkowskiphantom energy
So what: A memorable illustration that model naming/marketing shapes which speculative scenarios get attention and taken seriously.
Apparent evidence for w < -1 can be an artifact of fitting an incomplete theory: a time-varying Newton's constant (scalar-tensor gravity) can trick analyses into inferring phantom-like behavior even when none exists, though threading all observational constraints is very hard.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumscalar-tensor gravityNewton's constantphantom energyBrans-Dicke
So what: General caution: inferred parameters depend entirely on which theoretical possibilities the analysis allows; 'surprising' fits often reflect model omissions.
“the constraints you get out depends on the theoretical possibilities that you allow for that you put in when you start your analysis.” ▶ 1:18:15
Modified gravity as an alternative to dark energy and dark matter 4
Modifying gravity to eliminate dark matter (MOND and variants) is now effectively ruled out because its predictions for CMB anisotropies came out false, unlike the situation 25 years ago before that data existed.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highMONDmodified gravitydark mattercosmic microwave background
So what: A clean case of a theory being killed by later data; template for tracking when a long-lived alternative hypothesis crosses into ruled-out territory.
“the idea of Mand and other modifications of gravity made predictions for the microwave background anisotropies and they came out false. They have been ruled out by the data.” ▶ 1:29:21
Carroll, Duvvuri, Trodden, and Turner were among the first to explicitly propose f(R) gravity (adding 1/R to the Einstein-Hilbert action) to explain cosmic acceleration without dark energy, launching the now-popular f(R) research program.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highf(R) gravitymodified gravityMichael TurnerMark TroddenVikram Duvvuri
So what: Shows how a tool the originator himself under-valued became a major sub-field; a lesson in the value of new methodological tools over any single model result.
“the whole program of what is now called F of Ravity became super duper popular.” ▶ 1:50:53
MOND's motivation is a genuine numerical coincidence: the acceleration at which galaxies begin to need dark matter is roughly the same across very different spiral galaxies and is numerically close to today's Hubble constant.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highMONDMordehai MilgromHubble constantdark matterVera Rubin
So what: Explains the seductive empirical pattern behind modified-gravity enthusiasm and why coincidences can mislead theorists into whole research directions.
Attempts to modify gravity that only kick in when gravity is weak (small curvature) run directly against effective field theory expectations, which predict new physics appears at high energies, not in the low-energy/large-distance infrared regime.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: higheffective field theorymodified gravityf(R) gravity
So what: A general heuristic for judging speculative physics: modifications in the infrared are far more suspect than ultraviolet ones.
“you don't expect things to become weird when uh spacetime becomes approximately flat, fields become weak, distances become large” ▶ 1:38:43
Observational program and science-funding context 3
A large fleet of instruments is actively probing dark energy: the Nancy Grace Roman space telescope (recently launched), the Rubin Observatory (formerly LSST), the Euclid satellite, and the DESI collaboration.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highNancy Grace Roman Space TelescopeRubin ObservatoryLSSTEuclidDESI
So what: The concrete roster of dark-energy missions relevant to any space/science-funding or astronomy-instrumentation investment theme.
Carroll argues the dark-energy probing program is scientifically worthwhile even if the answer is just the cosmological constant, because the same surveys collect broadly valuable data on supernovae, large-scale structure, and early galaxies.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumdark energysupernovalarge scale structurecosmology
So what: A funding-justification framework: broad multi-purpose instruments de-risk investment because they yield returns regardless of whether the headline hypothesis pans out.
“maybe we learn something completely different... We're still doing cosmology. We're still collecting data.” ▶ 27:43
Fundamental physics and cosmology move slowly by nature: a prediction made in 1999 (polarization rotation) can take decades to become testable because the required telescopes must first be built, so there is a long lag between theory and confirmation.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmologyfundamental physics
So what: Sets realistic time horizons for cosmology payoffs; decades-long lags argue against expecting near-term resolution of dark-energy questions.
“it takes decades to make telescopes that are good enough to test that kind of prediction.” ▶ 1:53:35
Reasoning-under-uncertainty and research methodology 4
Carroll repeatedly notes his own error of dismissing ideas (like f(R) gravity) as 'not interesting' because they lacked novel properties, only to see others build major programs on them; he frames recognizing a new tool's downstream value as a distinct skill from having the idea.
anecdote🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highf(R) gravityscientific methodology
So what: A transferable lesson for evaluating early-stage bets: value the platform/tool a result enables, not just the result itself.
“I realize it's a new tool for people to play with. Like once you say, 'Oh, maybe I can modify gravity in this way. Maybe other more clever people than you can... make progress.'” ▶ 1:50:38
Carroll advocates an explicitly Bayesian stance on open cosmological questions: maintain credences across the cosmological constant, scalar fields, modified gravity, and unknown options, and reserve probability for new data to update those credences.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highBayesian reasoningdark energycosmology
So what: A disciplined decision framework for any high-uncertainty domain: hold a distribution over hypotheses rather than committing to one narrative.
“We're trying to be good basians. We're trying to say that there's a credence for uh it being a scalar field or modified gravity or various other kinds of things we haven't thought of yet.” ▶ 1:52:38
Theory must run ahead of data because knowing which new observations to pursue is often driven by theoretical speculation; if acceleration had not been predicted-as-possible, no one would have built experiments to measure the dark energy equation of state.
causal🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmologyscientific methodologydark energy
So what: Explains why speculative theoretical work has practical value: it directs where expensive experimental resources get allocated.
“you can't just sit around and wait for the data because sometimes coming up with the theoretical ideas suggests to you that you should go look for certain pieces of data” ▶ 1:26:49
Carroll calls the discovery that the universe is accelerating the single most surprising and profound discovery in fundamental physics during his professional career, and says it still drives his current work including a recent cyclic-universe paper.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmic accelerationdark energyde Sitter spacecyclic universe
So what: Signals where a leading theorist believes the highest-value open problems in fundamental physics lie.
“this discovery that the universe is accelerating is the single most surprising and profound discovery in fundamental physics in the time that I've been doing fundamental physics professionally.” ▶ 1:54:42
